My grandmother watched me set it on the kitchen table and said nothing, just handed me a tube of epoxy from the junk drawer like she'd been expecting this exact moment.

That turtle lived in a plastic storage bin in our mudroom for six weeks. I named it Copernicus, which felt appropriately serious. Every morning before the school bus came, I checked whether the epoxy was holding along the fracture line, whether the lettuce I'd shredded was eaten, whether the shallow water dish had been used. I became obsessed with understanding how a turtle shell actually heals, which led me to the Harrisonburg public library forty minutes away, which led me to a veterinary biology textbook I could barely parse, which led me to email a herpetologist at Virginia Tech who, remarkably, emailed me back.

Dr. Muñoz told me that a turtle's shell is living bone fused to its spine, that it heals the way a human rib heals, slowly and from the inside. She said the epoxy was fine as a temporary seal but that the real repair was happening underneath, cell by cell, in total darkness. I thought about that for a long time. I think about it still.

I live in a town of eleven hundred people in the Shenandoah Valley, where the nearest traffic light is twenty-two minutes away and the cell service drops out along the ridge behind our property. People sometimes ask me what there is to do here, and the honest answer is that most of what I do involves paying close attention to things that don't move fast. I know which fence post the eastern phoebes nest on every April. I know that the soil in our lower field is heavier clay than the upper field because of how the creek flooded decades before I was born. I know the sound our barn roof makes in a specific kind of January wind, a low harmonic moan that my mother says means the nails are loosening but that I privately believe is something closer to singing.

This is not a fashionable kind of knowledge. Nobody is building an app around it. But learning about Copernicus's shell taught me something about the way I already thought, gave me a name for it. I am drawn to slow, hidden processes. In my AP Biology class, the unit that consumed me most was cellular respiration, not because the Krebs cycle is inherently thrilling but because I couldn't stop marveling that this invisible cascade of chemical reactions is happening constantly inside every living thing around me, the barn cats, the soybeans, my own grandmother dozing in her recliner. The world is full of quiet machinery.

I released Copernicus at the edge of our woods in late August. The epoxy was still visible, a pale ridge like a scar, but Dr. Muñoz assured me that new bone had likely bridged the fracture beneath it. I set it down in the leaf litter and it sat motionless for a full four minutes. I counted. Then it walked into the ferns without urgency, like it had somewhere to be but wasn't late.

I want to study biology in college because I want formal tools for understanding the processes I've spent my life noticing informally. I want to learn the language for what happens in the soil, in the shell, in the space between a wound and its repair. I come from a place where things take time and nobody rushes the work. I think that's prepared me for science more than I originally understood. The real repair happens underneath, cell by cell, in total darkness. You just have to be patient enough to trust it.

The summer I turned fifteen, I watched my father cry for the second time in my life. The first was when Grandpa Earl died. This time, it was because the county extension office told him our soil's phosphorus levels had crashed to nearly nothing, and the winter wheat we'd planted across forty acres wasn't coming back.

I remember standing in the kitchen doorway while he sat at the table with the soil report spread out like a medical chart delivering bad news. My mother's hand was on his shoulder. Outside, the fields looked fine to someone driving past on Route 9—just brown dirt waiting for spring, same as always. But underneath, something had gone wrong that you couldn't see.

That invisible failure is what I keep thinking about. Not just in soil, but everywhere.

I live in Elgin, Kansas, population 87. We are the kind of town that doesn't appear on most maps, where the post office operates out of a converted garage and the nearest grocery store is a thirty-two-mile drive to Howard. Growing up here, I learned early that the world beneath the surface matters more than the world you can photograph. The roots matter more than the stems. The aquifer matters more than the pond.

After Dad got that soil report, I started reading about soil health obsessively, checking out books from the Howard library that the librarian said nobody had touched in years. I learned about mycorrhizal networks—the fungal threads that connect plant roots underground, shuttling nutrients and chemical signals between organisms that look, aboveground, completely separate. Scientists call it the "wood wide web." A mother tree can recognize her own seedlings and send them extra carbon through these networks. Dying trees dump their resources into the system for others to use. There is an entire economy of mutual aid happening in the dark, in silence, in the last place most people think to look.

I became that kid who brought soil samples to the dinner table, who ruined three of my mother's baking sheets trying to measure organic matter by burning soil in the oven at 450 degrees. I tested our fields, the ditchline, the old cemetery plot behind the Methodist church. I mapped the differences. The cemetery soil was alive—dark, crumbly, dense with fungal threads—because nobody had plowed it or sprayed it in over a hundred years. Our field soil was pale and compacted, and when I put it under the dissecting microscope I borrowed from my biology teacher, Mrs. Pruitt, I could see how empty it was. It looked like a abandoned apartment building compared to the cemetery's crowded city block.

Dad and I started experimenting with cover crops—crimson clover, daikon radish, a mix of species designed to feed the soil biology rather than extract from it. We couldn't afford to convert everything at once, so we tried five acres. That first spring, when I dug into the cover-cropped plot and found earthworms clustered thick as fingers in a fist, I felt something I can only describe as relief so physical it made my eyes sting.

I know soil science isn't what most people find riveting. But what captivates me is the principle underneath it: that the most critical systems are often the least visible, and that degradation can advance for years before anyone notices. I think about this when I read about mental health in rural communities, about the slow erosion of small-town economies, about aquifer depletion across western Kansas. The collapses that matter most are the ones that happen underground, gradually, while the surface still looks like it's holding together.

I want to study what's hidden and failing. I want to be someone who reads the soil report before the crop dies, who pays attention to the fungal networks—literal and metaphorical—that hold systems together. I come from a place most people drive through without stopping, from a family that works ground most people never think about. I've learned that the least glamorous questions are often the most urgent ones, and that kneeling in the dirt is sometimes the most important position you can take.

The summer I turned fifteen, I helped my father pull a dead calf from a drainage ditch behind our property. It had been there maybe two days, bloated and stiff, its legs jutting at angles that made it look like a piece of furniture someone had thrown away. The smell was so thick I could taste it, something metallic and sweet that stayed in my sinuses for hours afterward. My father hooked a chain around its hind legs and we dragged it with the four-wheeler to a spot near the tree line where he said the coyotes would take care of it. That night at dinner, I ate pot roast and didn't think twice about it.

I've been turning that moment over in my mind for two years now, not because it was traumatic but because it wasn't. I grew up in Elmore, Kansas, population 587 on a generous day. Death here isn't an abstraction. It's a barn cat crushed under a hay bale, a rooster dispatched on a stump with a hatchet, a deer hanging from a gambrel in someone's garage in November. By the time I was twelve, I had seen more dead animals than most adults in cities see in a lifetime, and I had developed what I'd call a functional numbness about it. Things die. You deal with the body. You move on.

But in sophomore biology, Mrs. Peralta showed us a documentary about industrial livestock operations, and something shifted. The footage showed pigs in gestation crates so narrow they couldn't turn around, chickens with beaks trimmed to nubs, cattle standing knee-deep in their own waste. Kids in my class looked away or made jokes. I just watched. What unsettled me wasn't the suffering itself, exactly. It was recognizing that the numbness I had cultivated on our small farm had been industrialized, scaled up, turned into an economic strategy. The same emotional callus I developed from dragging a calf out of a ditch had been built into an entire system where millions of animals existed in conditions that required everyone involved to not feel anything.

That realization sent me down a path I never expected. I started reading—not assigned books but things I found myself: Temple Grandin's work on humane livestock handling, Wendell Berry's essays on agrarian life, articles about regenerative agriculture. I checked out books from the county library in Stafford, twenty miles away, because Elmore doesn't have one. I discovered that the question I was really asking wasn't whether it's acceptable to raise and kill animals for food. Growing up here, I've made my peace with that cycle. The question was about attention. What happens to a society that builds systems designed to eliminate the need to pay attention to suffering?

This question extends far beyond agriculture. I see it in how we consume news, scrolling past photographs of disaster with the same glazed efficiency as a worker on a processing line. I see it in how my town talks about the families who lost their farms to corporate operations—with a resigned shrug, a muttered "that's just how it goes." Numbness isn't just personal. It's structural. And it's most dangerous when it feels like common sense.

I don't want to romanticize my upbringing. Elmore is a place where people work brutally hard, where kindness is expressed through casseroles left on porches and combines lent without question. But it's also a place where certain kinds of reflection are treated as impractical, even suspicious. When I told my father about what I'd been reading, he listened for a while, then said, "You think too much." He wasn't being cruel. He was telling me, in his way, that thinking doesn't pull calves out of ditches.

He's right. But I've come to believe that thinking is how we decide which ditches we're willing to tolerate. I want to study the systems we build and the silences we maintain to keep them running. I want to pay attention on purpose, even when—especially when—the numbness would be easier.

The summer I turned fifteen, I found a box turtle on County Road 12 with a crack running down its shell like a lightning bolt frozen in keratin. I brought it home in my lap, holding it steady over every pothole between the Hendersons' cattle gate and our gravel driveway, and I spent the next three weeks learning how to repair a living thing with epoxy and fiberglass patches, following a YouTube tutorial filmed by a wildlife rehabilitator in Tennessee.

That turtle changed the way I think about knowledge and where it lives.

In Carverton, Kansas, population 487, we have one gas station, one church, and zero stoplights. The nearest veterinarian who treats exotics is an hour and forty minutes east in Wichita. My school doesn't offer AP Biology. When I told my neighbor Earl about the turtle, he said his grandfather used to fix cracked shells with beeswax and prayer, and honestly, that wasn't far off from what the internet suggested — just updated materials and a steadier faith in cyanoacrylate adhesive.

What struck me was the collision. Earl's inherited knowledge, passed down through generations of people who lived close to box turtles and broken things, overlapped almost perfectly with the professional advice I pulled from a screen in my bedroom. The rehabilitator in Tennessee talked about stabilizing the fracture, keeping the wound clean, maintaining humidity. Earl talked about keeping the turtle in a damp box in the shade and letting it eat dandelion greens until it seemed right. They were describing the same wisdom in different vocabularies.

I started noticing this pattern everywhere. My mother knows when rain is coming because the cattle bunch together near the tree line. She has never read a study on barometric pressure and bovine behavior, but researchers at the University of Arizona published one in 2018 that essentially confirms what she has observed her entire life. My father can look at soil and tell you what it needs. He crumbles it between his fingers, smells it, watches how water moves across its surface. He has never used the word "tilth" but he understands the concept in his palms.

For a while, this made me wonder whether formal education even mattered. If Earl could fix a turtle and my mother could predict weather, why chase degrees? But the more I sat with that question, the more I realized I was framing it wrong. The point isn't that one kind of knowledge replaces the other. The point is that they're two dialects of the same language, and fluency in both makes you a better listener.

The turtle healed. I released her in the tall grass behind our barn in late July, and I watched her walk into the bluestem without looking back, her patched shell catching the evening light. I felt something complicated — proud, sad, small, enormous. I had held a wild life in my hands and guided it back to its own.

That fall, I started a small wildlife rehabilitation notebook. I log every injured animal I encounter, what I do, what I research, what Earl or my parents or the older farmers suggest. I cross-reference folk knowledge with published studies when I can find them. I have seventeen entries now. A red-tailed hawk with a dislocated wing. A fawn that lost its mother to a combine. A clutch of painted turtle eggs I relocated from a drainage ditch.

I want to study biology not because I think a university will hand me knowledge I don't already touch every day, but because I want to learn the grammar underneath the language my community already speaks. I want to bring that grammar home. Carverton doesn't need someone to tell its people they've been wrong. It needs someone who can explain, in terms that travel, why they've been right — and help protect the land and creatures they've been quietly tending all along.

Earl still asks about the turtle sometimes. I tell him she's out there. He nods like he already knew.

The summer I turned fifteen, a calf got stuck in the fence line behind our property and died before my dad and I could free her. She'd twisted herself trying to get through a gap that was never quite big enough, and by the time we found her in the morning, her legs had given out. We stood there in the wet grass while the sun came up pink over the silo, and my dad said something I've been turning over ever since: "She couldn't see the whole fence. Just the gap."

I grew up in Harlan County, Kentucky, population 26,000 spread across mountains that make everything feel both contained and infinite. Our high school had 340 students. The nearest bookstore was forty minutes away. I didn't know a single person who had studied abroad or worked in a laboratory until I started reading college brochures, which arrived in our mailbox like dispatches from a country I'd only heard described. So when I started taking AP Biology my junior year and encountered the concept of bounded rationality — the idea that humans make decisions based on limited, incomplete information rather than total knowledge — I felt something click into place that I couldn't quite articulate yet.

Bounded rationality was proposed by economist Herbert Simon, who argued that we don't optimize; we satisfice. We work with what we can see. The calf couldn't perceive the full fence, so she chose the gap that looked like freedom and made it her ending. I've been guilty of the same logic. Growing up where I did, I unconsciously mapped a small set of futures: stay, mine, or leave and lose yourself. Those were the options I could see. Not because they were the only ones, but because the fence I knew had only three gaps.

What's engaged me isn't just the concept itself but what it demands of us once we understand it. If our reasoning is structurally limited by our experience, then the only way to think more completely is to deliberately seek what we don't already know. That sounds obvious until you realize how rarely anyone actually does it. My AP Biology teacher, Mr. Combs, kept a map of the world on his classroom wall with pins in every place a former student had gone. There were clusters around Lexington and Louisville, a few in Cincinnati. One pin in London. One in Tokyo. I used to stare at it and wonder whether those students had left because they could see more of the fence than I could, or whether leaving had been the thing that revealed it.

I started reading outside my assignments. Not always the right things — I went through a phase obsessed with behavioral economics that my mom called "your weird money phase" — but the habit of searching for frameworks that enlarged my view became something I genuinely needed. I'd come home from helping with our tobacco crop, hands sticky and green-stained, and read about cognitive bias until dinner. There was something grounding about the contrast. The physical, immediate, certain work of farming alongside the slippery, expanding work of trying to understand how we understand.

What I've come to believe is that place isn't limitation. Harlan gave me a specific education in consequence, in patience, in the difference between what looks like a solution and what actually is one. What it couldn't give me alone was range. I want to study cognitive science and behavioral economics because I want to understand how people construct reality from incomplete information — and how we might build better structures for each other. How we design choices, institutions, communities that don't trap people in gaps too small for them.

The calf couldn't see the whole fence. I'm not sure any of us can, entirely. But I'd like to spend my life building a longer view.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a dead oak tree at the edge of our soybean field and decided to count its rings. I dragged my thumbnail across the exposed stump, lost count somewhere around eighty, and started over. My grandfather watched from the fence post, chewing on a piece of grass, not saying anything. When I finally got to ninety-three, he told me the tree had been there before his father planted that field. Before the road. Before almost everything I could see from where I was standing.

That moment cracked something open in me. Not dramatically — more like a slow, widening gap.

I grew up in Hardin County, Kentucky, where the population of my town sits around eight hundred people and the nearest bookstore is forty minutes away. My world had obvious edges. The grain elevator, the volunteer fire station, the church my family has attended for four generations. I knew the name of every dog on our road. When I wanted information about something, I asked my dad or I looked it up myself, usually in a library that closed at five on weekdays and wasn't open Sundays at all.

So when I developed an obsession with dendrochronology — the science of reading time through tree rings — I had to teach myself almost entirely from scratch. I read downloaded PDFs late at night on my phone, squinting at diagrams of cross-sections and drought patterns. I learned that trees record history in their bodies. A narrow ring means a hard year, thin resources, struggle. A wide ring means abundance. The wood is a document.

What gripped me wasn't just the science. It was the idea that the land itself had been narrating our lives all along, and I hadn't known how to read it.

I started paying attention differently. The creek behind our property floods almost every spring, and I had always accepted that as weather, as luck, as something adults complained about. But I began researching historical precipitation records for our county and cross-referencing them with the rings on trees I found. I built a rough timeline going back about one hundred and twenty years. Our creek flooded on a pattern. The land remembered its own rhythms even when the people on it forgot.

My science teacher, Mr. Alcorn, let me take over a corner of his classroom for the project. He told me it was the kind of thinking that didn't require a city to produce it. I've held onto that. Because I think sometimes I've felt the smallness of where I come from as a limitation, as a set of doors that were never installed, let alone opened. The nearest university is an hour away. Most people I know haven't left. A few cousins went to community college; that felt ambitious to almost everyone.

But dendrochronology taught me something that applies beyond tree rings. It taught me that depth isn't the same as access. That rigor is possible anywhere there is patience and curiosity and a willingness to sit with a question until it opens. My grandfather never went past eighth grade, but he could read a field the way I am only beginning to learn to read a stump. His knowledge ran deep in a different direction.

I want to study environmental science. I want to understand how climate stresses write themselves into living systems, how landscapes remember what humans prefer to ignore. I want to bring what I've learned from a corner of Hardin County into conversation with research that spans continents and centuries.

The oak tree I counted was ninety-three rings old. It had survived the Dust Bowl, two world wars, the death of American small farming as a dominant way of life. It didn't survive the ice storm of 2021.

I keep the photograph I took of that stump. The rings are still legible. I'm still counting.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a dead coyote at the edge of our soybean field.

It was July, and the heat made everything smell twice as strong. I was walking the fence line the way my dad had asked me to, checking for places where deer had pushed through the wire. The coyote was on its side in the tall grass, already stiff, its amber eyes open and filmed over with something pale. I stood there for maybe ten minutes, just looking at it. I had seen dead animals before — plenty of them — but something about this one stopped me.

What stopped me was its teeth. They were perfect. Intact, white, arranged with this mechanical precision that seemed almost designed. I remember thinking: designed by what, exactly?

That question followed me home and wouldn't leave.

I grew up in Harlan County, Nebraska, population just under three thousand, where the dominant explanations for things tended to come from two sources: science class and church. Both felt, to me, like they were speaking a different language than the one the coyote was speaking. My biology textbook described natural selection in clean, triumphant terms. My pastor described creation with equal confidence. Both were telling me something was settled. Neither explanation touched the specific strangeness of standing in a July soybean field looking at perfect teeth on a dead animal.

That fall I started reading outside the textbook. Not to find a third answer, but because I had developed a suspicion that the interesting territory lived in the friction between explanations rather than inside any single one.

I read about evolutionary developmental biology — evo-devo, researchers call it — which studies how small genetic changes can produce dramatic physical transformations. I read about convergent evolution, how eyes independently evolved in species with no common ancestor, as if certain solutions kept getting rediscovered. I started to understand that evolution wasn't just a slow grind of competition but something stranger: a process that seemed almost to rhyme with itself across time.

This didn't dissolve my original question. It complicated it in ways I found more interesting. Why do biological systems seem to trend toward complexity? Why does life, pressed through the constraints of survival, keep producing structures — like coyote teeth, like eyes — that exceed what seems strictly necessary?

I brought some of this to my biology teacher, Mr. Foss, who was patient and honest. He told me I was bumping up against philosophy of science, which he couldn't teach me but could point me toward. He handed me a book by the paleontologist Stephen Jay Gould, and I spent the rest of October reading it in the truck while my dad ran errands in town.

Gould kept using the word contingency — the idea that history is unrepeatable, that if you rewound the tape of life and ran it again, you'd get something entirely different. The coyote I found wasn't inevitable. That particular arrangement of calcium and keratin, those specific teeth, existed because of a cascade of unrepeatable accidents. Which somehow made the teeth more remarkable, not less.

I want to study evolutionary biology in college because I want to live closer to these questions professionally. But more than that, I've learned something about how I think. I think best when I'm standing in the friction between what a textbook says and what a thing actually is. I think best when I'm outside, in a field that smells like heat and soybeans, asking a question that doesn't have a clean answer yet.

I still don't know exactly what the coyote was telling me. But I know I've spent three years listening carefully. And I know that the questions I can't resolve are the ones I want to spend my life working on.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a dead coyote in our soybean field.

It was August, suffocating and still, and I was walking the rows checking for aphids the way my dad had shown me. Then there it was — gray-brown fur, ribs showing through, mouth slightly open. I stood over it for a long time. I didn't run back to the house screaming. I crouched down and looked at it, the way it had collapsed mid-stride like something had simply switched off inside it.

That fall, I started reading everything I could about animal cognition.

I grew up in Holt, Missouri, population 463. We have a grain elevator, a Casey's General Store, and a church that seats more people than actually live here. The nearest library with a decent science section is forty minutes away, so I spent a lot of time on my school's single desktop computer, burning through internet connection that felt borrowed. I read about corvids that mourn their dead, elephants that return to the bones of their families, chimpanzees that carry their deceased infants for weeks. I printed papers on dopamine loops in rats and tacked them above my desk next to a poster of the 1985 Royals.

What I kept circling back to was consciousness. Specifically: where does it start?

This question sounds philosophical, but for me it was viscerally physical. I grew up around death — livestock that didn't make it through winter, deer season, the slow decline of the family dog. In Holt, death isn't sanitized. It happens in front of you. So when scientists debated whether a chicken experiences fear or merely exhibits fear-like behavior, I found myself genuinely frustrated by the distinction. I had watched our chickens. I had seen what panic looks like in a feathered body.

My biology teacher, Mrs. Callahan, who is genuinely brilliant and trapped in a school that doesn't deserve her, pointed me toward Antonio Damasio's work on somatic markers — the idea that emotions are fundamentally about the body registering its own state. I read his book twice. It cracked something open in me. Consciousness, Damasio argues, is less about thought and more about feeling the body feel. Which means the question of animal consciousness isn't just philosophical. It's neurological, evolutionary, chemical.

I started keeping a journal, not of my feelings but of observations. The way our border collie's pupils dilate when she hears thunder. The specific posture our cattle assume before a storm rolls in from the west. Small, particular things that felt like they mattered even if I couldn't prove why.

Junior year I entered the regional science fair with a project on stress hormone levels in free-range versus confined poultry. I borrowed equipment from the ag department and read methods sections of papers I barely understood. I didn't win. A kid from Kansas City did a project on water filtration that was objectively more polished. But I had done something that felt, for the first time, like actual science — which is mostly just refusing to stop being curious in an organized way.

The coyote in the field never left my thinking. I don't know why it died. Mange, maybe. Hunger. Something internal and invisible. But it died alone in the middle of a human-made landscape, mid-stride, and something about that felt significant in a way I'm still working out.

I want to study neuroscience. Not because I have answers, but because that coyote gave me a question I can't outrun. What does any creature experience in the moment everything stops? Does experience require a witness?

I genuinely don't know. That's why I need to keep going.

In the heart of a bustling city, nestled between towering skyscrapers and vibrant street markets, is a place that has profoundly shaped my perspective: the community garden. Initially, it seemed like an unlikely destination for someone who had never shown particular interest in gardening. Yet, what started as a volunteer requirement for my environmental science class transformed into a source of inspiration and a catalyst for my personal growth.

Upon my first visit, I was struck by the paradox of the garden’s existence—a small patch of nature amidst concrete and chaos. The more time I spent there, the more I became captivated by its subtleties. Each plant has its own story, showcasing resilience and diversity in a shared space. This microcosm of collaboration and coexistence sparked my curiosity about ecosystems and sustainability, but even more so, it engaged my thoughts on community and connection.

Digging into the soil and tending to the plants gave me a tangible understanding of patience and responsibility. I found that nurturing a tiny seedling required careful attention and consistency, not unlike cultivating relationships with people. In the quiet moments of weeding and watering, I reflected on how these actions mirrored broader environmental efforts. A garden thrives through cooperation between its elements, and similarly, our world flourishes through harmonious interactions between diverse communities.

This experience ignited a deeper appreciation for the intricacies of nature and how essential it is to preserve these delicate systems. I began reading about sustainable practices and became involved in initiatives beyond the garden. My newfound interest led me to join our school's environmental club, where I collaborated with peers to implement recycling programs and educate younger students about the importance of sustainability.

Each project taught me how individual actions can spark communal changes. The realization that even the smallest contributions can have significant impacts shifted my thinking from focusing solely on grand ambitions to understanding the tremendous value in incremental progress. This shift was instrumental in shaping my approach to other areas of my life, whether it was academic responsibilities or personal relationships. As my perspective broadened, so did my ability to analyze problems critically and empathetically.

Moreover, the garden gave me insight into cultural connections. During community events, I encountered people from diverse backgrounds, all united by a common goal. Discussions around gardening tips often led to sharing stories about our cultural practices related to the land and food. These interactions expanded my understanding of cultural nuances and emphasized the importance of inclusivity and respect in any collective endeavor.

As I nurture my passion for environmental science, I am continually inspired by the thought that every decision we make, no matter how small, can contribute to a larger narrative of change. The community garden, with its simple beauty and complex lessons, remains a place where I am reminded of the power of perseverance and collaboration. My experiences there have shaped me into someone who values learning from the natural world and applying those lessons to foster understanding and action.

This engagement with the garden has not only influenced my academic interests but has also enriched my worldview. It has taught me about balance, interconnectedness, and the importance of stewardship—principles I strive to integrate into my daily life. As I prepare for the next chapter in my education, I carry with me the knowledge that growth, whether of plants or people, is nurtured through consistent care and respect for the environment we share. My journey started with a simple assignment but evolved into a commitment that I intend to pursue throughout my college years and beyond.

The clang of pots and the warm aroma of spices have always been familiar symphonies and fragrances in my home. My parents, both ardent food enthusiasts, have instilled in me a deep appreciation for cooking. However, it was not until my sophomore year that cooking morphed from simply a family activity into a subject that truly captivated my thinking, engaging me on a deeper level than I could have anticipated.

It all began when my school offered a culinary arts elective, and I eagerly enrolled, driven by nostalgia and curiosity. On the first day of class, I anticipated a straightforward experience—perhaps a few cooking techniques here and there, and, naturally, sampling my creations. But as the weeks unfolded, the class compelled me to think critically about food as an expression of culture, history, and creativity. 

The culinary arts class was unlike any other I had taken before. My teacher, Ms. Alvarez, challenged us to consider the stories behind our ingredients. “Cooking is a global language,” she would say, “a bridge between people, regardless of their background.” I remember vividly the assignment that sparked a change in how I viewed cooking. We were tasked to choose a dish from any culture, research its origins, and prepare it while telling its story. I chose paella, a vibrant Spanish dish that seemed both complex and intriguing.

Researching paella's origins was a journey through time; I learned about the dish’s creation by Valencian workers who utilized available rice, saffron, and vegetables. I found myself drawn into tales of how it evolved from a meal for laborers to a symbol of Spanish cuisine celebrated worldwide. But more importantly, I saw how each ingredient was chosen with intention, each step rooted in tradition. 

In the kitchen, as my classmates and I shared our research and dishes, I noticed how food fostered diversity and unity. We shared laughter and stories spanning different continents. My friend Olivia spoke passionately about her family’s traditional borscht, while Ahmed described how making couscous conjured memories of his grandmother’s kitchen. These stories, filled with heritage and personal significance, reflected how profoundly intertwined cultural identity is with the food we eat.

Cooking paella demanded patience and meticulousness. It required understanding the interplay of flavors, appreciating the slow sizzle of saffron and the precise layering of ingredients. Attempting to replicate an authentic paella taught me to respect and celebrate culinary diversity, emphasizing the importance of authenticity and attention to detail. This process of creation became meditative—a dance between precision and creativity.

Furthermore, exploring different cuisines opened my eyes to the global challenges of food security and sustainability. I began thinking about the impact of our food choices on the planet, considering how traditions could adapt to promote environmental consciousness. This realization inspired my involvement in the school's sustainability club, where I advocated for farm-to-table activities and organized discussions about sustainable agriculture.

The culinary arts taught me to appreciate the connections between individuals and cultures beyond the visible. Cooking became more than a hobby; it transformed into a lens through which I understood the world. It taught me that food can convey messages more profound than words; it holds history, nurtures bonds, and inspires change. 

Engaging with food as a subject has sparked a broader interest in cultural studies and environmental sustainability, which I am eager to explore further in my academic journey. It has highlighted the importance of preserving cultural heritages while striving for a sustainable future. 

This experience has shown me that even the most everyday activities, like cooking, can hold layers of meaning and offer insights into human connection and global challenges. As I move forward, I am committed to maintaining and expanding these lessons—celebrating diversity, advocating for sustainability, and finding meaning in the shared experiences of life.

Navigating through high school, I have often been drawn to the idea of storytelling. Whether it's through texts, oral traditions, or visual arts, stories are the threads that weave our world together. The most profound encounter with storytelling came during a creative writing workshop I attended sophomore year. This experience transformed how I perceive narratives and left an indelible mark on my thinking.

That workshop was an exploration of storytelling in its rawest form. Our instructor, an accomplished local author, challenged us to think beyond the traditional confines of beginning, middle, and end. We were tasked with creating a multifaceted character who defied clichés. At first, I was overwhelmed, but as I sat with pen in hand, I began to understand that stories are not merely about plot. They are about the complexities of human experience and emotion.

My character was a teenage girl who moves to a new city in the middle of the school year. Initially, I drew from the familiar trope of a girl struggling to fit in. However, through revising and reflecting, I saw an opportunity to dig deeper. I gave her an interest in astrophysics—a passion she discovered while watching the stars with her grandfather. Through this, I began to understand how stories could illuminate lesser-seen aspects of our lives. My character wasn't defined by her challenges, but by her dreams and passions.

This process opened my eyes to the subtleties of human emotion and the myriad of experiences that shape us. I saw how storytelling could be a medium for empathy. By delving into the lives of characters different from myself, I developed a deeper understanding of perspectives previously foreign to me. Crafting narratives allowed me to explore questions about identity, belonging, and the universal search for meaning.

Beyond the workshop, I noticed how storytelling can bridge gaps between diverse communities. In my school, where students hail from various backgrounds, I organized a storytelling event. This initiative aimed to create a platform where students could share personal stories in a safe and supportive environment. Students who rarely interacted discovered commonalities through shared experiences. It was then I realized that storytelling is not just about creativity; it's a tool for building connections and fostering understanding.

Furthermore, engaging with stories does not stop at personal expression. I have found storytelling's influence in the subjects I study, particularly history and literature. These disciplines tell the stories of our world and the people who shaped it, allowing us to learn from the past. While studying historical events, I am eager to explore the personal narratives behind them, the perspectives that textbooks often overlook.

The idea of storytelling now occupies my mind far beyond the confines of a simple hobby. It is a lens through which I approach the complexities of the world. Whether it's through writing my own stories or listening to the stories of others, I am constantly seeking out narratives that challenge my perceptions and broaden my understanding.

Storytelling has taught me that every person has a narrative worth telling. It encourages me to ask questions and listen intently. In the future, I hope to use storytelling as a conduit for change, advocating for voices that are often unheard. Whether I pursue journalism, history, or the arts, the skills I honed through storytelling will guide me in understanding and conveying the human experience.

Storytelling is an endless journey. It has engaged my thinking by encouraging me to examine the world through diverse lenses, question my assumptions, and embrace complexity. Each story, whether fictional or true, adds another layer to our shared human experience. As I embark on the next chapter of my life, I eagerly anticipate discovering new stories, writing my own, and, in doing so, connecting with others in meaningful ways.

One subject that has continually engaged my thinking is the concept of resilience. Throughout my life, I've encountered various challenges that have tested my limits and forced me to adapt, learn, and grow. The idea of resilience has become a core part of how I approach difficulties and setbacks, providing both a framework and a source of motivation.

My interest in resilience began during my freshman year of high school when I joined the cross-country team. As someone relatively new to long-distance running, the prospect of enduring miles at a time was daunting. The first few practices were particularly tough, with my legs aching and my stamina lacking. Quitting seemed like an easier option than pushing through the discomfort. However, as I learned more about the importance of resilience, I started to change my perspective. I realized that the ability to bounce back and persist despite fatigue was not only applicable to running but also to various aspects of life.

Over time, I embraced the resilience needed in cross-country running. With each practice and competition, I noticed gradual improvements in my stamina and speed. This progress was a direct result of my decision to persevere. I started to see setbacks, like a poor performance in a race, as opportunities to learn and improve. The lessons I learned on the track started to spill over into my academic life. When faced with difficult subjects like calculus or physics, I applied the same resilience I developed during running. Understanding complex concepts required effort and persistence, but by pushing through the challenges, I gained mastery over the material.

My sophomore year presented another test of resilience with the introduction of a challenging biology course. Initially overwhelmed by the density of material, I debated dropping the class. However, reflecting on my experience in cross-country, I resolved to tackle the subject head-on. I began to break down the workload into manageable sections and sought additional tutoring to strengthen my understanding of difficult topics. As the year progressed, my initial fear transformed into a genuine interest in biology, eventually leading to my participation in the Science Olympiad. This experience highlighted the importance of resilience in turning apprehension into curiosity and confidence.

Resilience has also played a crucial role in my personal life. During my junior year, my family went through a difficult period due to my father's unexpected job loss. The uncertainty and stress put a strain on our household, making it hard to concentrate on school or extracurricular activities. However, drawing from the resilience I cultivated in other aspects of my life, I supported my family by taking on additional responsibilities at home and finding ways to contribute positively to our situation. This experience taught me the value of adaptability and optimism, reinforcing my belief that even in challenging circumstances, growth is possible.

As I reflect on these experiences, I see resilience as more than just bouncing back from setbacks; it is about embracing challenges and finding strength in adversity. It has become a fundamental part of my identity, propelling me to approach life's obstacles with determination and hope. The concept of resilience has shaped my personal and academic journey, consistently engaging my thinking and influencing my decisions. It has taught me that while life may present unforeseen challenges, the ability to persevere is within my control. As I prepare to embark on my college journey, I am confident that the resilience I continue to develop will guide me through new challenges and opportunities, helping me to build a future where I can thrive.

In embracing resilience, I have learned invaluable lessons about the importance of tenacity and endurance. These principles will not only aid me in my future academic endeavors but will also serve as a foundation for a fulfilling and meaningful life.

For as long as I can remember, I have been drawn to puzzles. When I was little, that meant scavenging for the corner pieces of jigsaw sets and rearranging them until the picture made sense. As I grew older, the puzzles changed form—from cardboard pieces to computer problems, from illustrated pictures to patterns of logic and code. What never changed was the steady fascination that came from trying to understand how something fits together. That curiosity eventually led me to computer science, a subject that has completely captured my attention and changed the way I approach problems inside and outside the classroom.  

I first encountered computer programming during my freshman year, when I joined a small after‑school coding club. At the time, I had no idea what strings of letters and numbers on a screen could accomplish. When I wrote my first "Hello, world!" program, I was amazed by how a few lines of text could create something new—a message that responded because I told it to. Through that simple project, I began to see the computer as both a puzzle and a tool, something that could be shaped to express ideas.  

As the challenges became harder, I discovered that computer science is not just about typing commands; it’s about problem solving, patience, and creativity. I remember spending hours trying to fix a program that kept crashing. My first instinct was frustration, but after rewriting and researching, I noticed that each mistake revealed something useful. Debugging taught me humility: sometimes failure is simply information in disguise. That mindset began to influence how I approached schoolwork in general. When I ran into a challenging math proof or a confusing chapter in history, I stopped seeing it as an obstacle and started seeing it as another problem to decode.  

What interests me most is how computer science connects to so many other fields. Last year, for example, I worked on a small project that analyzed regional data about air quality. While my program was simple, I realized the power behind it—a few lines of code could make hidden information visible and meaningful. It showed me that technology, when used thoughtfully, can contribute to solutions for real‑world issues. That realization broadened how I think about the world. Computers are more than machines behind screens; they are instruments that can help people communicate, solve medical mysteries, protect the environment, and explore space.  

Yet what keeps me most engaged is not the big futuristic possibilities but the everyday process of learning and improving. Each time I tackle a new problem, I feel that same excitement I felt during my first coding session. There is clarity in watching a program finally run smoothly, satisfaction in knowing that persistence paid off. It has also taught me to appreciate collaboration. Coding often seems like an individual activity, but I’ve learned that the best solutions come from exchanging ideas, explaining logic to others, and absorbing their suggestions. That teamwork has helped me become less self‑conscious about asking questions and more confident in sharing what I know.  

The more I study computer science, the more I realize that it mirrors how I want to live: observant, adaptable, and open to constant learning. The field is always changing, and so are its challenges. That endless evolution excites me rather than intimidates me. It reminds me that understanding the world—like solving a complex puzzle—is never truly finished. There will always be another pattern to uncover, another connection to make, and another moment when everything fits together just right.

The first time I tried to fix our school’s broken 3D printer, I had no idea what I was doing. The machine sat in the corner of the engineering lab, half covered by a tarp, its screen flashing an error I couldn’t decipher. Everyone else had given up on it, deciding it wasn’t worth the trouble. But something about that printer drew me in—not just because I wanted it to work, but because I couldn’t stand seeing a tool for creativity fall silent. That day began a long process of trial, failure, and curiosity that has shaped the way I think and learn.

At first, I tried to solve the problem the way I approached most schoolwork: by following directions. I watched tutorial videos and read the manual, making note of every step. But nothing worked. The printer kept jamming or stopping mid-print. It was frustrating, but also strangely fascinating. Each failure raised new questions. Why did the gears stop rotating? What made the nozzle clog? I started to realize that understanding something fully meant going beyond directions and learning to think through the system itself.

I spent afternoons experimenting, keeping a messy notebook full of sketches, temperature readings, and guesses. I read about thermoplastics and how different materials melt and cool. I talked with my engineering teacher about possible causes and built small tests to isolate variables. Slowly, the pieces of the puzzle started to connect. I discovered that a faulty sensor was misreading the filament feed, and after replacing it, the printer finally came to life, laying down its first smooth layer in months.

That small victory became more than just a technical fix. It made me see how questions can lead to discovery, not just answers. I had moved from following instructions to investigating, from consuming knowledge to creating it. When I later joined the robotics team, that same mindset guided me. Instead of seeing problems as setbacks, I began to see them as invitations to explore. A motor that failed under pressure wasn’t an obstacle; it was a chance to understand torque, balance, and design.

As I followed this curiosity further, I realized I was drawn not only to machines but to systems in general—how things connect and depend on one another. In economics class, I recognized patterns that reminded me of engineering loops: inputs, outputs, and feedbacks that determined outcomes. In biology, I appreciated the precision of physiological systems that operate like coordinated networks. The same spirit of questioning that helped me fix a 3D printer helped me see the invisible relationships that shape everything from ecosystems to economies.

What engages me most now is the process of learning itself—the patient, sometimes tedious search for how and why things work. It doesn’t always lead to immediate success, but it always leads somewhere interesting. The feeling I had when the 3D printer finally printed again was not triumph or relief, but understanding. I realized that curiosity, persistence, and the willingness to fail are all part of thinking deeply.

When I imagine college, I picture an environment full of machines, experiments, and ideas waiting to be explored. I hope to bring the same mindset I discovered in that engineering lab: a determination to look beyond what appears broken, to find patterns and connections, and to approach every question as an opportunity to build something new.

When I was ten, my parents bought a small telescope at a yard sale. It was scratched and a little wobbly, so it took several attempts before I could see anything besides blurry light. One night, after adjusting the focus again and again, I finally caught a clear view of the moon’s edge—its craters sharp and bright. That moment stayed with me, not just because it was beautiful, but because I realized how much patience it took to bring something distant into focus. Since then, my curiosity about space has shaped the way I think and learn.

At first, my fascination was purely visual. I collected space posters, watched documentaries, and memorized facts about planets. But as I grew older, my interest expanded from the images to the ideas behind them. What forces keep galaxies together? How do scientists measure something billions of light-years away? I began to see astronomy not just as something to observe, but as a way of questioning everything we assume about our place in the universe.

In middle school, I joined an after-school science club, where we built small models of rockets and tested them on the soccer field. I loved the combination of creativity and calculation—measuring the angle of the launch, checking the wind, and seeing if our predictions were right. Those experiments gave me a sense of ownership over my learning. It didn’t matter that the rockets sometimes nosedived into the grass; what mattered was understanding why.

During high school, this curiosity deepened through physics. The first time I truly understood Newton’s laws, something clicked. The equations that once looked like meaningless symbols suddenly connected to the flight of those small rockets—and even to the orbit of the moon I’d admired years earlier. I realized science wasn’t a collection of distant facts but a language that explains motion, energy, and balance. I began to approach problems like puzzles, not chores.

Still, what engages me most about studying space is its mixture of the known and the unknown. There’s comfort in the precision of a formula, but there’s also excitement in the mysteries that no formula can yet solve. When astronomers talk about dark matter or the origins of the universe, they admit how much they still don’t know. That humility, combined with persistent curiosity, inspires me. I try to bring that same mindset into other areas of my life—staying open to questions rather than rushing toward easy answers.

One project that tested this was my junior-year research paper on exoplanets. At first, I felt overwhelmed by the technical language in academic articles, but the more I read, the more I understood how discovery builds step by step—how one observation leads to another hypothesis. Writing that paper taught me both patience and precision. It reminded me of adjusting that old telescope: clarity takes time and practice.

Outside the classroom, my interest in space has also connected me to others. I volunteer at our local library’s “Science Saturdays,” where younger kids explore topics like gravity and light through simple experiments. Watching their faces light up when they see how a flashlight and a mirror can create a rainbow brings me back to my own first spark of wonder. I’ve learned that curiosity is contagious, and sharing it deepens it.

Sometimes I think about that original telescope, still sitting on a shelf in my room. It’s a reminder of where my thinking began—with the desire to see more clearly. Whether I’m studying astrophysics, analyzing data, or simply looking up at the night sky, that same motivation guides me. I want to understand complex systems, from the physical forces that move the planets to the human drive that pushes us to ask how and why.

Ultimately, the subject that engages my thinking most—space—has taught me as much about life on Earth as about the stars. It has shown me that exploration starts with curiosity, grows through persistence, and never truly ends. I am eager to keep asking questions, to keep focusing and refocusing until each new discovery, however small, comes into view.

When my tenth-grade biology class began its unit on genetics, I expected another stretch of memorizing vocabulary and filling charts. I had always done well in science, but mostly because I was methodical, not because the material sparked any excitement. That changed the day our teacher demonstrated how a single mutation in DNA can alter the structure of a protein and cause a visible change in an organism. Watching that process unfold on the screen, I realized that behind every small biological detail lay an invisible chain of logic. It was as if a hidden code governed everything living, from a blade of grass to each of us sitting in the classroom.

After that lesson, I found myself thinking constantly about how information is stored and transmitted. I stayed after class to ask my teacher why some mutations were harmful while others led to beneficial adaptations. She suggested that I look into evolutionary genetics, and I began reading articles and watching lectures online with increasing fascination. For the first time, I wasn’t studying for a grade; I was studying because I wanted to understand. The idea that life could be both unpredictable and rule-bound struck me as beautiful. It reminded me that complexity does not always come from chaos, but often from patterns repeated in slightly different ways.

By the start of junior year, I decided to join the science club, something I had avoided before because I assumed it was only for “serious” students. Instead, I discovered a group of people who were just as curious as I had become. We spent afternoons planning small experiments: testing how plants responded to different light wavelengths, or comparing the bacteria that grew on our phones to those on school door handles. These were modest projects, but designing them felt like creating miniature versions of the discoveries I had admired in my readings. I learned how to narrow a question, form a hypothesis, and accept that results often contradicted expectations.

One experiment became a turning point for me. I was analyzing how temperature affected enzyme activity, but my data did not match what the textbook predicted. At first I assumed I had made a mistake, and I repeated the experiment multiple times. Eventually I realized that my samples had been exposed to a draft from a nearby air vent, changing their temperature more unevenly than I had measured. While the outcome was flawed scientifically, it taught me how fragile and yet valuable real observation can be. Since then, I have learned to pay attention not only to the answer but also to the process that produces it.

As my interest deepened, I started viewing other subjects differently. Reading literature, I began noticing how authors construct characters whose choices resemble genetic mutations—small deviations leading to major consequences. In history, I looked for the “inheritance” of ideas passed down through generations. Even in math, I saw parallels between the logic of equations and the logic of life. What once seemed like isolated disciplines became, in my mind, variations on the same theme: how information changes and persists.

Now, as I plan for college, I know I want to pursue biology not just as a subject, but as a way of thinking. It requires curiosity balanced by discipline, imagination guided by evidence. It challenges me to accept uncertainty while seeking patterns. I don’t yet know which branch of the field I will follow—perhaps molecular genetics, perhaps bioinformatics—but I know that I want to keep asking questions that reveal the hidden systems shaping our world. The lesson I first glimpsed in that tenth-grade classroom remains the same: beneath the surface of every living thing lies a story written in code, waiting for someone to learn how to read it.

Most people assume that attention is something automatic, a light that turns on when something is important. I used to think that too. Then, during my sophomore year, I started noticing how often my mind slipped away from what I was doing. I would open my laptop to finish homework and, without really deciding to, end up checking messages, reading headlines, or jumping between tabs. Nothing dramatic had happened, but I began to wonder who was really in charge of my thinking: me, or whatever was loudest at the moment.

That question has engaged me ever since. It began as a small frustration, but it turned into a subject I think about almost every day. I started paying attention to attention itself. I noticed that my concentration was not simply a matter of discipline or laziness. It was shaped by habits, surroundings, technology, and even the way I talked to myself. When I told myself to “focus,” I often became more aware of my distraction. When I removed my phone from my desk, however, work seemed easier before I had used any willpower at all.

Once I started seeing this pattern, I began experimenting with it. I kept a notebook where I wrote down when I worked best, when I drifted, and what conditions made a difference. I found that I could read difficult material longer in the library than in my room, and that a clear plan helped more than vague determination. Even ten uninterrupted minutes often mattered more than an hour of half-attention. These observations were simple, but they changed the way I approached school and the rest of my life.

The subject grew larger for me because it connected personal experience to bigger questions. Why do so many tools designed to help us think also compete for our attention? Why does being busy sometimes feel productive even when little gets done? Why is quiet increasingly uncomfortable? These questions do not have easy answers, but they have made me more curious about psychology, design, and human behavior. I have come to see attention as more than a study skill. It is a way of deciding what deserves a place in our minds.

This idea has also changed my relationships. When I am distracted while someone is speaking, I am not only missing information; I am failing to show respect. Real attention is one of the clearest forms of care. I have started putting my phone away during conversations and trying to listen without preparing my response in advance. That sounds basic, but it has made me more present with friends and family, and it has shown me how rare it is to feel fully heard.

I am still far from mastering my own attention. Some days I am focused, and some days I am scattered. But that is partly why this subject continues to engage me. It is not a problem I can solve once and forget. It is an ongoing practice and an ongoing question. In college, I want to keep exploring how people think, what shapes their choices, and how environments can either fragment or strengthen the mind. Learning, I have realized, is not only about gaining new ideas. It is also about learning how to hold on to them.

When my grandfather began forgetting the names of everyday objects, my family started labeling things around the house. “Spoon” went on the kitchen drawer. “Towel” went on the bathroom shelf. “Shoes” appeared by the door in my mother’s careful handwriting. At first, the labels seemed like a practical response to a difficult problem. Over time, they became something else for me: a visible reminder that language is not just a school subject or a tool for communication, but one of the main ways people hold on to the world.

Before that year, I had mostly thought of words as things to use correctly. I liked grammar, memorized vocabulary, and enjoyed the satisfaction of finding the precise phrase in an essay. Language felt orderly. It was a system that could be studied and mastered. Watching my grandfather struggle changed that idea. I saw that when a word disappears, what is lost is not only a sound or a definition. Something in the relationship between a person and an object weakens. A chair is still there, but it becomes strangely distant if you can no longer call it a chair.

That realization began to follow me into other parts of my life. In my Spanish class, I started paying closer attention to the fact that some ideas moved easily between languages while others did not. A direct translation often carried the meaning but not the feeling. At home, I noticed how my parents changed their tone, humor, and even values slightly depending on whether they were speaking English or our first language. In English, my father sounded more concise and practical. In our home language, he became more expressive and patient. I became interested in the possibility that language does not simply reflect thought, but shapes it.

I started reading about linguistics on my own, first out of curiosity and then with real commitment. I learned about aphasia, language acquisition, and the debate over whether grammar is learned or innate. I was especially drawn to the question of how people create meaning from limited symbols. A small set of sounds can produce poems, arguments, promises, and prayers. Children can generate sentences they have never heard before. People can speak across time through books, across distance through screens, and across loss through memory. The more I learned, the less language seemed ordinary.

This interest also changed how I listened. During lunch at school, I began noticing the small shifts in speech that signal belonging. A student uses slang with one friend and formal language with a teacher. Someone shortens a story to sound casual, or stretches it to hold attention. In debate club, I saw that persuasion depends not only on evidence but on framing. The same facts can sound urgent, reassuring, or doubtful depending on word choice and rhythm. I became more aware that language is never neutral. It carries power, identity, and assumption all at once.

At the same time, my grandfather’s world grew smaller. There were days when he could still tell stories from his childhood in vivid detail, and other days when he would stop in the middle of a sentence and look frustrated, as if the right word were standing just beyond a locked door. I could not unlock it for him. What I could do was sit with him, point to the labels, and wait. Those moments taught me a different side of language: not performance, but patience. Communication is not always about saying more. Sometimes it is about meeting someone halfway when words fail.

The subject that has engaged my thinking, then, is language itself, not only as an academic field but as a human bond. It is structure and improvisation, science and art. It can clarify ideas, but it can also preserve dignity. I want to keep studying how language works in the brain, in communities, and in relationships because I have seen what happens when it weakens and what it makes possible when it holds. The labels in my grandfather’s house were simple pieces of paper. To me, they became a beginning: proof that even ordinary words are carrying more of our lives than we realize.

At first, I thought maps were mostly about directions. They told you how to get from one place to another, how far two cities were, or where a river curved around a town. I liked them in a practical way, the same way I liked schedules and neatly labeled folders. But over time, maps became something more to me. They turned into a way of thinking, a way of asking questions about people, choices, and the spaces we create for ourselves.

My interest began with a simple habit. On long car rides, I would stare at the navigation screen even when I already knew where we were going. I liked watching the blue line adjust when we missed a turn. I liked seeing roads branch into neighborhoods and then disappear into smaller streets. At some point, I stopped seeing the map as a background tool and started seeing it as a story. Every road had a reason for being there. Every boundary had been decided by someone. Every blank space on the screen made me wonder what had been left out.

That curiosity followed me beyond highways. In middle school, I spent a lot of time looking at historical maps online. I compared old maps of my town with current ones and noticed how ponds had been filled, roads had been widened, and farms had become parking lots. The town I thought I knew suddenly looked temporary. Places that seemed permanent had only existed for a few decades. The map showed me that communities are not fixed. They are revised, layer by layer, often in ways most people barely notice.

As I kept thinking about maps, I realized they are never completely neutral. A map can guide, but it can also emphasize. It can clarify, but it can also erase. What gets labeled matters. What gets centered matters. Even the choice of colors can make one region seem more important than another. This idea changed the way I looked at information in general. I became more aware that every chart, article, and image reflects decisions about perspective. Instead of asking only, “What does this show?” I started asking, “Why does it show this, and what does it leave out?”

That question became especially important to me during a local volunteer project. I worked with a community group helping organize donated food deliveries to different apartment complexes. We used a printed map to plan routes, and at first it seemed straightforward. But after a few weekends, I noticed that the map did not capture the difficulty of the actual trips. It did not show which buildings had confusing entrances, which sidewalks were broken, or which families were likely to be working multiple jobs and unavailable at certain times. The official map was accurate in one sense, but incomplete in another. We ended up drawing our own notes all over it. By the end, the paper looked messy, but it was more truthful because it reflected lived experience.

Since then, I have been engaged by the idea that knowledge is both structured and unfinished. Maps appeal to me because they combine precision and uncertainty. They represent the world carefully, yet they also remind me that no representation can be total. I find that balance intellectually exciting. It has made me more patient with complexity and more interested in the systems people build to understand reality.

I know I will probably keep returning to maps, whether I study geography, public policy, history, or something else entirely. What engages my thinking is not only the object itself but the habit of mind it encourages. A map asks you to locate yourself, but it also asks you to notice what lies beyond the edges. For me, that is the beginning of learning: finding where I am, recognizing what I do not yet see, and wanting to look more closely.

When I was younger, I thought maps were just tools for getting from one place to another. They were the folded papers in my parents’ car, the route lines on a phone screen, or the diagrams hanging in classrooms. Over time, though, maps became something much more interesting to me. I started to see them not only as guides to location, but as records of choice, perspective, and human intention. The subject of maps, and more broadly the way people represent space, has engaged my thinking because it shows me how information can be both useful and deeply shaped by the people who create it.

My interest began with a simple habit. I would zoom in and out of online maps for no real reason, tracing roads through unfamiliar towns and following rivers until they disappeared into larger bodies of water. At first, I was just curious about places I had never visited. Soon I began noticing patterns. Highways seemed to connect some communities while skirting around others. Borders looked sharp on a screen, even though I knew the realities behind them were often complicated. Some locations were labeled in bold print, while others seemed almost invisible. The more I looked, the more I wondered what determined what appeared important.

That question led me to think about maps less as neutral objects and more as arguments. A map always includes and excludes. It chooses scale, names, colors, boundaries, and symbols. It tells you where to look. Even a subway map, which seems purely practical, simplifies distance and geography in order to make movement understandable. That realization changed how I saw other forms of information too. Charts, essays, news stories, and even conversations all work in similar ways. They do not simply present reality. They organize it.

One experience made this especially clear to me. In a history class, I compared several maps of the same region from different time periods. Although they represented similar land, they seemed to tell entirely different stories. One emphasized political control, another trade routes, and another physical geography. None of them were false, but none of them were complete. I remember being struck by how the same place could be redefined depending on the priorities of the person drawing it. That moment stayed with me because it made an abstract idea feel concrete: perspective is not a minor detail added after the fact, but a central part of how knowledge is built.

Since then, I have found myself drawn to questions that begin with “Why is this arranged this way?” I ask that when reading an article, looking at data, or studying a historical event. Maps trained me to notice structure. They made me more aware of what is centered, what is left at the edges, and what assumptions guide a presentation. I do not think this habit has made me cynical. Instead, it has made me more attentive. If anything, it has increased my respect for the challenge of trying to represent a complex world clearly and responsibly.

What engages me most is that mapping combines precision with interpretation. It depends on measurement, but it also depends on judgment. That balance appeals to me because I am interested in both analysis and meaning. I like the idea that something can be technically accurate and still raise deeper questions about whose viewpoint it reflects.

I know I am not drawn only to maps themselves. I am drawn to the larger problem they represent: how people make sense of complexity. That problem appears in science, history, politics, and everyday life. College appeals to me because it is a place where I can keep asking those questions in more rigorous ways. A map may seem like a finished product, but to me it is the beginning of an inquiry. It reminds me that every representation of the world is also an invitation to think more carefully about it.

The first time I truly understood the weight of a single question, I was sitting in my junior year philosophy elective, and our teacher asked us something deceptively simple: "Can you ever truly know another person?" I remember the room going quiet for a moment before a few students offered quick answers, mostly along the lines of "sure, if you spend enough time with them." But something about the question lodged itself in my mind and refused to leave. It followed me home, sat with me at dinner, and kept me awake that night staring at my ceiling.

Before that class, I had never really questioned the nature of human connection. I assumed that knowing someone was a straightforward process. You learn their favorite foods, their fears, the stories from their childhood, and eventually you accumulate enough information to say you know them. But the more I thought about it, the more I realized that knowing facts about a person and truly knowing them might be entirely different things. My best friend since elementary school, someone I have spent thousands of hours with, still surprises me regularly. She will react to something in a way I never would have predicted, and in that moment I am reminded that there is an entire inner world inside her that I will never fully access.

This realization did not make me sad, though. Instead, it made me curious. I started reading about the philosophy of mind and the problem of other minds, which is the idea that we can never directly experience anyone else's consciousness. I found myself drawn to thinkers like Thomas Nagel, whose famous essay "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?" argues that subjective experience is fundamentally inaccessible to outside observers. If we cannot even imagine what it is like to be a bat, how can we claim to fully understand another human being whose inner life is shaped by experiences completely different from our own?

This line of thinking changed the way I interact with people on a daily basis. I became a better listener, not because I learned some technique, but because I genuinely started approaching every conversation with the understanding that the person in front of me contains depths I cannot see. When my younger brother gets frustrated over something that seems trivial to me, I pause now instead of dismissing him. His experience of that frustration is real and complex, even if I cannot feel it myself. When a classmate shares an opinion I disagree with in a discussion, I find myself more interested in understanding how they arrived at that opinion than in proving them wrong.

I also started to appreciate the role that vulnerability plays in bridging the gap between people. While we may never fully know another person, the act of trying matters enormously. Every honest conversation is an attempt to narrow that distance, even if we can never close it completely. I think there is something beautiful about that effort, about caring enough to keep trying to understand someone even when perfect understanding is impossible.

This idea has also shaped my academic interests. I want to study psychology and philosophy in college because I want to keep exploring the boundaries of human understanding. I am fascinated by questions about empathy, consciousness, and what it means to connect with another person in a meaningful way. These are not questions with clean answers, and that is exactly what draws me to them. I have learned that the most important questions are often the ones that resist easy resolution.

Looking back, I am grateful for that single moment in a philosophy classroom that cracked open my thinking. It taught me that intellectual engagement does not always come from textbooks or assignments. Sometimes it comes from a question that catches you off guard and refuses to let you go. That question about knowing other people did not give me an answer, but it gave me something better. It gave me a new way of seeing the world and the people in it.

The first time I really thought about water was when ours stopped working. I was fourteen, and a pipe burst in our basement during a cold snap in January.
