At my high school, there are maybe fifteen of us who could be described this way, though we found each other less through recognition than through accident. My friend Yesenia and I realized our kinship sophomore year when we both showed up to an AP Chemistry class with fee waiver forms instead of the fifty-dollar materials fee. We didn't discuss it. We just knew. After that, we started studying together, sharing the textbook we'd both borrowed from a teacher because neither of us could afford to buy one.

What Yesenia taught me, without meaning to, is that helping each other is not charity. It's infrastructure. Her older sister had taken AP English two years before her and left behind annotated books and essay drafts in a Google folder titled "For Yesenia." When I saw it, I felt something shift in my chest. Someone had thought ahead. Someone had decided that what they'd figured out was worth passing forward.

I started doing the same thing. I made a document listing every scholarship I'd found that doesn't require citizenship status. I shared it in a group chat with eight people, and they added more, and now it has forty-three entries and I've never even met some of the people who contributed. That document is a small thing, but it is the most honest thing I have made. It is my community writing itself into existence.

The hardest part of being first-generation isn't the not-knowing. It's the not-knowing what you don't know. You can't ask your parents which professors to seek out or how to read a financial aid package because they've never seen one either. The gaps are invisible until you fall into them. What my community has taught me is to look for each other at the bottom of those gaps and figure out how to climb out together.

My father's hands are something I think about when I get tired. He did not go to college. He has carried boxes in the cold for twenty years so that I could sit in a warm room and learn things. I do not want to just graduate and walk away from that history. I want to go to college and return to my community changed and useful, the way Yesenia's sister left that folder behind, saying: I was here, and now here is what I learned, and it belongs to you.

My grandmother keeps a notebook full of phone numbers written in pencil, because she says ink is too permanent. She crosses people out and rewrites them when they move, when they change, when they become someone new. I grew up watching her do this at the kitchen table in our apartment in Bridgeport, Connecticut, updating her list of everyone in our Salvadoran community like she was tending something alive.

I am the first person in my family who will go to college. I say this plainly, without the weight people sometimes expect me to perform around it. What I feel when I say it is not triumph exactly—it is more like vertigo, the particular dizziness of standing at an edge and looking in both directions at once.

The community I belong to is the one that fills my grandmother's notebook. It is the women from her church who babysit each other's children in rotating shifts, the man named Ernesto who translates documents at the library on Tuesdays, the WhatsApp group my mother has named "Familia y Amigos" where someone is always posting a prayer or a plumber recommendation or a picture of a quinceañera. It is loud and specific and impossible to summarize. It is also, in ways I am still learning to articulate, the thing that shaped my mind.

When I was twelve, my mother lost her job at the packaging warehouse. For three weeks, neighbors left food outside our door—tamales wrapped in foil, a bag of plantains, once a full pot of soup that was still warm. Nobody announced it. Nobody asked to be thanked. I remember standing in the doorway holding that pot and feeling something I didn't have a word for yet, something that has since become the center of how I understand generosity: it was structural, not individual. It was a system people had built from necessity and kept running out of habit and love.

I started thinking about systems. I took AP Economics junior year partly because I wanted a language for what I'd witnessed. I learned about informal economies and social capital and mutual aid, and I kept recognizing our neighborhood in the definitions. But the textbook made it sound historical, or distant, the way museums make things sound. What I knew was current and breathing.

This is where community has taught me to be skeptical of abstraction. My grandmother does not update her notebook because of social capital theory. She does it because Marisol moved to Meriden and still deserves a birthday call. The difference matters to me, and I think it will matter in how I study and what questions I ask.

I also belong to this community in the way that creates friction, which I think is the honest thing to say. Going to college means I will leave for months at a time. It means I will learn things that change the way I see, and then I will come home and sit at that kitchen table and feel the distance between what I know and what I can say. My cousin José did this when he came back from the Army. I watched him searching for the right word in the wrong language, reaching across some gap that hadn't been there before. I think about him often.

What I want to say is that I do not plan to resolve this tension neatly. I do not believe the narrative where education lifts you out of your community like a prize. I believe in my grandmother's notebook. I believe that belonging to something means you carry it with you and that it changes shape as you change but does not disappear. I am going to college because of what I learned in that community—about systems, about care, about the work of keeping people legible to one another.

I am bringing the notebook with me. I will add names in pencil, because she taught me that people become someone new, and that this is not loss.

My grandfather keeps a notebook. It's spiral-bound, navy blue, and the cover is warped from years of being stuffed into the pocket of his work pants. Inside, in handwriting that leans hard to the right like it's trying to escape the page, he recorded every bill he ever paid. Electricity. Rent. The hospital when my father was born. Numbers crossed out and rewritten, budgets squeezed and re-squeezed. He gave it to me the summer before senior year. "So you know where you come from," he said, which at the time I thought was about money.

I am the first in my family to apply to a four-year university. My parents immigrated from the Philippines before I was born, settling in Daly City in a neighborhood where everyone's parents worked the same rotating hospital shifts and kept rice cookers running around the clock. Growing up, I understood college as a word that meant later, something you reached for the way you reached for a light switch in a dark room — urgent, necessary, but never fully visible until you were already there.

The community I belong to is not defined by a single culture or a single ZIP code. It's defined by the particular weight of being the first. The first means your parents cannot help you fill out the FAFSA because they have never seen one. It means watching your school counselor's eyes flicker — just briefly — when you list your intended major as biochemistry. It means your older cousins, the ones who took community college courses and stopped, look at you with something you eventually understand is hope wearing the mask of skepticism.

Junior year, a classmate asked me who was helping me with my college essays. I listed my English teacher, a college access nonprofit, YouTube tutorials on the Common App. He laughed, not unkindly, and said his college counselor had already reviewed three drafts. I didn't feel sorry for myself in that moment. I felt something sharper — the specific clarity of understanding exactly what you're working against, which is different from defeat.

What being a first-generation student has taught me is that there are two kinds of knowledge. There's the knowledge that gets transmitted, handed down through dinner table conversations and family friends who went to the same schools and know which professors to take and which to avoid. And then there's the knowledge you have to construct yourself, brick by brick, from incomplete instructions. My family gave me the second kind. They gave me the habit of figuring things out from raw materials — my mother, who taught herself medical billing from library books; my father, who watched YouTube videos to learn how to repair the car he couldn't afford to take to a mechanic. I grew up watching them solve problems that had no pre-existing template, and I absorbed that as normal.

What I've found is that my community — these other first-generation students, many of us sitting in the same library cubicles, texting each other links to fee waivers and scholarship deadlines — we share something that is genuinely hard to articulate without sounding like a motivational poster. It's not resilience, exactly. Resilience implies we bent and sprang back. It's more like fluency. We became fluent in systems that were not designed with us in mind, and that fluency is a form of intelligence I've come to take seriously.

My grandfather's notebook wasn't just about money. It was a record of attention — proof that every number mattered, that nothing was too small to document and honor. I think about it when I feel overwhelmed by everything I don't yet know about the college process. I open a note on my phone and I start listing: what I've learned, what I've figured out, what I still need to find. I am building my own notebook. I don't know yet what I'll hand to someone else someday, but I know I'll have something to give.

My grandmother keeps a notebook. It's spiral-bound, red, and so stuffed with loose papers that a rubber band holds it shut. Inside: recipes, addresses of cousins in Oaxaca, phone numbers for doctors who speak Spanish, a folded photocopy of her naturalization certificate. She built this notebook over forty years because nobody told her where anything was. She had to find everything herself, write it down, and hope the next person would ask.

I am the next person who asked.

Growing up in Boyle Heights, I belonged to a community defined less by what we had than by what we were learning to navigate. My neighbors were busboys, seamstresses, night-shift security guards — people who sent their kids to school with notes that said *please help him with this form* because the form was in English and the fear of getting it wrong was enormous. I watched my mother spend an entire Saturday on hold with the Department of Motor Vehicles, passing the phone to me whenever the automated system demanded spoken responses. I was eleven. I did not know what a lien was. I figured it out.

That figuring-out became the texture of my adolescence. When my cousin Yesenia got accepted to community college and had no idea what FAFSA meant, I sat across from her at the kitchen table with a laptop and we worked through it together for three hours. When our neighbor Mr. Alcántara's son got a disciplinary notice from school, and Mr. Alcántara couldn't parse the language — "a formal hearing regarding a Level II infraction" — he knocked on our door and I translated, not just the words but what they actually meant for his son's record. I became someone people came to, not because I was exceptional, but because I had accumulated a specific and urgent kind of literacy.

I used to feel embarrassed by this role. My friends at school talked about their parents handling college stuff, SAT prep, all of it. I was Googling "what is an AP exam fee waiver" at midnight, alone, because no one around me had been here before. It felt like running a race where everyone else had been handed a map.

But I've started to understand the notebook differently.

My grandmother didn't write things down because she was weak. She wrote things down because she was thinking about me before I existed. The notebook is infrastructure. It's her saying: *I paid the cost of not knowing so you don't have to pay it fully.* And what I've built — the hours on phone calls, the sitting-beside people with forms, the willingness to say "I don't know but let's find out together" — that is also infrastructure. It lives in people's confidence. In Yesenia finishing her financial aid. In Mr. Alcántara's son keeping his clean record.

What my community gave me is a particular seriousness about knowledge. Information isn't abstract for us. It's the difference between someone's parent getting their license renewed or not. It's whether a kid understands his rights in that hearing room. I don't experience learning as a luxury or an ornament. I experience it as something with stakes.

I want to study public policy because I want to build the systems that shouldn't require a notebook to navigate. I want to be in rooms where those decisions get made, carrying the specific knowledge of what it costs when systems assume everyone already knows the language.

But I also want to keep the notebook. Not the red spiral one — that belongs to my grandmother, rubber band and all. My own version. Filled with what I've learned, who showed me, what it took. Left open on the table for whoever comes and knocks on the door and needs to figure something out.

I'll be there. I know how to do this part.

The gravel driveway crunches under my tires as I pull up to the one-pump gas station at the heart of our town. Mullins, population 1,257, is the kind of place where everyone knows your grandmother’s apple pie recipe and whether or not you showed up to church last Sunday. It’s the kind of place where community isn’t just a word; it’s the air we breathe and the rhythm we walk to. In Mullins, we’re like a family, sometimes annoyingly close-knit, but mostly supportive, in the way that truly matters.

Growing up here, my connection to this community was woven through shared experiences and stories that only make sense in this tiny dot on the map. Take Friday nights, for instance. The sun barely dips below the horizon before cars line the field next to the high school. The entire town seems to funnel into the bleachers at our football games, regardless of the standings. Even those less interested in the game itself find a spot to chat about the gossip du jour or what Mrs. Hennessey is knitting this week.

Playing trumpet in the marching band, I learned the true essence of teamwork. Balancing the squawks of instruments with our steps, we were a patchwork quilt, each note and player a different color and texture. During one particular homecoming parade, it started to drizzle right as we stepped off. Kids dressed in Halloween costumes lined the streets, candy bags clutched tightly as umbrellas bobbed like mushrooms in a field. As the rain soaked through our uniforms, I expected the crowd to disperse. Instead, they cheered louder, an echoing warmth in the gray downpour. Their encouragement wasn’t just for the score on the board but for every one of us, from athletes to band members to cheerleaders.

When Grandma fell ill last spring, this town was our rock. It was like the scenes from those black-and-white movies where neighbors show up at your door with casseroles and enough soup to drown a small country. The women’s auxiliary at church organized meal trains, and notes filled with prayers and well-wishes piled high on our kitchen table. Sitting by her hospital bed, I read those words aloud, simple yet profound in their earnestness. Each note was a reminder that we weren’t alone and that every handshake and smile at the Farmers' Market contributed to this protective weave of care.

Not all parts of small-town life are idyllic, of course. There’s an intimate complexity that comes with growing up in a place where everybody knows your business. When I once dyed my hair neon-blue on a whim, I couldn’t escape the raised eyebrows for weeks. Yet through these experiences, I’ve learned resilience and the courage to embrace differences as just another part of our collective identity. It’s a skill I’ve honed: turning whispers into laughter, and scrutiny into acceptance.

This relationship with Mullins has taught me that community is less about geography and more about spirit. It’s there in the ways we rally around each other, and in how we cherish the everyday moments shared. These moments, like the taste of apple pie still warm from the oven or the sound of cicadas underscoring summer evenings, shape who I am and guide who I aim to become. 

As I look toward college and the burgeoning world beyond, I carry with me the spirit of Mullins. I envision nurturing communities wherever I go, drawing from this deep well of understanding and support that my small-town life has gifted me. With every unfamiliar place and person I meet, I hope to plant the seeds of belonging that I’ve harvested here, in a town that may be small in size but immense in heart.

The heartbeat of my community is the grain elevator where the corn gets weighed and measured before it’s sent off to places I can only imagine. Here, in our small rural town where the school buses have more miles than I have probably traveled in my lifetime, everything seems like a closed circle: the sunrise at the crack of dawn and the sunset staining the fields in warm hues that one can feel as much as see. Our town is centered around agriculture, and though the seasons rotate predictably, each year feels like a new story waiting to be unfolded on the endless acres of farmland.

Growing up, I was always the one with dairy stains on my jeans, splattered from early morning calf feedings, and a geometry book open during lunchtime, trying to balance chores with school. Our town is small enough that everybody knows everybody else's business, but big enough that you can always find a friend at the county fair. Every Saturday, my brothers and I would sit on worn hay bales with other farm kids our age and plan our futures in between bites of homemade pie—a slice of life the town proudly claims its own.

This community wraps itself around each member like a comforting old quilt, full of patches and stories, worn but sturdy. Each person carries their own thread, woven into the fabric of our town’s narrative. On Sunday mornings, as hymns drift like a fresh breeze out the open church doors, the shared spirit of togetherness suffuses the crisp air. My fingers have learned to turn pages of hymnals almost as easily as they handle a plow, feeling connected to the generations that have sat in these same pews before me.

Every autumn, the harvest festival pulls us from the fields and into conversation. Working alongside seventy-year-olds who remember when horses dragged the plows, arm in arm with kids as young as seven hauling pumpkins into trucks, I understand that community is not just where you plant your feet but where you invest your heart. It’s as though time spins slowly in our tiny town but deeply, encircling us all in shared stories and labors like rings in an ancient tree.

Education is one area where I've felt the roots and the reach of my community most poignantly. Our school is a place where history lessons are often taught with local stories woven in, told by teachers who once sat in our very seats. I’ve had the privilege—and sometimes the burden—of learning in a place where a high school class barely hits double digits and where everyone's voice has space to echo, making your failures seem as public as your victories. But it's here I learned the power of listening and lending a hand, lessons not found in textbooks but in shared glances across chipped wooden desks.

Perhaps the most profound understanding I've developed within this community is how to collaborate, leaning into the idea that one's individual success is always tied to the growth and resilience of others. We are a symbiosis of hard work and shared laughter—a town united in its effort to create a corner of the world that feels right with every sunrise.

As I prepare to leave for college, my parents' proud voices echo in every corner of our modest home. My acceptance is a part of them, an extension of our community’s aspirations. I carry with me not just the lessons learned in this close-knit community but the sense of determination that rural living instills—a quiet persistence honed against cold winds and hot sun, where fields stretch farther than dreams once reached.

I know stepping outside this circle means tales of my own to cultivate in places where buildings stand taller than any grain tower. Yet, the heartbeat of my town will always set my rhythm, simple and constant, reminding me of where my journey began.

Dust caressed my worn-out sneakers as I walked the familiar, gravel road home from school. My small town, population 1,232, sits cradled by a patchwork of sun-drenched fields and meandering creeks. At a glance, we're fifty miles from a movie theater or recognizable chain restaurant; unhurried, untouched by fast-paced convenience, this is Applewood. It's as much a part of me as the freckles on my face or the drawl that occasionally dances through my speech. Growing up here feels like living in a postcard, if postcards had the aroma of freshly mown hay and sun-baked earth. However, Applewood's true identity isn't captured in a snapshot; it thrives in the community simmering beneath the surface.

Coffee breaks at Tilly’s Diner are tinted with the soft buzz of lawnmowers and the distant trill of cicadas, where Mr. Thompson, the postman, knows everyone’s birthday better than most. The community baseball team, the Applewood Aviators, has been my family’s pastime since I was old enough to hold a mini-bat, my grandfather in the stands weaving yarns of games won by whiskers in the 1970s. It's not the games themselves but the tapestry of shared experiences and whispered traditions that fills me with an undeniable sense of belonging.

As I grew older, I took on patchwork responsibilities that mirrored the fabric of my town. Perhaps the most profound was my time as part of the volunteer fire department. Walking into the station for the first time, I was met with the peppered hair and calloused hands of people who had known me since diapers, their eyes expectant but not unkind. This was a rite of unpredictable passages, a silent agreement to be there for each other through cornfield blazes or the more demanding conflagrations of life.

On twilight drills, I'd feel the humid breath of the Kentucky air puff against my neck, with crickets chirping as my fellow volunteers and I rehearsed, practiced, prepared. There was a silent pact, an unspoken promise that burned hotter than any fire we were called to quell. It was about trust—trust in each strap we buckled, in the hoses we grasped, and in the knowing nod that passed among us when duty called. I learned, not just how to operate the water hydrant, but how to fortify the invisible threads that bind a community together.

Despite its size, or maybe because of it, Applewood nurtures a spirit that's uncommonly resilient. Aunt May's apple pies, a local legend, are baked with an unsolicited sweetness she claims cures even life’s most bitter disappointments. I recall the summer when Melanie’s barn caught fire; it wasn't just the fire department that answered the call. The whole town arrived. Kids like me, some of whom I competed against in spelling bees, heaved buckets alongside seasoned farmers. Our hands rolled out the same history, each motion knitted with cultural stitches and familial seams.

These ties transcend mere neighborliness; they're born from shared soil and a collective will. Here, every farewell is unavoidably bittersweet, a reminder of our tiny global bubble’s impermanence. As I anticipate trekking beyond familiar fields to pursue higher education, I carry fragile but unyielding portions of Applewood with me, hoping to share them wherever I roam.

Sometimes, when the evening sky turns soft orange and the day wanes, I climb the faded wooden steps of our porch and feel the world slow down. I think of the unchanging lane that leads home and how, despite the uncertainties that lie beyond, this place has taught me the foundational art of connection. It's easy to shrug off small-town roots as a hindrance, but Applewood holds whispers of persistent truths. It isn't the space I occupy, but the community I embrace that informs the person I continue to become. Whether battling metaphorical fires in a college setting or bridging cultural chasms with unfamiliar classmates, the creed of Applewood endures: It's not merely about finding my place in the world. It's about creating a world where everyone feels they belong.

The creaking bleachers of Miller High School's gym hold more memories than I can recount. From the annual Thanksgiving basketball tournament to school dances with streamers sagging under the weight of our collective teenage awkwardness, the gym is more than a place; it’s the heartbeat of our small town. Nestled amidst endless fields and crisscrossed by dirt roads, our community is tiny enough that everyone's name is known, yet vast in the tapestry of stories woven between us.

As the daughter of the town’s only veterinarian, my days often began at the local farm where I would help my dad attend to cows that had somehow found a sharp edge to cut themselves on, or a horse with a mysterious limp. Patchy overalls and muck boots were my childhood attire, and the sweet smell of hay often lingered on my clothes, a scent that tethered me to this land like a whispered promise.

Our town, Maple Ridge, is one where the air smells of earth and the sounds of life are a symphony of distant tractor engines, birds, and laughter echoing across fields. Life here is simple, in ways both beautiful and challenging. It’s the kind of place where honesty and kindness matter; filler words like "fine" and "okay" won’t do when you ask someone how they’re doing.

One could say my relationship with our community is akin to rooting oneself under an expansive, blue sky—a continual grounding amidst vastness. My sense of belonging here was most profoundly felt when we rallied together during the flood of '21. The normally placid Maple River had swelled into a monster, and it was as if the whole town exhaled in unison—faces drawn to the rising, muddy waters that threatened to swallow everything. I remember standing shoulder to shoulder with neighbors, forming a line like an assembly of ants beneath towering piles of sandbags. Despite the fear knotting in my stomach, there was a comfort in the closeness and a silent understanding passed from one gaze to another.

It’s strange how necessity blurs boundaries: those I seldom spoke with became the folks I ate hastily-prepared sandwiches within a church basement, laughing despite the bleakness outside. Standing knee-deep in water, I felt the pulse of community—strong, unwavering. We were individuals bound by circumstance but united through genuine care for one another.

Yet, amid this unity, I often found myself questioning my place here. Our town is not immune to the drawbacks of insularity: perspectives are sometimes narrow, change is approached with caution, and the world beyond is often regarded with skepticism. I am both of this place and yearning for the horizon. Classes in environmental science opened my eyes to ecosystems beyond our farms, where biodiversity isn’t just a term but a vibrant tapestry of living things. I’ve spent late nights perched on my windowsill, imagining what lays beyond the starlit silhouette of Willow Creek.

Leading the school’s nature club became an outlet for bridging my rural roots with a more global perspective, one that considers sustainability beyond grain synthetics and crop yields. We started small—a recycling drive during the summer softball league games, using seed paper for projects, cleaner energy discussions—but it was a beginning.

The heart of my relationship with Maple Ridge lies in this dance of gratitude and gentle rebellion, seeking to honor my roots while stretching towards the expansive unknown. I’m aware that the world is so much larger than our weather-beaten fences, but these pastures have become both my launching pad and anchor.

The essence of belonging, I’ve learned, is mirrored in the fields that surround us: growth is a season, ever-changing, and dependent on the soil from which it springs. Soon, I will leave this patch of earth, taking with me the lessons learned and the strength of knowing my roots run deep, whether I'm here or a thousand miles away.

The town limit sign is crooked, leaning like it’s too tired to stand straight after years of prairie wind. Beneath it, the words “Population: 812” are faded enough you can see the older number underneath—839—if the sun hits right. I’ve lived there all seventeen years of my life. It’s the kind of place where people wave at every car, whether they recognize the driver or not, and where gossip beats the postal service by several days. For most of my childhood, that smallness felt safe, like a fence around the world. Now I realize it’s also something I’ve been quietly negotiating my entire life.

Our high school has 96 students total, counting the freshmen. On Fridays, football is automatic attendance; there’s no point pretending you have other plans. I never played, but I did run the scoreboard. The controls are sticky with layers of dried soda, and you have to slap the reset button twice to make it listen. I liked sitting above the bleachers, alone but part of everything—the whole town watching a field lit brighter than Main Street ever gets. Around me: the smell of popcorn oil, a toddler crying over a dropped snow cone, our math teacher yelling at the ref. It’s messy, loud, and completely ours.  

Still, there’s a different kind of quiet that lives here too. The kind that fills space when you’re the only person on your road applying to an out-of-state college. My friends talk about staying—working on ranches, at the grain cooperative, or maybe the county office if they’re lucky. They’re not wrong to want that; the land anchors you deep. But as much as I love the way the wheat fields shift like a slow tide or how the stars look when the power flickers out, I keep feeling something tugging outward, like I’m supposed to see what’s beyond the county line. That tug sometimes makes me feel disloyal, like I’m turning my back on the people who raised me.

Every spring, our town hosts a clean-up day. It’s organized chaos: whole families in work gloves piling trash bags into pickups, tractors hauling abandoned tires from the creek. When I was younger, I thought it was just about beautifying the park so the neighboring towns wouldn’t call us “Rust Junction.” But a few years ago, as I helped repaint the gazebo with Mrs. Finn—a widow who remembers everyone’s birthdays—I realized it’s really about something else. We don’t have many resources or shiny new buildings, but people here fix what they can, together. There’s a kind of dignity in that, a quiet belief that you contribute what’s in your hands, not what’s in your wallet.

That idea has shaped me more than I admit. When our senior class planned a fundraiser for new library computers, no one waited for some big donor. The mechanic donated an old engine for us to raffle, Grandma baked three pies, and I spent two weekends sorting aluminum cans to redeem for cash. We raised enough for exactly two computers and one rolling chair. In most places, that’s nothing. For us, it felt monumental—a reminder that community can mean refusing to give up, even when the result is small and scrappy.

I used to think belonging meant similarity, but maybe it means responsibility. My community taught me that you don’t measure care by abundance; you measure it by effort. When Mrs. Finn waves from her porch as I drive by, her hand lifted high and slow, I know she isn’t just being polite. She’s saying, “We see you.” And when I leave next fall, that wave will travel with me, a reminder that even in a town too small for stoplights, we built something lasting—the willingness to show up for each other, with whatever we have.

The first time I tried to explain where I’m from to someone outside of Perry County, they asked if it was near “any big cities.” I laughed and said, “If you drive forty minutes in any direction, you’ll hit cows before you hit people.” That’s mostly true. My community is three hundred people, a blinking stoplight, and acres of corn that stretch out like patchwork until they touch the sky. It sounds quiet, maybe even boring, but for me it’s a place humming with small, stubborn life.  

In the summer, the hum becomes literal—chainsaws buzzing as neighbors cut fallen limbs after a storm, screen doors slamming as kids run between yards, tractors whining low as they crawl home at dusk. Everyone notices everything. When old Mrs. Hanlon’s front porch light stays off one evening, Mr. Derry checks on her before breakfast. When our hens escape, they inevitably appear in someone else’s garden, and we get a call before we even know they’re gone. Community isn’t an optional club here; it’s the air we breathe.

I didn’t appreciate that closeness until I was thirteen and my dad’s truck broke down on Route 14 in mid-February. The thermometer said eleven degrees. He called me from the shoulder, frustrated, saying the tow company couldn’t come for two hours. But before he even hung up, a neighbor—Mr. Lyons, still in his seed cap and coveralls—had stopped. He handed Dad a thermos of coffee from his own truck and waited until the tow arrived. Later, when I asked him why he didn’t just drive by, he shrugged and said, “That’s just what you do.”  

That simple phrase has run through my head ever since: that’s just what you do. It explains why Mrs. Polk leaves baskets of zucchini on porches, why the high school band plays at every funeral, why people who argue fiercely about politics will still share a pie at the church potluck. We help because there’s no alternative if you want this place to keep going. Over time, I realized that “helping” meant more than polite gestures—it meant taking part, even when it’s messy or inconvenient.

My main contribution became the volunteer fire department’s junior program. My older brother joined first, and I followed, nervous about being one of two girls on the crew. The first night, I could smell diesel and smoke baked into the walls. The chief, Mr. Baird, handed me a pair of gloves still damp from the last call and said, “You ready to get dirty?” I nodded, but my hands trembled. That night we practiced rolling hoses and hauling them through the gravel lot until our arms burned. I loved it instantly—the teamwork, the adrenaline, the trust.

When the siren wails now, my body reacts before my brain does. I grab gear, climb into the truck, and within minutes we’re out on winding roads that I know like veins on the back of my hand. Sometimes we’re helping an old farmer pull his combine from a ditch; sometimes it’s scarier, like the night a neighbor’s barn caught fire. We fought it for hours, steam rising in white bursts against the orange glow. The barn couldn’t be saved, but the neighbors formed a bucket line anyway, passing water just to keep the house safe. That’s what community feels like: sweat, ash, exhaustion, and still refusing to stop.

Being part of that crew taught me to see strength in unshowy acts. Leadership here doesn’t mean speeches or titles; it means staying until the last hose is coiled, calling a widow the next day to see if she found her cat. As I think about leaving for college, I know my world will expand beyond cornfields and volunteer sirens. But I also know that the kind of person I want to be—steady, reliable, part of something larger—came from this place. Wherever I go, I’ll still carry the smell of smoke that has seeped into my coat and the habit of noticing when a porch light stays off too long.

Every morning before the bus came, I could hear the hiss of irrigation sprinklers through our open kitchen window. The scent of alfalfa drifted in, damp and green, mixing with the smell of my mom’s instant coffee. Our town—population 1,704—was the kind of place where you recognized every pickup by sound. My community wasn’t defined by lines on a map so much as by shared chores: stacking hay bales in July, unloading canned-goods donations at the firehouse, serving ham and scalloped potatoes at the Methodist fundraiser.  

For a long time, I didn’t think much about belonging. Belonging was automatic. You waved to whoever drove by. You stopped for ten minutes at the gas station because someone’s grandpa wanted to talk about the soybean market. On Friday nights, the bleachers at the high school football field filled with families, even if their kids had graduated twenty years ago. I played trumpet in the marching band, and sometimes during halftime I’d look at the crowd—a quilt of flannel shirts under the floodlights—and feel both comforted and fenced in.  

My cousin Liza once said our town was “safe but small-thinking.” At fifteen, that felt dramatic, but I started to understand. When I told people I wanted to study environmental science, neighbors teased, “Plan on saving the planet, city girl?” They didn’t mean harm; they genuinely couldn’t imagine why I’d want to leave a place that already felt perfect to them. Still, I noticed the way the creek behind our house ran lower each summer. I saw how farmers had to irrigate more, how the cottonwoods were thinning out. The world outside our town line seemed to be reaching in, quietly changing things.  

My turning point came junior year, when our biology teacher, Mr. Arnett, asked for volunteers to join the county conservation club. I signed up mostly for extra credit. Our first project was cleaning trash from the Wolf Creek banks. The group met at six on a freezing Saturday morning, the kind of chill that stings inside your nostrils. We dragged out old tires, rusted cans, and a car bumper half-buried in mud. At first it felt like punishment. But near noon the sun finally broke through the fog, and I watched silt swirl back into clear water where we’d scooped debris away. There was color again—stones mottled pink, minnows flashing between ripples.  

After that, I started organizing more cleanups. At first hardly anyone came besides a few classmates and my mom. But word slipped around after I posted photos on the community Facebook page. The volunteer list grew: retired farmers, middle-schoolers, even the sheriff. One Sunday, as we toted black trash bags along Main Street, Mrs. Peterson from the diner handed out lemonade cups. “You’re showing us how to care different,” she said. That phrase—care different—stuck with me.  

It turned out my place in the community wasn’t fixed after all. I could stretch its borders by inviting people to look at what surrounded them a little closer, the way I had. The farmer who used to call me “city girl” now asks about rain data and soil testing. It’s strange and thrilling to feel like a bridge, standing with one foot in familiar alfalfa fields and the other pointed toward possibilities my town had never considered.  

When I think about leaving for college, I imagine sound again—the hiss of sprinklers, the clunk of boots on the porch, the muffled thud of a basketball bouncing in our driveway. Those noises built my rhythm. I still belong to the people who taught me to wave at every truck, even as I hope to study the systems that connect faraway rivers and fields. My community grounded me in attention: how to notice what’s changing, how to care until caring becomes habit. If I can bring that habit wherever I go, I’ll always carry home with me, the way the scent of cut hay clings to the cuffs of your jeans long after you’ve left the field.

When I was twelve, I learned that a tractor tire weighs about 400 pounds. I know this because I helped my neighbor, Mr. Latham, roll one across his barn floor when his back went out during hay season. Our town, Green Hollow—population 1,204 if you count the occasional newborn calf—runs on small exchanges like that: borrowed tools, shared gossip, quiet favors offered before anyone has to ask. I used to think that was just normal life. Only as I got older did I realize it was also a kind of language—a way our community says “we’re here for you” without ever speaking the words.

My family’s hardware store sits on Main Street between the post office and a hair salon that also sells bait. When I was little, I’d sit on the paint-can pyramid behind the counter and watch my dad greet everyone by name. He’d know that Mr. Harris prefers the shorter nails for fence work or that Mrs. Jacobs’s porch swing needed a fresh chain. The bell over the door rang like punctuation, marking the rhythm of our days. It felt safe, predictable, maybe even dull. By high school, I wondered if I was missing something bigger out there, where life wasn’t measured by when the corn tassels turned gold.

My turning point came junior year, when a summer storm ripped the roof off the Methodist church. The day after, half the town showed up with hammers and casseroles. I was assigned to carry old shingles to the dumpster. The air smelled like wet plywood and coffee from the thermos someone passed down the pew line. In the middle of it all, I noticed how people worked in sync without being told—Mr. Latham’s slow, careful movements balancing the teenagers’ energy, Mrs. Stiles humming to keep rhythm as she handed out nails. It wasn’t just about fixing a roof; it was about stitching the town back together after the wind tried to break it apart.  

That day, I understood that community wasn’t something you simply belong to—it’s something you help make, board by board. I started looking for my own small ways to build it. I organized a weekend project repainting the faded “Welcome to Green Hollow” sign. The letters came out a little crooked, but drivers still honked and waved in approval. I helped the elementary school set up a reading corner, collecting donated books from dusty attics. Every time I stacked one, I thought about how many quiet acts keep our town upright, invisible as nails holding a barn wall.

Sometimes I still dream about places with subways or skyscrapers or bookstores that stay open past nine. But I’ve realized that growing up here gave me something just as valuable—an instinct for noticing how people connect. In Green Hollow, everyone’s life is visible, even the parts that hurt. When the dairy farmer’s wife got sick, you saw casseroles appear on their porch before she even came home from the hospital. When my friend Evan’s dad lost his job, the volunteer fire crew quietly paid their electric bill. That visibility can feel exposing, but also deeply human. You learn to look out for one another because you know how quickly a flat tire, a storm, or a bad harvest can tilt everything off balance.

As I think about leaving for college, I know I’m stepping into a different kind of community—one built not on shared geography but on shared curiosity. Still, I’ll carry Green Hollow’s lessons with me: that real belonging isn’t about owning the right zip code or speaking the same slang, but about noticing what needs to be done and doing it without being asked. Whether it’s a campus club, a lab group, or a neighborhood in some faraway city, I want to learn their unspoken language too, and to keep listening for the quiet ways people say, “We’re here for you.”

In my town, the only stoplight blinks yellow after 10 p.m., and everybody still calls the grocery store “the IGA” even though the sign changed years ago. I grew up on a county road outside of town, where mailboxes lean from being clipped by truck mirrors and the radio fades in and out between stations. The place I belong most deeply is not just the town itself, but the volunteer fire department auxiliary, a group I joined because in a small rural community, helping is less of an activity than a language.

My first memory of the fire hall is the smell. It was a mix of coffee that had sat too long on the burner, wet concrete, diesel, and the sugary glaze from the donuts people brought on Saturday mornings. My dad is a volunteer firefighter, and when I was little, I used to sit at the folding tables coloring on the backs of old fundraiser flyers while the adults planned fish fries and pancake breakfasts. I liked the sound of their voices carrying over each other, the scrape of metal chair legs, the way people who disagreed about almost everything still worked side by side when someone needed a new roof or a ride to chemotherapy.

When I turned fourteen, I was finally old enough to help officially with the auxiliary. At first my jobs were small and unglamorous: stacking styrofoam containers, stirring industrial-size cans of green beans, wiping tables after fundraisers, and taping raffle baskets in cellophane that never folded the way I wanted. I remember standing in the hall kitchen at six in the morning, hair smelling like fryer oil before the sun was fully up, listening to the firefighters joke while they hauled out griddles. I had always thought service would feel dramatic, like sirens and urgency. Instead, I learned that most of it looks like refilling napkin holders.

That was the lesson that changed me. In our town, people do not usually ask for help directly. Pride runs deep here, sometimes deeper than common sense. So support has to be observant. You notice that the Wilsons’ porch light has been off for three nights, and someone drops off a bag of groceries. You hear that a farmer broke his wrist during hay season, and by afternoon three neighbors are in his field. At the fire hall, I learned to pay attention to the quiet signals: who lingered after an event because they did not want to go home to an empty house, who slipped an extra twenty into the donation jar but never came through the line for dinner, who always said “we’re fine” a little too quickly.

Last winter, after a house fire on the edge of town displaced a family with two little boys, the auxiliary organized a community supper. I helped call local businesses for donations, and I was terrified at first because everyone in town knew my voice as “Rick’s daughter,” not as someone asking for support on her own. But one hardware store owner said yes before I finished my sentence. The librarian donated desserts. The gas station put out a jar by the register. That night, I watched people in muddy boots and church coats fill the hall until there were no seats left. The boys ran between tables with frosting on their faces while their mother cried quietly near the coffee urn. We raised enough money to cover a security deposit and winter clothes. I went home smelling like dish soap and smoke, feeling for the first time that I had contributed something larger than my last name.

Belonging to this group has shown me that community is built through repetition: setting up tables, remembering names, showing up again. In a place as small as mine, it is easy to assume the world is limited to what you can see from the edge of a soybean field. The auxiliary taught me the opposite. It taught me that even in a town with one blinking light, people can create a network wide enough to catch each other. And it taught me that I want to spend my life becoming the kind of person who notices, stays, and helps before being asked.

On Friday nights in my town, the football field lights are bright enough to turn the soybean leaves silver. Everybody goes, even if they are not really there for football. Babies in ear protection sleep against their mothers’ shoulders, old farmers in seed-corn caps lean against the fence, and the band plays a little too fast because the cold makes our fingers stiff. In a town with one blinking stoplight and a Dollar General where people catch up in the toothpaste aisle, community is less something you join than something you inherit.

For a long time, I thought belonging to this town meant being exactly like it. My classmates had last names painted on mailboxes along gravel roads, and their family stories were stitched into church directories, 4-H ribbons, and cemetery stones. My family was part of that fabric too, but in a quieter way. We lived just outside town in a white house with a sagging porch, and my parents worked long hours, so I spent a lot of time with my grandpa. He knew everyone, or at least enough to wave first. Riding with him in his dented Ford pickup, I learned that community could be mapped by gestures: two fingers lifted off the steering wheel, a pie dropped off after a funeral, a tractor pulled from a ditch before the sheriff even got called.

The group that taught me this most clearly was our volunteer fire department, even though I was too young to wear the gear. My grandpa has been on it for thirty-two years. As a kid, I would sit at the metal folding table during pancake breakfasts, stuffing plastic forks into napkins while the firefighters moved around me in navy shirts darkened with sweat from the griddle. The station smelled like syrup, coffee, and a trace of diesel that never really left. People came to eat, of course, but they also came because this was where need and pride met in public. Farmers who hated asking for help bought extra raffle tickets for families whose barns had burned. Teenagers who rolled their eyes at everything still stacked chairs at the end of the morning.

When I got older, I started helping for real. I organized canned goods after our holiday food drive, logged donated tools after a storm tore through the next county, and ran plates of scrambled eggs to tables crowded with people who all seemed to know my name. At first I liked the comfort of that recognition. Then I began to notice its weight. In a small town, being known means being responsible. If Ms. Porter from the post office told me her husband’s back was acting up, I would probably see their driveway unshoveled before school the next week. If someone missed two church potlucks in a row, people noticed.

That closeness can feel suffocating. News travels faster than internet out here. There were years when I wanted to disappear into a place where nobody asked whose daughter I was or whether I was “going to stay local.” But the older I got, the more I understood that our town’s constant attention was, at its best, a form of care. Not perfect care, not always gentle, but real. When my dad was injured and couldn’t work for several weeks, casseroles appeared on our porch in mismatched dishes. One night, after freezing rain turned our road into glass, someone I never identified plowed our driveway before dawn. No note, no text, just two clean tracks through the ice.

That is the community I belong to: not a flawless rural postcard, but a network of people who have practiced showing up for one another in ordinary, stubborn ways. Being part of it has made me more observant and less casual about other people’s struggles. It has taught me that service is not dramatic most of the time; it looks like remembering, noticing, carrying, returning. In college, I want to bring that habit of attention with me. I may leave the town where everyone knows my truck before they know my face, but I will keep the instinct it gave me: when something needs doing, pull over, get out, and help.

On Friday nights in my town, there are three places to be: the football field, the Dollar General, or the volunteer fire station. For most of my childhood, I rotated between all three, but the fire station is where I learned what it means to belong to a community that survives by noticing one another.

I grew up in a farming town with one blinking red light, two churches, and enough gravel roads that everyone’s truck carried a permanent coat of dust. My dad joined the volunteer fire department when I was in elementary school, and because childcare in a town of 1,200 people often looks like “bring her with you,” I spent a lot of evenings at the station doing homework at the plastic folding table while radios crackled above me. The building smelled like diesel, coffee that had been reheated too many times, and the rubbery inside of fire boots drying by the wall heater. I knew the names painted on the backs of the jackets before I knew all my multiplication facts.

At first, the department seemed to me like a collection of unrelated adults: a mechanic, a soybean farmer, the school secretary, the owner of the feed store. Then I started paying attention to what happened when the siren sounded. Mr. Alvarez would leave the line at his auto shop with grease still on his forearms. Mrs. Carter, who checked me out library books on Tuesdays, would pull on turnout gear over her flowered scrub top. People arrived half-finished from their regular lives and became a team in under two minutes.

Because I was the kid always in the corner, people forgot to filter themselves around me, and I got to see the quieter side of service. I saw firefighters mop mud off the station floor after pulling a pickup out of a flooded ditch. I watched them deliver casseroles to a family whose barn had burned before dawn. No one called a meeting to assign those tasks. In our town, helping was less a grand gesture than a reflex, like reaching out a hand when someone slips on ice.

When I was fourteen, that reflex turned toward my own family. My grandfather collapsed in his hay field during July baling season, and the ambulance took twenty-two minutes to reach him from the next town over. Before it arrived, two volunteers were already there because one had heard my grandmother’s call on the scanner and the other had seen my uncle wave from the road. I remember standing by the gate, sweat sliding down my back, the air full of cut alfalfa and engine heat, watching people I knew from pancake breakfasts and parade duty kneel in the dirt beside my grandfather. He survived, and for weeks afterward, neighbors kept showing up with things we had not asked for: a repaired fence, a bag of groceries, a cooler of sweet tea, three teenagers who stacked hay until sunset without being told twice.

That summer changed my place in the department’s orbit. I enrolled in a community CPR class at the station, then started helping with fundraisers, washing trucks, and organizing the shelves of donated supplies. Eventually I became the teenager who ran the children’s table at the annual pancake breakfast, pouring too much syrup and showing little kids how to sit in the cab without touching anything important. I also started noticing who was missing from our version of community. New families who had moved in for work at the poultry plant rarely came to town events. Some didn’t know they were invited; some didn’t speak much English; some had learned not to assume they belonged in small places like ours.

For a Spanish class project, I made bilingual flyers for the pancake breakfast and safety fair, then asked my teacher to help me translate basic fire-prevention information. It was a small thing—printer paper, imperfect grammar, tape on the post office window—but that year I watched two mothers I had never met bring their kids to the station. One little boy in a dinosaur T-shirt clung to my leg until I let him hold the plastic fire hat. His mom told me, in careful English, that she had driven by the building for months without knowing if it was “for everyone.” Her question stayed with me because it exposed something I had taken for granted: community is not just the people who would come if called; it is also the people who need to know they are allowed to answer.

I belong to the volunteer fire station not because I wear gear or ride on calls, but because it taught me that a strong community is built in the ordinary moments before emergencies happen: folding chairs set out at a fundraiser, information translated well enough to be useful, names remembered, doors left open. In a small rural town, people like to say everybody knows everybody. What I have learned is that real belonging takes more than being known. It takes being welcomed, expected, and counted on.

At 5:15 every morning, the parking lot behind Miller’s Feed and Seed smells like diesel, wet hay, and coffee strong enough to wake the tractors before the farmers. That is where my town gathers before sunrise. Men in seed caps lean against truck beds, women in scrub tops stop in after night shift, and somebody is always loading fifty-pound bags while talking about rain like it is a relative with a temper. I grew up there, not because my family owned the store, but because my mom worked the register and I spent half my childhood on a cracked stool behind the counter, doing homework between the birdseed and the bolts.

In a town of 1,842 people, a store like Miller’s is more than a business. It is our bulletin board, complaint department, weather station, and unofficial city hall. If a calf got out, somebody mentioned it at Miller’s. If the high school baseball team made sectionals, the whole front window filled with hand-painted signs. If the river rose too high, people came in not just for sandbags but for updates. I learned early that community in a small town is built less by speeches than by repetition: showing up, asking after someone’s mother, remembering who needs their feed carried out because their shoulder never healed right after harvest three years ago.

When I was younger, I thought belonging meant being known. In my town, being known comes easy. People recognized me as “Deb’s girl” long before they knew my actual name. At basketball games, in line at the post office, at church potlucks, I was attached to my family like a label on a mason jar. For a while, that felt comforting. Then, around middle school, it felt small. I wanted to be seen as more than a daughter, more than a cashier’s kid, more than someone whose life could be summarized by who her grandpa was and what road she lived on.

So I started volunteering at the store for real. Not just sitting on the stool, but stocking shelves, sweeping grain dust, learning invoices, and loading feed. At first, I did it to prove I was useful. Then I kept doing it because work at Miller’s gave me a different way into my town. People talked more honestly when I was carrying bags beside them than when I was standing silently behind my mother. Mr. Alvarez, who farms soybeans south of town, told me how a late frost could erase months of planning in one night. Mrs. Jenkins, who always bought sunflower seeds and peppermint candy together, admitted she mostly came in to have somebody to talk to since her husband died. I began to understand that our community was held together not by sameness but by dependence, quiet and constant.

The summer after freshman year, a storm tore through our county and knocked out power for three days. Freezers thawed, roads flooded, and cell service flickered in and out like it was undecided. Miller’s stayed open with a generator. People came in muddy boots and damp jeans, buying batteries, ice, tarps, and whatever canned food was left. My job was simple: carry things, restock, keep the coffee filled, and write down purchases when the card reader failed. But in those three days, I saw the store become exactly what my town needed. The volunteer firefighters grabbed supplies and left cash on the counter because there was no time to wait for change. A dairy farmer charged phone batteries for strangers in his truck. My mom made peanut butter sandwiches in the break room and sent me out with paper plates. Nobody asked whose problem it was. They just stepped toward it.

That storm changed the way I understood my place here. I had spent years worrying that my town was too small, too slow, too predictable. Sometimes it is. News travels faster than internet, and everybody has an opinion on your business. But smallness also means that when something breaks, there is no illusion that someone else will fix it. The “community” I belong to is not an abstract hometown pride. It is a network of people who keep showing up for one another in work boots, scrubs, seed caps, and mud-streaked pickup trucks.

I used to think leaving for college would mean leaving that community behind. Now I think I am taking its habits with me: notice who is missing, carry what you can, refill the coffee, learn people’s names, and understand that belonging is not just being recognized. It is being relied on.

The first time I realized our town was small enough to be invisible was when I typed "Kendrick, Idaho" into Google Maps and had to zoom in four times before the dot appeared. Population 303, though Mrs. Halverson at the post office insists it's 305 because she counts her two cats. She's not entirely wrong. In Kendrick, everything counts, including the cats.

I grew up in a place where the volunteer fire department is also the parade committee, the search-and-rescue team, and the group that organizes the Locust Blossom Festival every June. My dad has been a volunteer firefighter since before I was born, which means I spent Saturday mornings at the station eating powdered donuts while grown men argued about the optimal pressure for a two-inch hose line. I learned to make coffee in a 40-cup percolator when I was nine. I learned that community isn't something you join so much as something that absorbs you, like water into clay soil.

The thing about a town this small is that reciprocity isn't a concept—it's the air you breathe. When our roof started leaking three winters ago, Dale Thiessen showed up with a nail gun and his son-in-law before my mom had even finished describing the problem on the phone. She hadn't asked for help. She'd called to ask if Dale knew anyone affordable. He laughed at the word "affordable" and said he'd be over by noon. In exchange, my mom made eight quarts of her blackberry jam that summer and left them on Dale's porch without a note. No note was necessary.

I have a complicated relationship with this. I love that I can walk into the Grange Hall on any Thursday and find someone who watched me take my first steps, someone who will hand me a plate of scalloped potatoes and ask about my calculus grade in the same breath. But I've also felt the weight of a place where everyone knows your story before you've decided how to tell it. When my parents separated the summer before sophomore year, the casseroles arrived before the paperwork was even filed. People meant well. They always mean well. But I remember wanting desperately to be anonymous, to exist somewhere no one would touch my shoulder in the cereal aisle at Harvest Foods and say, with unbearable tenderness, "How are you holding up, sweetheart?"

I've thought a lot about why I stayed involved anyway—why I kept showing up to help set up tables at community dinners, kept volunteering for the Locust Blossom cleanup crew, kept making that terrible percolator coffee. I think it's because Kendrick taught me that belonging isn't comfort. It's friction and awkwardness and someone knowing your pain before you've processed it, and it's also Dale Thiessen on your roof in December, and it's also blackberry jam on a porch, and it's also Mrs. Halverson counting her cats because in a town this small, every living thing that stays is making a choice to stay.

I am making a choice to leave, and that feels like the hardest thing Kendrick has asked of me. Not because anyone is stopping me—everyone here has told me to go, to see what's out past the wheat fields. But leaving a place that shaped you this deeply feels like pulling a thread from fabric. You worry the whole thing will unravel. Then you remember that fabric is stronger than any single thread, and so is a town that has buried its dead, rebuilt its barns, and counted its cats for over a hundred years.

I will carry the percolator coffee taste with me. I will carry all of it.

The first time I understood what our town was, I was nine years old, standing in the back of Mr. Kazinski's feed store while my dad haggled over the price of alfalfa cubes. Mr. Kazinski had a jar of butterscotch candies on the counter that I'd been stealing from since I could reach it, and he'd been watching me steal from it for just as long. That afternoon, while my dad loaded the truck, Mr. Kazinski leaned over and said, "You know I count those every night, right?" He winked. I never could tell if he was joking.

Our town, Hardin, sits in south-central Montana with a population that hovers around 3,700 depending on who's been born and who's decided to leave. The Bighorn River cuts through on its way somewhere more interesting. We have a Super 1 Foods, a Subway, two stoplights, and a high school where I've had the same thirty-two classmates since kindergarten. I know which of them are allergic to peanuts, which ones cry during standardized testing, and which ones have parents going through divorces they think nobody knows about. They know the same things about me.

This closeness is not always comfortable. When my mom's depression got bad my sophomore year and she stopped coming to basketball games, I didn't have to explain anything. Mrs. Trujillo, who runs the concession stand, just started sending home foil-wrapped plates of nachos with me after every game, extra jalapeños, the way my mom likes them. Nobody said a word about her absence, which was both a kindness and its own strange pressure, the weight of people knowing your family's shape even when it buckles.

I am Crow on my mother's side, and Hardin sits at the edge of the Crow Reservation. This means our community holds two overlapping worlds that don't always overlap gracefully. At school, I've watched teachers gloss over the Battle of the Little Bighorn like it's a footnote instead of something that happened twenty minutes from our parking lot. I've sat through assemblies where the only acknowledgment of Native students was a dreamcatcher clip art on the diversity poster. But I've also watched my grandmother sit on the bleachers next to ranchers' wives, all of them screaming at a referee with equal fury, temporarily fused by their shared opinion that the call was garbage.

What I've learned from Hardin is that community isn't a feeling. It's a practice, sometimes a clumsy one. It's Mr. Kazinski letting a kid take his butterscotch. It's Mrs. Trujillo's nachos appearing without being asked for. It's also the arguments at town council meetings about water rights that go on so long the stenographer falls asleep, and it's the silence in classrooms where certain histories get smoothed over because the truth is inconvenient.

I joined our school's oral history project junior year, recording interviews with elders from both the reservation and the town. Sitting across from a ninety-one-year-old Crow woman named Agnes White Hip while she described the smell of her grandmother's frybread, and then the next week sitting with a white rancher named Dale who described the exact same county fair from 1968 but remembered completely different details, I started to see that a community is not one story. It is a room full of people telling different stories about the same place and choosing, daily, whether to listen.

I don't romanticize Hardin. The winters are brutal, opportunities are scarce, and the lots where businesses used to stand are now just gravel and memory. But it taught me that showing up matters more than agreeing. That a jar of butterscotch on a counter can be a whole philosophy of generosity. That the most honest thing a community can do is hold contradiction without flinching.

I want to carry that practice forward, into rooms much bigger than Mr. Kazinski's feed store.

The first time I realized our town was small enough to feel like a single organism was the morning Mr. Hadley's barn caught fire. I was eleven, eating cereal in my pajamas, and within twenty minutes I watched through our kitchen window as fourteen pickup trucks materialized on the county road like white blood cells rushing toward a wound. Nobody called a meeting. Nobody sent a group text. The smoke was the signal, and the response was automatic.

I grew up in Elmore, Kansas, population 587 on the sign, though Mrs. Dietrich at the post office will tell you it's closer to 540 since the Pfeifer kids left for Wichita. It's the kind of place where the gas station, the grain elevator, and the Lutheran church form a triangle that contains approximately ninety percent of public life. The fourth point, if you're generous with geometry, is the baseball diamond behind the elementary school, where I've played since I was old enough to swing a bat without falling over.

That diamond is where I learned what belonging actually costs. Not in some inspirational-poster way, but in the literal sense that when our backstop rusted through the summer before my freshman year, twelve families pooled money from tight budgets to replace it. My dad contributed two hundred dollars we probably needed for truck repairs. I remember my mom pressing her lips together when he wrote the check, not angry exactly, but performing the private math of sacrifice. That's community: the quiet negotiations between what you need and what the place you love needs from you.

I've been the scorekeeper, the pitcher, the kid who drags the chalk liner across the base paths on Saturday mornings. I've swept the dugout after games, picking up sunflower seed shells that stick to the concrete like tiny barnacles. Some summers I've practically lived in that red dust, feeling it settle into the creases of my elbows and the spaces between my teeth. The diamond doesn't belong to a league or an organization. It belongs to Elmore, which means it belongs to everyone and specifically to whoever shows up.

Showing up is the whole currency here. When I got mononucleosis sophomore year and missed three weeks of school, Mrs. Trujillo left enchiladas on our porch on four separate occasions. Mr. Koenig mowed our lawn without asking. These weren't extraordinary gestures in Elmore. They were the ordinary rhythm of a place where invisibility is impossible and neglect is a kind of sin. You cannot disappear in a town of 540 people, which is both the comfort and the claustrophobia of it.

Because I'd be lying if I said the closeness never suffocated me. Everyone knew when my older sister dropped out of community college. Everyone had an opinion about it, delivered gently at the grocery store with a hand on my mom's arm and a voice lowered to that particular frequency of concern that's really just curiosity wearing a church dress. I've wanted to leave Elmore with an ache that sometimes keeps me up at night, staring at my ceiling and imagining cities where nobody knows my name.

But here's what I keep returning to: the morning of the barn fire, my dad didn't deliberate. He put on his boots and drove toward the smoke. There was no calculation about whether Mr. Hadley deserved help or whether the effort was convenient. The response was cellular, deep in the tissue of how people here are built. I've absorbed that. It's in me now, this impulse to move toward the trouble rather than away from it, to show up even when showing up is expensive.

I want to leave Elmore. I also want to carry its best instinct with me — that automatic drive toward the smoke, that willingness to write the check even when the math doesn't quite work out.

The first time I realized Harlan, Kentucky was small was when my seventh-grade pen pal from Chicago asked how many Starbucks we had. I wrote back zero and she didn't respond for two weeks, like I'd told her we lacked running water.

We have 1,347 people, one stoplight that stays blinking yellow after nine p.m., and a Family Dollar that functions as our social hub. On any given Saturday, you'll find my papaw leaning against the ice freezer out front, talking to whoever walks by about the weather, the Wildcats, or whose coonhound got loose again. This is my community — not a club I joined or an organization with bylaws, but the actual tangled web of people who watched me grow up, who knew my mama before she was my mama, who will tell you exactly what I did wrong at the church potluck in 2016 when I dropped an entire Crock-Pot of chicken and dumplings on the fellowship hall carpet.

In a place this size, you belong to everyone. Mrs. Combs at the post office knows my SAT score because my grandmother told her, and she taped a handwritten "GOOD JOB ELLIE" note to our P.O. box. When my dad had his back surgery, seven different casseroles showed up on our porch in three days, each one covered in aluminum foil with reheating instructions written in Sharpie. The Bevins family brought a blackberry cobbler even though they and my dad haven't spoken properly since a property line dispute in 2009. That's how it works here. The cobbler comes regardless.

But belonging to everyone also means everyone has opinions about you. When I started talking about applying to colleges out of state, I felt the shift. At the gas station, Doug Turner asked my dad if I thought I was "too good for here now." My aunt posted a Facebook article about how young people are "abandoning Appalachia" and tagged me in it with a single emoji — the eyes. Even people who loved me seemed to treat my ambition like a small betrayal.

I spent most of junior year feeling guilty about wanting to leave, which is a strange thing to feel guilty about. I'd sit in my room filling out application fee waivers and feel like I was drafting a secret letter of resignation from the only place that had ever claimed me. Some nights I'd drive out to the overlook on Pine Mountain where you can see the whole town laid out below, the lights so sparse you can count them, and I'd try to figure out whether wanting more meant I loved this place less.

What I eventually understood is that Harlan made me someone who wants more precisely because of what it gave me. My papaw, who never finished high school, taught me to read a topographic map and identify twenty species of trees by their bark alone. My neighbor Miss Linda, a retired teacher, gave me her personal collection of novels when the school library couldn't afford new ones — boxes of Toni Morrison and Barbara Kingsolver with her pencil notes still in the margins. The community that worries I'll leave is the same community that made me curious enough to want to explore.

I am not abandoning Appalachia. I carry it in specific, physical ways — in my accent that flattens certain vowels beyond repair, in my instinct to bring food to anyone going through hardship, in my habit of waving at every single passing car because on our roads, you always know the driver. I carry it in my understanding that a place can love you in complicated, imperfect ways and that you can love it back just as imperfectly.

Last Thanksgiving, Doug Turner stopped me outside the Family Dollar. I expected another comment. Instead, he handed me a twenty-dollar bill and said, "For your books up there at school." I tried to refuse it. He closed my hand around it and said, "You come back and tell us about it."

I plan to.

The summer I turned fourteen, a drought killed most of Mr. Hensley's soybean crop, and within a week, our whole town knew about it. Not because anyone announced it. Because someone left a casserole on his porch, and then someone else left another one, and by Thursday there were eleven foil-covered dishes stacked outside his door and a handwritten note that said, simply, "We'll figure it out together."

Calhoun, Missouri has 427 people, one stoplight, and a grain elevator that you can see from anywhere in town. I grew up understanding that those 427 people were not background characters in my life. They were my life.

My grandmother, Nana Bette, has lived on the same half-acre since 1967. She knows the name of every dog within a two-mile radius. She knows which families are struggling and which kids need a meal after school, and she operates on the quiet assumption that knowing creates responsibility. I absorbed this from her the way you absorb a smell — without deciding to, without even noticing, until one day you realize it's part of you.

In Calhoun, I learned things that don't appear on any curriculum. I learned that Mr. Pitchford at the hardware store would walk you through rewiring a light switch for forty-five minutes even on a Saturday afternoon. I learned that the high school basketball games mattered not because of the score but because they were the one weekly moment when every generation showed up in the same building, cheering for the same thing. I learned that grief here is communal — when Mrs. Torres lost her husband, the whole town lost something, and we all felt the specific weight of that empty pew at First Baptist.

But Calhoun also taught me its limitations, and this is where my relationship with home gets complicated. When I started writing poetry in eighth grade and mentioned it in conversation, a few people didn't quite know what to do with that information. When I said I wanted to study environmental science, someone told me that sounded like something they talked about in cities. There were moments I felt the town's borders pressing against me like the edges of a frame.

What I've wrestled with — really wrestled with, lying awake listening to coyotes at two in the morning — is how to carry this place forward without being flattened by it. Because Calhoun gave me something I don't take lightly: the understanding that community is not decorative. It is structural. It holds people up when they cannot hold themselves.

I watched it hold Mr. Hensley. I watched it hold my neighbor Darlene during her second round of chemotherapy, when someone from town drove her to Jefferson City for treatment every single Tuesday for eight months, different person each week, no one keeping score. That's not small-town sentimentality. That's a functional philosophy about what humans owe each other.

I want to bring that philosophy somewhere it can grow larger. I want to study how communities respond to environmental stress — drought, yes, like Mr. Hensley's fields, but also flooding and heat and the slow erasure of rural economies. I want to understand what makes some towns survive and others dissolve. And I think growing up in one gives me a kind of knowledge that no textbook manufactures.

When I leave Calhoun — which I will, which I need to — I won't be escaping it. I'll be extending it. Every casserole left on a porch represents a decision that other people's pain belongs to you too. I want to make that decision at scale.

Nana Bette told me once that a place teaches you what matters, and then it's your job to go matter somewhere. I'm not entirely sure she meant it that way, but I've been holding onto that sentence for three years now, turning it over, and I think it's the truest thing I know about myself.

The summer I turned fifteen, a drought cracked our fields into a puzzle of dead soil, and my father's hands shook when he checked the rain gauge each morning. By August, he'd stopped checking. What I remember most isn't the financial terror that sat at the dinner table with us every night — it's that by the second week of August, there were casseroles on our porch. Labeled dishes from the Hendersons, the Kowalczyks, the Meachams. Nobody called first. They just came.

I grew up in Harlan, Wyoming, population 847, which means I grew up inside a particular kind of knowing. Everybody knew whose truck that was in the ditch on Route 12. Everybody knew when Dale Pittman's wife got sick. Everybody knew my grandfather's name before they knew mine.
