For most of my childhood, this felt suffocating — like living inside a snow globe that someone kept picking up and shaking just to watch the same flakes fall.

I wanted out. I was twelve when I started ordering library books from the county catalog just to read about cities where nobody would recognize me at the gas station. I rehearsed invisibility the way other kids rehearsed sports.

But the drought changed something in how I read the community I'd been trying to escape.

I watched my father — a man who once drove forty-five minutes to help a neighbor he barely liked pull a calf in the middle of winter — become someone who could barely accept help himself. He'd stand on the porch looking at those casserole dishes like they were an accusation. One evening I asked him why he wouldn't just call the Hendersons and say thank you properly, maybe invite them over.

"Because then I'd owe them something," he said.

"You already do," I told him. "That's kind of the whole thing."

He looked at me for a moment like I'd accidentally said something true.

What I've spent the last three years understanding is that Harlan operates on a ledger nobody audits. The debts are real but they're also impossible to repay, and somehow that's the point. When I started keeping score of all the ways this town held people up — the informal babysitting network, the fuel someone left anonymously in our tank the October after the drought, the way the hardware store extended credit without paperwork — I realized I had been completely misreading what I lived inside.

I'd been calling it suffocation. But it was actually structure.

I'm still complicated about Harlan. I don't think small and rural automatically means wise or good. I've watched this community fail people too — quietly exile anyone who doesn't fit a certain mold, avoid conversations it should be having. My best friend Dani moved away junior year partly because the silence around her felt louder than anything anyone said. I think about that. I think about who the casserole system doesn't reach.

But what I carry with me going into college is a hard-won and specific education in interdependence. I know what it looks like when a community decides, wordlessly, that a struggling family will not go hungry. I know that my father's pride and his community's loyalty were tangled in a way that neither he nor the Hendersons needed to explain aloud. I know that belonging sometimes means being known before you're ready.

I want to study sociology because I want to understand the architecture of these invisible systems — not to romanticize them, but to map what works and what fails and who falls through. I want to bring Dani into the equation. I want to ask whose porch gets the casseroles and whose doesn't, and why.

The drought ended in September. My father eventually called Henderson. I watched him through the kitchen window, standing in the yard, talking and then laughing about something, his back finally straight again.

Some debts make you stand taller. I'm still learning which ones.

The grain elevator stands at the edge of Hartwell like a declaration. Every morning for the first sixteen years of my life, I watched it catch the sunrise through my bedroom window — that enormous concrete cylinder turning pink and gold while the rest of the town was still gray. I didn't understand then what it meant to belong to a place the way I belonged to Hartwell, Nebraska, population 847.

Hartwell is the kind of town where people know what your father drives and what your grandmother's maiden name was and whether your family's fields got hail damage last August. That used to embarrass me. In sixth grade, I tried wearing a different backpack to school because I'd heard someone say my old one looked babyish, and by second period, Mrs. Kowalski had already called my mom. The town watched you, and the town talked, and for years I felt the watching like a hand on my shoulder I couldn't shake.

But then the Delaney barn burned down.

It was a Tuesday in October. By Wednesday morning, seventeen families had shown up with food, lumber, and work gloves. Nobody organized it. Nobody sent emails. People just knew, the way you know a storm is coming from how the air smells, and they came. My dad drove me out there that Saturday, and I watched a man named Carl Birch, who had argued with Dave Delaney about a property line for twenty years, swing a hammer beside him in the cold without saying a word about it. They worked until it was too dark to see.

I started paying different attention after that.

I noticed how the Petersons left zucchini on porches because their garden always grew too much, and how everyone pretended not to know who'd left it. I noticed how games against rival towns filled the bleachers with people who hadn't been to school in forty years. I noticed how Hartwell's silence — that wide, unpopulated quiet that used to feel like absence — was actually a kind of fullness, a collective breath the town was always taking.

I also noticed what the town couldn't do.

When my friend Tomás needed a Spanish-language tutor, there wasn't one. When I wanted to take AP Chemistry, we didn't have a teacher who'd ever taught it. When Kayla Reyes came out her junior year, some of that watching turned cold and unforgiving. The same closeness that built the Delaney barn could also build walls. I held both things without knowing how to resolve them.

That unresolved feeling is what pushed me toward environmental science and community planning. Hartwell's aquifer has been dropping for fifteen years. The young people leave and the old people stay and the schools get smaller and the businesses close, and the people who remain love the place with a ferocity that has nowhere useful to go. I want to understand how to help communities like mine become more sustainable — not just ecologically but socially, in ways that can hold their Tomáses and their Kaylas and their contradictions.

I am not nostalgic for Hartwell in the way people are nostalgic for places they've left behind cleanly. I carry it unfinished. I carry the grain elevator and Carl Birch's hammer and the property line dispute and Kayla's face in the hallway, all of it inseparable. A community isn't the version of itself it performs at its best moments. It's everything together — the generosity and the failure, the watching and the belonging, the barns that burn and the ones that get rebuilt.

When I leave for college, I will be the youngest person in my family to do so. The town will know this. Someone will mention it to my grandmother at the post office. And I think now, instead of shrinking from that, I want to carry it forward the way the elevator carries light — visibly, and not for myself alone.

The August before my junior year, the grain elevator burned down.

I know that sounds like the setup to a country song, but it wasn't poetic. It was 2 a.m. and the whole sky over Stratford, Nebraska turned the color of a peach, and every single person in town—all 847 of us—stood in the elementary school parking lot watching smoke eat the silhouette of the tallest structure we'd ever known. Mrs. Bauer was in her bathrobe. Coach Dreiling had shaving cream on one side of his face. My grandfather, who hadn't left his house after dark in three years, stood next to me in unlaced boots, watching something he'd driven past every morning for sixty years disappear.

Nobody said much. That's the thing about Stratford people—silence isn't empty here. It carries weight.

I grew up believing small towns were simple. Four churches, one stoplight, a Casey's General Store where Mr. Hensley knows your order before you reach the counter. I thought I understood this place completely. Then the elevator burned, and I realized I'd been confusing familiarity with knowledge.

The next morning, farmers started calling each other. Not about compensation or logistics—just checking in. Dale Kosmicki drove forty minutes to bring my grandfather coffee and stayed four hours. The volunteer fire department, twelve men who had been awake all night, showed up to help the Pflaster family move their stored grain by hand before it spoiled. Nobody organized this. Nobody sent a group text. It just happened, because here, a neighbor's disaster is never only a neighbor's disaster.

I've wrestled with this community my whole life. I've resented the gossip that travels faster than actual news, the way everyone knows when you fail a test or when your parents argue. I've felt the claustrophobia of being known so completely—how there's no clean slate, no reinvention. When I joined the academic team in eighth grade, half the town acted confused, like I'd declared a major in something impractical. "What's that gonna do for you?" Familiarity here can calcify into expectation.

But the fire cracked something open in me.

Standing in that parking lot, I started actually looking at the people around me instead of past them. I saw Mr. Elman, who lost his wife last winter, gripping my grandfather's elbow in the dark. I saw Destiny Marchetti, who I'd dismissed as someone I had nothing in common with, organizing a meal schedule on her phone at 3 a.m. I saw my own father cry, which I'd never seen before, because the elevator was where his father taught him to test moisture levels in wheat, where he'd worked every summer at sixteen.

Stratford taught me that interdependence isn't weakness—it's just honest. Most people need other people, and pretending otherwise is a luxury that rural poverty never really afforded us. When your tractor breaks during harvest, you ask for help. When you have extra, you give it away before it goes bad. I used to find this embarrassing, this constant visible need and generosity. I wanted the self-sufficiency I imagined in cities, in universities, in anywhere else.

I don't want that anymore. Or rather, I want to carry what I've learned here into those larger places.

I want to study environmental policy specifically because I've watched my community make impossible calculations—stay and work land that's getting harder to farm, or leave the only place that actually holds you. I want to understand systems well enough to help people like Dale and my grandfather have more options. I want to bring to a university campus the particular skill Stratford trained into me without my knowing: the ability to recognize that the person standing next to you in crisis is more complicated than your assumptions, and more worth knowing.

The elevator is gone now. They poured a foundation for a smaller building last spring. My grandfather drove past it every morning of his recovery.

He said it looked like a beginning.

Growing up in a small, tight-knit neighborhood has profoundly influenced my understanding of community and my role within it. Our neighborhood, composed of families from varied backgrounds, celebrates a diversity that has shaped me into a person who values inclusivity and mutual respect. The people and experiences shared in this community have taught me the importance of connecting with others and the power of collective effort toward a common goal.

In our neighborhood, annual events like summer block parties and holiday gatherings serve as pivotal moments that bring us together. These occasions highlight our shared commitment to fostering a supportive environment. I look forward to the block party each year, not just for the fun and food, but because it offers a time to collaborate toward organizing something bigger than any single individual could achieve alone. From selecting music to arranging activities for the children, each task is an opportunity for us to bond and appreciate each person's contributions.

My role in this community has evolved significantly as I have grown older. Early on, I was a participant in these gatherings, learning from the camaraderie and the spirit of togetherness that infused them. As I matured, I assumed responsibilities that allowed me to give back, such as coordinating games during the summer party or helping set up for our holiday celebrations. These responsibilities taught me organizational skills and the significance of leadership grounded in service to others.

Additionally, I have seen how the diversity in our neighborhood enriches our interactions. Members share stories of their heritage, customs, and traditions that paint a rich mosaic of cultures. This exposure has instilled in me an appreciation for the differing perspectives and experiences people bring to the table. In particular, I cherish the opportunity to learn from my neighbors about their various cultures and beliefs. This has expanded my worldview and inspired me to embrace differences with empathy rather than judgment.

One unforgettable experience was assisting in a community clean-up initiative. After noticing increased litter in our local park, residents came together to restore it to its original beauty. This project underscored the power of community action. No single individual could have made the park pristine, but our joint effort transformed it within a weekend. Working alongside my neighbors, I realized how shared efforts can tackle even daunting challenges, embodying the spirit of cooperation and resilience.

These occasions have also increased my confidence in my ability to effect change. Witnessing tangible results from our collective hard work has encouraged me to take initiative in other areas of my life, such as in school and extracurricular activities. Whether leading a group project or volunteering at local organizations, the lessons from my community have taught me the value of stepping up and contributing my unique skills and perspectives.

Being part of this community has also taught me about empathy and support. I have observed and learned that everyone, at some point, will face challenges and will need the support of those around them. Whether comforting a neighbor during a difficult time or celebrating a personal milestone, we are reminded that our lives are interconnected. These interactions have propelled me to be a listener and a friend, qualities I believe are essential in any community.

As I anticipate the transition to college, I carry these experiences and values with me, hoping to find new communities to be part of. I am eager to contribute positively to the university setting and learn from the diverse perspectives of others. My neighborhood has provided me with a strong foundation in collaboration, respect, and empathy, which I aim to carry into my collegiate pursuits and beyond. Ultimately, my relationship with my community has shaped my understanding of belonging, and I am motivated to cultivate that same sense of connection wherever life leads me next.

Growing up in a small town in the Midwest, I have always felt a strong connection to my local library. The library isn’t just a place where books are kept on dusty shelves; it’s a gathering spot, a place where stories are shared, and a community is built. From a young age, I have been a regular visitor, and my relationship with the library evolved significantly over time. The library is where my love for reading and learning was nurtured, and it’s where I eventually became involved in volunteer work.

As a child, the library felt magical. I would wander through the aisles, my fingers brushing against the spines of books filled with worlds waiting to be discovered. I remember joining the summer reading program, which kept me engaged during those long, warm days. The librarians were friendly faces who encouraged my curiosity, recommending books that broadened my horizons. The library became my sanctuary, a space where I could explore my interests and let my imagination soar.

In high school, my connection to the library deepened when I began volunteering there. It started as a requirement for community service hours, but quickly became much more. I found myself committing to regular shifts, assisting at events, and eventually taking part in organizing activities for younger children. This experience allowed me to see firsthand the impact the library had on the lives of others in my community.

Volunteering at the library taught me valuable lessons about responsibility and communication. It wasn’t just about shelving books; it was about interacting with patrons, understanding their needs, and providing assistance. I also had the opportunity to lead storytime sessions for kids, which challenged my public speaking skills and taught me how to engage with different age groups. The library has been a pivotal part of my education outside the classroom. It has helped me develop a sense of belonging, community service, and leadership. It’s a place that continues to shape me, pushing me to think critically about the world around me and how I can contribute to it. The bonds I've formed there have not only enriched my high school experience but also inspired me to pursue a future in which I can continue to support and uplift those around me through education and community service.

In reflection, my relationship with the library is more than just my love for books; it represents my connection to the community and my growth as an individual. I have learned that community service and active participation can influence not only our own lives but also the lives of those around us. As I move forward into college and beyond, I am eager to seek out opportunities where I can continue to learn from and contribute to communities, just as I have with my local library.

When I first joined my high school’s environmental club, I didn’t think of it as joining a community. I just wanted to do something responsible and maybe strengthen my college applications. But over time, I realized that the group was more than a collection of students working on projects—it was a small network of people who shared an idea of what caring for the world could look like. Through working and growing with them, I began to see how a community can shape identity, purpose, and perspective.  

At my first meeting, I was quiet. The older students spoke confidently about composting systems and local recycling policies, while I barely understood the terms. I signed up to help plant trees in a nearby park mostly because I felt too awkward to say no. On that chilly Saturday morning, I stood next to a sophomore named Dave, who explained the correct depth of the holes and teased me good-naturedly when my first sapling tilted sideways. By the end of the day, my hands were caked in mud, but I felt unexpectedly proud. The activity had been small, yet it connected me to something larger than myself—a piece of land that would, hopefully, grow greener because of our work.

After that, I started coming to every meeting. We discussed ways to reduce plastic use in the cafeteria and to campaign for better recycling bins around town. I began researching facts on ocean pollution and suggesting poster designs. Gradually, I noticed that I wasn’t just participating; I was contributing ideas that the group actually used. When my suggestion for a “No Straws Week” turned into a schoolwide event covered in the local newspaper, I felt the shared excitement of accomplishment. We weren’t changing the entire world, but we were improving our small corner of it.

Working with people who cared about the same issues taught me how to listen. Not every member agreed on which projects mattered most. Some prioritized campus gardening, while others wanted to push for renewable energy education. Early on, I would get frustrated when my proposals weren’t chosen. But over time, I learned how to debate respectfully, support peers, and celebrate group successes even when they weren’t mine. The environmental club became a kind of workshop where I learned democratic cooperation on a small scale.

The group also affected how I saw my everyday habits. Before joining, I hadn’t thought much about the waste I created or the energy I consumed. After participating in weekly discussions, I found myself turning off lights more consistently, carrying a reusable bottle, and encouraging my family to compost. I was surprised at how community involvement could ripple outward into personal responsibility. My parents began saving food scraps for our new compost bin, and soon my younger brother was reminding me to recycle properly. The club’s influence reached beyond our meetings—it changed my home.

One of the most memorable experiences came during our annual cleanup of the riverbank near downtown. The day was cloudy, and most of us were covered in dirt and mosquito bites by noon. As we filled the last bags, I looked at the piles of trash we had collected and the clearer stretch of water behind us. It seemed like a metaphor for community itself: messy work that, when shared, reveals something better underneath. I understood more fully that belonging to a group isn’t always about comfort—it’s about commitment and shared effort.

Now, as a senior serving as club president, I spend less time digging holes or collecting cans and more time guiding others. I plan meetings, coordinate events, and mentor new members who arrive shy and hesitant, just as I once was. Watching them find confidence reminds me of my own growth within this circle.  

My relationship with the environmental club has shown me that community is not just about being surrounded by people who agree but about learning, cooperating, and improving together. The group has made me more aware, more patient, and more hopeful. Whether I’m working on environmental issues in college or something entirely different, I know that what I learned from this community—how to listen, lead, and act with purpose—will stay with me everywhere I go.

When I first joined the robotics team at my high school, I wasn’t sure I belonged there. I had always liked science, but robotics seemed like a world of wires, gears, and code that only the “tech geniuses” understood. On the first day, everyone seemed to be speaking a language I didn’t know, filled with abbreviations and programming terms. I remember standing awkwardly near the corner of the lab, holding a wrench but not really knowing what to do with it. That moment could have been the end of my story with the robotics team, but instead it became the beginning of something much larger—a community that shaped how I see collaboration and discovery.

At first, my contributions were small. I helped organize tools, took notes, and tried to follow the logic behind others’ designs. What struck me was how patient the team was with newcomers. Older members invited me to ask questions, even when they were busy finishing a build before a competition. Slowly, I started to learn more—how to set up circuits, fix coding errors, and understand why one design worked better than another. Each step felt like solving a puzzle, and each success, even a small one, came with encouragement from the group.

By my second year, I was no longer just watching. I remember one particular meeting when our robot kept losing balance during testing. We couldn’t figure out why. Instead of stepping back, I offered to look at the weight distribution in the design. After some trial and error, I suggested shifting the battery placement and adjusting a set of brackets. It worked. That small victory gave me confidence not only in my technical ability but also in my role as a problem-solver within the team.

The robotics team didn’t just teach me to build machines; it taught me how to build trust. Collaboration became the core of everything we did. Sometimes that meant staying late to help a teammate rewire a circuit. Other times it meant mediating differences when ideas clashed. I learned that working in a group meant listening carefully, respecting each person’s approach, and recognizing that the best solutions often come from combining perspectives. It wasn’t about whose idea was best; it was about what worked best for the team.

As competitions approached, the atmosphere changed from casual experimentation to focused determination. Long evenings in the lab tested our patience and teamwork. When our robot malfunctioned during our first regional match, disappointment hung heavy over the group. But instead of giving in to frustration, we gathered around the table and began troubleshooting. In that moment, I felt the strength of our community. Everyone contributed skills, from programming fixes to creative mechanical solutions. By the next round, we were back in the game, cheered on by both teammates and rival schools who respected our perseverance.

By junior year, I found myself mentoring new members the same way older students had once guided me. Teaching basic wiring techniques and explaining code became opportunities to pass along the culture of curiosity and cooperation that had shaped me. Seeing a new student’s eyes light up when something finally clicked reminded me how far both of us had come.

Through robotics, I discovered that community isn’t defined just by shared interests; it’s defined by shared effort. Each meeting, each setback, and each success pulled us closer together. I learned that belonging is not something you find overnight—it’s something you build, bolt by bolt, through the willingness to learn and the courage to contribute.

When I first joined my high school’s environmental club, I did it mostly because I needed another activity to fill the community service requirement. I didn’t think of myself as an “environmental person,” and I certainly didn’t expect the group to change the way I looked at community. The first meeting I attended was awkward; everyone seemed to know each other already, and I stood at the edge of the room pretending to study the recycling posters on the wall. But by the end of that semester, I had found not only a set of friends but also a collective purpose that made my school feel smaller and more connected.  

The environmental club met every Thursday after school, crammed into a classroom that smelled faintly of dry-erase markers and potting soil. The early meetings were filled with big ideas—solar panels for the roof, a school garden, a plastic ban—but few clear plans. I watched as our adviser patiently guided us to take one project at a time. We began with something simple: a campaign to switch from disposable plastic utensils in the cafeteria to compostable ones. My role was to design the posters and talk to students about why we were making the change.  

Those conversations were not always easy. Some classmates mocked us or ignored us, saying it wouldn’t make a difference. Yet as I talked to more people, I learned how to explain our cause in ways that connected to their concerns—whether it was cleaner parks, less waste in landfills, or the school’s reputation for sustainability. I started to understand that community isn’t just about agreement but about finding shared motivation. That realization gave me a new kind of confidence.  

When the switch to compostable utensils finally happened, it felt like a victory much larger than the project itself. The cafeteria looked the same, but I knew that behind every tray was a network of students who had written proposals, met with the principal, and convinced a food-service company to make a change. The day we saw the new utensils appear, we all stayed late after school, picking up trash around the courtyard and laughing about how something so small had taken months of effort.  

After that, I became one of the more active members. I co-led a drive to collect used textbooks for younger students, and I helped organize Earth Week activities. The club meetings turned into a place where I felt comfortable sharing both ideas and mistakes. When one project to start an herb garden failed because we underestimated the cost of soil and planters, no one got discouraged for long. Instead, we learned to scale down, plant seeds directly into recycled containers, and try again.  

Through these experiences, I realized that belonging to a community means contributing even when outcomes are uncertain. It’s about accepting that progress sometimes comes from persistence rather than perfection. I also noticed how the club reflected my broader community: diverse, occasionally divided, but ultimately united by small moments of teamwork. Seeing my peers from different backgrounds join forces to clean a beach or collect recycling taught me how collective action can turn individual effort into real change.  

By senior year, the environmental club was less a form of service and more a part of my identity. I began mentoring younger members, remembering exactly how it felt to stand silently at that first meeting. Now I make sure to greet newcomers and ask for their ideas before they fade into the background. In a way, that gesture represents what the group has taught me—the importance of inclusion, persistence, and respect for every role within a team. The community I once joined reluctantly has become one I now help to lead, reminding me that belonging often grows from the very act of showing up and caring enough to stay.

When I first joined the robotics team at my high school, I thought I was stepping into a world of wires, code, and competition. I had always liked tinkering with gadgets, but I didn’t fully understand how a collection of students could come together to build something complex from scratch. Over time, though, I realized that robotics was about more than engineering. It was about learning how people with different talents and personalities could form a community dedicated to a shared goal. That realization has shaped how I see collaboration, leadership, and problem-solving in every area of my life.  

During my sophomore year, I was one of only a few new members. The veteran students already had their inside jokes and routines, and I wasn’t sure where I fit in. At first, I hung back during meetings, afraid to suggest ideas that might sound naïve. When our robot didn’t move as expected during a test, I quietly observed as others debated what went wrong. One afternoon, the lead programmer asked if anyone wanted to take notes on what each subsystem was doing. It seemed like a small job, but it gave me a reason to ask questions, and I began to see the bigger picture of how electrical, mechanical, and software components connected. That small opening became my path into the group.  

By the time we entered competition season, I was writing code for the robot’s sensors and occasionally helping to troubleshoot mechanical issues. I also noticed how the moods in our workshop shifted from excitement to frustration depending on how well things worked. It became clear that technical skill wasn’t the only thing we needed. Communication mattered just as much. When people got tired or anxious, progress slowed, and misunderstandings spread easily. I started checking in with teammates during long build sessions, offering encouragement or simply making conversation. Over time, I found myself serving as a kind of bridge between different sub-teams. The feeling of belonging grew not only from contributing to our project but also from supporting the people around me.  

Our biggest challenge came during my junior year. Two weeks before a major competition, one motor repeatedly failed during practice runs. Tension filled the room as we tried to fix the problem before the deadline. At one point, I suggested rewriting a control loop to stabilize the current draw. Some team members were skeptical, but I explained my reasoning and proposed testing it. When the robot finally drove smoothly across the floor, everyone cheered. In that moment, I felt proud not just because my code worked but because we had trusted one another enough to listen and collaborate until the solution emerged.  

This experience taught me how communities form through shared effort and trust. No one person built our robot, just as no individual alone can define a community’s success. I learned to appreciate the quiet contributions as much as the dramatic breakthroughs—the hours of sanding parts, the spreadsheets of inventory, the handwritten labels that kept us organized. I also saw how learning from failure binds people together. After every competition, our team gathered in the school cafeteria to list what went wrong and what went right. Those debriefs weren’t always comfortable, but they reminded us that every setback was temporary when faced as a group.  

Now, as one of the team’s captains, I try to create the same welcoming atmosphere that helped me in my first year. I make sure new members know their questions are valuable and that every idea deserves respect. We celebrate small victories, like a perfectly soldered wire, because they’re stepping stones toward larger achievements. Robotics has become more than an extracurricular activity; it’s a community where I’ve learned how to turn curiosity into cooperation and setbacks into growth. Whether I’m coding, leading, or simply listening, I carry that sense of shared purpose with me. It’s taught me that belonging isn’t about fitting into a mold—it’s about helping to build one together.

Every Saturday morning, before most of my friends are awake, I unlock the side door of our local library and help set up folding tables in the community room. By nine o’clock, the room is full of elementary school students, retired volunteers, and a few sleepy high school tutors like me. We are there for the library’s free reading program, which pairs older students with younger children who need extra support. I joined as a freshman because I wanted volunteer hours, but over time the program became the community that shaped how I understand belonging.

At first, I thought of the group in simple terms. The tutors were the helpers, and the children were the ones being helped. I expected my role to be straightforward: explain vocabulary words, sound out difficult sentences, and offer encouragement. Instead, I quickly learned that the relationships in the room were more equal and more complicated than I had imagined. Many of the children arrived carrying frustrations from school, family responsibilities, or the embarrassment of feeling behind. They did not need someone to rescue them. They needed someone patient enough to sit beside them and keep showing up.

One student, Mateo, changed the way I saw the program. When I first met him, he would barely look at the page. He tapped his pencil, asked to go to the bathroom several times, and declared every book “boring” before reading the first sentence. I kept trying new strategies because I did not want to fail him. We read comic books instead of chapter books. We took turns reading dialogue in different voices. We set tiny goals, like finishing one page without stopping. Progress was slow, but it was real. The first time Mateo finished a short story and grinned instead of groaning, I felt proud in a way that was different from getting a good grade. It was shared pride, built from trust.

As I spent more time in the program, I began to notice that the community was held together by small acts that often went unrecognized. Mrs. Alvarez, the librarian who runs the sessions, remembers every child’s favorite series and every volunteer’s exam week. Mr. Chen, a retired accountant, arrives early to sharpen pencils and stack snacks in neat rows. The younger students celebrate one another’s successes, even when they are struggling themselves. Those details taught me that a community is not only defined by a common purpose. It is also defined by routines of care.

My place in the group changed too. I started as one of the quiet volunteers who waited for instructions. Now I help organize reading materials, welcome new tutors, and suggest ways to adapt lessons for students who lose focus easily. I have learned to be dependable rather than dramatic. In a culture that often praises leadership as taking charge, this community showed me another version of leadership: listening carefully, noticing what is needed, and doing it consistently.

The library reading program also connected me more deeply to the town where I live. Before joining, I moved through my week in separate parts: school, home, activities, and friends. The program linked those parts together. I began recognizing students and parents at the grocery store, hearing teachers mention the same children at school, and understanding how shared spaces like libraries can become anchors in a community. I realized that belonging is not automatic. It grows through repeated participation and mutual responsibility.

I know I will leave this library one day for college, but I will not leave behind what this group taught me. It taught me that communities are built less by grand speeches than by steady presence. It taught me that helping others often means letting them change you as well. Most importantly, it taught me that I do not want to be a person who stands at the edge of a group, benefiting from it quietly. I want to be someone who helps hold the door open, sets up the tables, and makes room for others to enter.

I used to think community was something that happened naturally, like a club you joined or a neighborhood you lived in. For a long time, I believed belonging was mostly about being surrounded by people who looked similar or shared the same schedule. Over the past four years, however, I have learned that community is not just a place where people gather. It is something built through repetition, trust, and the willingness to be responsible for one another. The group that taught me this most clearly is my school’s robotics team.

When I first joined as a freshman, I was intimidated by everything. The older students spoke in acronyms I did not understand, debated code and wiring diagrams at a speed that made me feel invisible, and seemed to know exactly where they belonged. I spent my first few meetings doing small tasks, organizing tools, holding pieces in place, and trying not to ask questions that sounded obvious. I admired the team, but I did not yet feel like part of it. I thought membership would arrive all at once, after I had proved myself.

Instead, belonging came in smaller moments. It came when a senior stayed after practice to explain a programming error I had been too embarrassed to mention. It came when our build failed during a scrimmage and no one blamed one person, because everyone immediately began looking for a solution. It came when I was trusted to lead a subteam project, even though I was not fully confident I could do it. Little by little, I understood that this community was not based on perfection. It was based on showing up for shared work.

That realization changed my relationship with the team. I stopped thinking of robotics as a place where I had to earn permission to speak and started treating it as a place where I had a responsibility to contribute. As a sophomore and junior, I became more involved in mentoring newer members. I remembered how overwhelming the workshop had felt at first, so I tried to explain things without making them seem simple or foolish. When a freshman apologized for not knowing how to use a tool, I told him that not knowing was the reason he had joined. Slowly, I found that teaching someone else often deepened my own understanding.

What I value most about the robotics team is that it combines individuality with interdependence. On competition days, each person has a distinct role, from coding to design to scouting, but no one succeeds alone. A robot only functions when separate systems work together, and our team is much the same way. The quiet student who catches a wiring mistake at the last minute matters just as much as the confident driver on the competition floor. Being part of this group has taught me to respect forms of contribution that are easy to overlook.

My relationship with the team has also shaped how I see myself. Before high school, I often waited for other people to define my role before I stepped into it. Robotics has made me more comfortable taking initiative, asking better questions, and accepting that leadership is often less about authority than about steadiness. Sometimes leadership means making decisions under pressure. Other times, it means cleaning the room after everyone leaves or noticing who has not spoken yet.

I know I will not remember every match score or every mechanical design we debated. What I will remember is the way a group of students with different strengths learned to rely on one another. In that workshop, I found more than an extracurricular activity. I found a community that showed me belonging is not passive. It is something we make together, one act of trust at a time.

On Friday nights, while many of my classmates are at football games or out with friends, I am usually in the kitchen of my family’s restaurant, tying on an apron and listening for the first rush of orders. The restaurant is small, loud, and often crowded, but it is also the place where I have learned the most about what it means to belong to a community. I used to think of it simply as my parents’ workplace and my weekend responsibility. Over time, I began to understand it as a gathering place for people whose lives overlap in quiet but meaningful ways. My relationship with this community has shaped how I see work, responsibility, and connection.

My family opened the restaurant before I was born, and for as long as I can remember, it has been part of the rhythm of my life. After school, I often did homework in a corner booth while my parents prepared for dinner service. As I got older, I moved from folding takeout menus to answering the phone, taking orders, and helping customers at the register. At first, I saw these tasks as repetitive and ordinary. I counted the minutes until closing and complained when my friends made plans I could not join. I felt as if I were standing just outside the life I wanted, watching it happen somewhere else.

What changed was not the work itself, but the way I began to notice the people around me. I started recognizing the same customers each week: the elderly couple who split one meal and always asked about school, the nurse who picked up dinner after late shifts, the group of construction workers who joked with my dad in a mix of English and Spanish. I noticed how my mother remembered allergies, favorite dishes, and even small details from past conversations. I saw that people were not only coming for food. They were coming for familiarity, comfort, and the feeling of being known.

As I became more involved, I found my own place within that environment. I learned to greet regulars by name and to tell when someone wanted conversation and when they simply needed a quiet moment after a hard day. During the pandemic, when our dining room closed, these relationships became even more visible. Customers left encouraging notes with tips, bought extra meals to support us, and asked sincerely how my family was doing. In return, we packed orders late into the night, adjusted to new routines, and tried to offer a sense of steadiness during an uncertain time. I understood then that our restaurant was not a business separate from the neighborhood. It was part of the neighborhood’s support system.

Being part of this community has also shown me that contribution is not always dramatic. Often, it looks like sweeping floors after midnight, translating for a customer who feels embarrassed, or staying patient when someone is frustrated. It means paying attention. It means understanding that small acts of reliability can build trust over years. In a world that often celebrates leadership as visibility or authority, my experience has taught me to value consistency and service. I have learned that communities are held together not only by big moments, but by ordinary efforts repeated again and again.

Because of this, I no longer think of the restaurant as the place that kept me from other parts of high school. It gave me a different education, one rooted in responsibility and human connection. It taught me how to listen, how to work with people of different ages and backgrounds, and how to take pride in making others feel welcome. Most importantly, it showed me that belonging is not passive. It is something built through participation, care, and presence. In serving this community, I became part of it, and in many ways, it became part of who I am.

Every Saturday morning, before most of my neighborhood is awake, I unlock the side door of our local library and help turn on the lights for the children’s reading program. The building is small and a little worn, with squeaky floors and shelves that never seem quite straight, but to me it feels like one of the most alive places in town. I first came there in middle school because my mother needed a quiet place to work after dropping me off from soccer practice. At the time, I thought of the library as simply a room full of books and rules about whispering. Over the years, however, it became the community where I learned how much people can give to one another through patience, consistency, and trust.

I officially joined the library volunteer group as a freshman in high school. My first jobs were simple: setting out crayons, taping up signs for events, and checking that stacks of donated books were sorted by reading level. I liked the routine, but I stayed because I began noticing the people who returned every week. There was the shy second grader who always picked books about sharks, the retired teacher who read aloud with dramatic voices, and the father who practiced English while helping his daughter sound out words. The library was not just a service; it was a meeting place for people who might not otherwise cross paths. Watching those interactions made me understand community as something built less by grand gestures than by repeated acts of showing up.

As I became more involved, my role changed. By sophomore year, I was helping lead the reading circle for younger students. At first, I was nervous that the children would see immediately that I was pretending to know what I was doing. Some of them could not sit still, some were embarrassed to read aloud, and some tested every boundary just to see if I would react. I learned quickly that authority mattered less than steadiness. When one boy refused to participate for three weeks in a row, I stopped trying to force him and instead saved a dinosaur book beside me each session. On the fourth week, he picked it up on his own and read two pages. That moment was small, but it taught me that belonging often begins when someone makes space for you before you are ready to step into it.

The community at the library has also challenged me. Our town includes families with very different incomes, backgrounds, and educational experiences, and those differences appear in quiet ways. Some children arrive with notebooks full of questions, while others come mostly because it is a warm, safe place to spend an hour after school. I used to assume helping meant giving people what I thought they lacked. The library taught me to listen first. Parents did not always want advice; sometimes they wanted affordable internet access, a familiar face, or reassurance that their child was not falling behind forever. Being part of this group has made me more attentive to needs that are practical as well as emotional.

What I value most about the library community is that everyone contributes something. The librarians organize with limited resources and endless creativity. The volunteers bring time and energy. The children bring curiosity, honesty, and a kind of joyful unpredictability that keeps the space from becoming too serious. I bring my own strengths too: reliability, calm, and a willingness to do unglamorous work without being asked. In a world that often celebrates leadership as standing in front, this community has shown me the importance of working beside others.

My relationship with the library began as convenience, became commitment, and has now become part of how I understand myself. It is where I learned that community is not defined only by shared identity, but by shared responsibility. When I think about the person I want to be in college, I imagine someone who looks for ways to strengthen the places that sustain others. I know I will enter a new environment with much to learn, but I will carry with me the habits this group has given me: to notice who is left out, to contribute steadily, and to believe that even ordinary spaces can change people.

When I was twelve years old, I walked into the community kitchen at our local Sikh gurdwara and picked up a peeling knife for the first time. My grandmother handed it to me with a simple instruction: "Every potato you peel feeds someone who is hungry." That afternoon, I peeled forty pounds of potatoes alongside strangers who felt like family, and I discovered a community that would shape the person I am becoming.

The practice is called langar, a free communal meal served to anyone who walks through the doors regardless of their background, faith, or status. Every Sunday, our gurdwara feeds between three and four hundred people, and the entire operation runs on volunteers. For the past five years, I have been one of them. What started as something my grandmother nudged me into has become one of the most meaningful commitments in my life.

My role has evolved over time. I graduated from peeling potatoes to cooking large pots of dal, then to coordinating volunteer schedules, and eventually to helping manage the kitchen's weekly grocery logistics. I learned how to estimate how much rice four hundred people require, how to negotiate bulk prices at the wholesale market, and how to delegate tasks to volunteers who range in age from ten to eighty. These are practical skills, but the lessons that matter most to me are not about logistics.

Langar operates on a radical principle: everyone sits on the floor together and eats the same food. There is no head table. There is no VIP section. A doctor sits beside a day laborer, and a teenager serves chai to an elderly woman she has never met. This practice of equality is not just symbolic. It has genuinely changed the way I see the world. In school, I notice how quickly we sort ourselves into hierarchies based on grades, clothing, or social circles. At the gurdwara, those categories dissolve. I have had some of the most honest conversations of my life while chopping onions next to someone whose name I learned five minutes earlier.

One Sunday stands out in particular. A man came in who was clearly living on the street. His clothes were torn, and he seemed hesitant to enter. I watched as one of the older volunteers, Uncle Harpreet, walked over and welcomed him as if he were a longtime friend. He guided the man to a seat, brought him a plate piled high with food, and sat down beside him to eat. There was no pity in it, no performance. It was simply one person recognizing another person's dignity. That moment crystallized something I had been feeling for a long time: that service is not about charity from above but about showing up as an equal.

This community has also taught me about accountability. When I oversleep and consider skipping a Sunday shift, I think about the three other volunteers who are counting on me to have the kitchen ready by nine. When I feel frustrated by a task that seems tedious, I remember that the work is not about me. It is about the woman who walks in after a night shift and needs a warm meal. It is about the family who is new to the country and does not yet know where to turn. Showing up consistently, even when it is inconvenient, has become a discipline that carries into every other part of my life.

I am not the most devout person at the gurdwara. I sometimes struggle with the longer prayer services, and I have plenty of questions about faith that remain unanswered. But I have never once questioned the value of what happens in that kitchen. Langar has taught me that community is built not through grand gestures but through the repetition of small, unglamorous acts of care. It is built through peeling potatoes, stirring pots, and sitting on the floor beside someone whose story you do not yet know but are willing to hear.

My grandmother was right about the potatoes. But she was also right about something larger: that the simplest acts of service can become the foundation of who you are.

Growing up as the youngest member of my family's weekend farmer's market booth, I spent most of my childhood wishing I were somewhere else. Every Saturday morning from April through October, my alarm went off at five, and I dragged myself to our local fairgrounds to help sell the vegetables my grandparents grew on their small plot of land just outside town. While my friends slept in and made plans for the day, I was arranging tomatoes and making change for strangers. For years, I saw the farmer's market as nothing more than an obligation, a chore that stole my weekends. It took me a long time to realize it was actually shaping who I am.

The farmer's market community is not something you join with an application or a membership fee. You become part of it slowly, through showing up week after week, through conversations exchanged while setting up canopy tents in the early morning fog, through the unspoken agreement that the woman who sells honey will always watch your booth when you need a bathroom break. Our market has about thirty regular vendors, and over the years they became an extended family I never expected to have. Mr. Delaney, who sells woodworked cutting boards, taught me how to talk to customers without being pushy. Maria, who runs the tamale stand two booths down from ours, showed me that generosity is a language everyone understands when she handed out free samples to anyone who looked like they were having a hard day.

What I value most about this community is how it brings together people who would otherwise never cross paths. Our market includes retired professionals, young families trying to build small businesses, immigrants sharing foods from their home countries, and teenagers like me who got roped in by their families. On any given Saturday, I might have a conversation with a retired engineer about the best way to grow peppers, then turn around and help a little kid pick out the biggest ear of corn. The market strips away the usual barriers that separate people, because everyone is there for the same basic reason: to share something they grew or made with their own hands.

Being part of this community also taught me about responsibility in a way that classroom lessons never could. When customers started coming to our booth specifically because they recognized me and trusted my recommendations, I understood that my presence mattered. I was not just filling a spot. I was part of a network of relationships built on consistency and honesty. If I told someone our peaches were sweet this week, they needed to actually be sweet, because that person would be back next Saturday and would remember.

Last summer, when my grandmother had knee surgery and could not come to the market for six weeks, I ran the booth mostly on my own. I was nervous at first, but vendor after vendor stopped by to check on me, offer advice, and make sure I had everything I needed. That experience showed me that the community I had been passively absorbing for years had actually absorbed me right back.

I still wake up at five on Saturday mornings, but I no longer wish I were somewhere else. The farmer's market taught me that community is not always something you choose. Sometimes it chooses you, and you are better for it. I have learned to listen, to show up, and to understand that the simplest interactions, a conversation over a basket of tomatoes, can become the foundation for something meaningful.

Growing up in a small town where everyone seemed to know each other by name, I never really thought about what it meant to belong to a community. It was just the air I breathed, the background noise of my life. But when I joined my local volunteer fire department as a junior member at sixteen, I discovered that belonging to something is not just about showing up. It is about choosing to show up when everything in you wants to stay home.

My town has a population of about three thousand people, and our fire department is entirely volunteer. There are no professional firefighters waiting at the station around the clock. Instead, there are teachers, mechanics, retirees, and now a high school student who heard a pager go off during a calculus test and felt her heart race with something other than math anxiety. When I first joined, I expected to learn about hoses and ladders and fire safety. I did learn those things, but what I did not expect was how much I would learn about myself and about the invisible threads that hold a community together.

My first real call came on a Wednesday evening in November. A kitchen fire had broken out in an older home on the edge of town, and when I arrived at the station, I could see the tension on everyone's faces. These were not strangers to the people inside that house. They were neighbors. The woman who lived there had taught Sunday school to half the crew when they were children. That night, I mostly stayed out of the way, handing equipment to more experienced members and watching how they worked together with a kind of silent coordination that seemed almost rehearsed. But it was not rehearsed. It was trust, built over years of training and shared purpose.

In the months that followed, I threw myself into training sessions every Thursday night. I learned how to operate the pump on our engine, how to perform search and rescue in a smoke-filled room, and how to administer basic first aid. I made mistakes constantly. I once connected a hose fitting backward during a drill, and the resulting spray soaked our assistant chief from head to toe. He laughed, handed me a towel, and showed me how to do it correctly. That moment taught me something important: in a real community, mistakes are not failures. They are opportunities for someone to show you patience.

What strikes me most about the fire department is how it dissolves the boundaries that normally separate people. At school, I exist in the usual social ecosystem of cliques and hierarchies. At the station, none of that matters. A seventeen-year-old and a sixty-two-year-old retired carpenter are equals when they are pulling a hose line together. Age, background, and occupation all fade when there is a shared mission. I have had conversations at the station that I could never have had anywhere else, learning about grief from a paramedic, hearing about resilience from a veteran, and understanding sacrifice from a single mother who still finds time to respond to calls at two in the morning.

This community has shaped my understanding of responsibility. Before joining, I thought responsibility meant completing my homework and keeping my room reasonably clean. Now I understand it as something deeper. It means being someone others can count on, even when it is inconvenient or frightening. It means answering the pager at midnight and driving through freezing rain because someone you may never meet needs help.

I do not know exactly what my future holds, but I know that the fire department has given me a framework for how I want to move through the world. I want to be part of communities that demand something of me, that challenge me to grow beyond my comfort zone, and that remind me how powerful it is when ordinary people decide to take care of each other. That is what belonging really means, and I carry it with me wherever I go.

When I was fourteen, I walked into the back room of our local library expecting to find a quiet place to study. Instead, I found eight people hunched over a sprawling board game, arguing passionately about whether a trade of two wheat for one ore was fair. That was my introduction to the Westfield Tabletop Gaming Club, and it changed the way I think about community, strategy, and what it means to truly belong somewhere.

Before joining the club, I had always been someone who floated between friend groups without really anchoring myself to any one of them. I played soccer in the fall, did robotics in the spring, and sat with different people at lunch depending on my mood. I was friendly with many people but deeply connected to very few. There was nothing wrong with this exactly, but I often felt like a guest in every room I entered, welcome but not essential.

The gaming club was different from the start. The members ranged from quiet freshmen to loud seniors, from people who loved fantasy novels to people who were obsessed with historical simulations. What united us was a shared willingness to sit down, learn complicated rules, and commit to an experience together. In a world where so many interactions happen through screens and last only seconds, spending three hours around a table with the same group of people felt almost radical.

I learned quickly that tabletop games reveal character in surprising ways. You discover who is patient and who is impulsive, who plays to win at all costs and who plays to make sure everyone is having fun. I noticed things about myself too. I tended to avoid conflict in games just as I did in real life, choosing safe strategies that kept me in the middle of the pack. One of the older members, a senior named David, pulled me aside once and said something that stuck with me. He told me that playing it safe was just another way of not really participating. He meant it about the game, but I heard it as something larger.

Gradually, I started taking more risks, both at the table and outside of it. I volunteered to teach new members how to play, which meant standing in front of a group and speaking with authority about something I cared about. I organized a charity game night at the library that raised over three hundred dollars for our local food bank. Eventually, I became one of the club's co-leaders, helping to choose which games we played and making sure newer members felt included. These were small acts of leadership, but they mattered to me because they were the first times I felt genuinely responsible for other people's experience in a group.

What I value most about this community is its culture of inclusion. Tabletop gaming can seem intimidating from the outside, with its dense rulebooks and specialized vocabulary, but our club made a deliberate effort to lower those barriers. We always had at least one simple game available for newcomers. We explained rules patiently and never mocked someone for making a strategic mistake. I came to understand that the best communities do not just happen naturally. They are built through countless small, intentional choices to make people feel seen and valued.

This experience has shaped how I want to engage with communities in college and beyond. I know now that belonging is not something you wait to receive. It is something you create by showing up consistently, investing in others, and being willing to take risks. A board game might seem like a trivial thing, but the connections formed across that table were anything but trivial. They taught me that community is built in the quiet moments of patience, generosity, and shared commitment, whether you are negotiating a trade, teaching a new player, or simply making room at the table for one more person.

For as long as I can remember, the wrestling room has been my second home. The smell of rubber mats and sweat, the sound of coaches shouting instructions, the feeling of exhaustion after a two-hour practice — these are the things that have shaped who I am over the past four years. Wrestling is not just a sport I participate in. It is a community that has fundamentally changed the way I see myself and the world around me.

I joined the wrestling team as a freshman mostly because my older brother had wrestled and made it sound exciting. I had no idea what I was getting into. The first few weeks were brutal. I lost nearly every practice match, struggled to keep up with conditioning, and questioned whether I belonged there at all. There were moments when I considered quitting and finding something easier to do with my time. But something kept me coming back to that room every afternoon.

What kept me there was the team itself. I noticed early on that wrestling has a unique culture compared to other sports. Individual performance matters enormously, but you cannot improve without your teammates. Every practice, someone has to be willing to let you work on a move, to push you when you are struggling, and to challenge you when you get comfortable. My teammates did all of these things for me, and I eventually learned to do the same for them.

By sophomore year, I had developed real friendships within the program. Older wrestlers took time to coach me on technique even when they had their own preparation to worry about. I began to understand that the team's success depended on everyone getting better, not just the top wrestlers. This idea was completely new to me. I had always thought of sports in terms of individual achievement, but wrestling taught me that community and personal growth are deeply connected.

My junior year brought the greatest challenge of my wrestling career. I suffered a shoulder injury in November that threatened to end my season. I sat on the sidelines for six weeks watching my teammates compete without me. During that time, I could have disconnected from the program. Instead, I found ways to stay involved. I kept track of statistics, helped younger wrestlers study film, and cheered loudly at every match. My coaches and teammates made it clear that I was still valued even when I could not compete.

That experience shifted my perspective in an important way. I realized that belonging to a community is not just about what you can perform or produce. It is about showing up consistently and caring about something bigger than yourself. When I returned from injury in January, I carried that understanding with me onto the mat.

This past season, my senior year, I served as one of the team captains. That role asked me to think carefully about how to bring people together. I tried to create the same environment that had helped me as a freshman — one where struggling was acceptable and effort was celebrated. I made it a point to talk to every person on the roster, not just the starters. I wanted everyone to feel that they had a real place on the team.

Wrestling has given me more than athletic skill or competitive experience. It has taught me patience, resilience, and the importance of supporting others even when you have nothing direct to gain from doing so. The relationships I have built in that wrestling room are among the most meaningful of my life. I have learned that a strong community is built through honesty, hard work, and genuine care for the people around you.

As I prepare to move on to college, I know that these lessons will stay with me. I am eager to find new communities where I can both contribute and grow. The wrestling room taught me how to do both, and I am grateful for every difficult moment it gave me.

For as long as I can remember, I have been part of my school's marching band. What started as a way to continue playing the clarinet I had picked up in fifth grade became something much more significant over time. The marching band is not just an extracurricular activity to me — it is a community that has shaped who I am in ways I did not expect.

When I first joined as a freshman, I was overwhelmed by the size of the group and the demands of the program. We practiced six days a week during the summer, spending hours in the heat learning formations and perfecting our music. I remember thinking that I had made a terrible mistake signing up. The senior members seemed impossibly confident, and I struggled to keep up with both the physical demands of marching and the mental challenge of memorizing our entire show.

What changed my perspective was a moment during our third week of practice. I had made the same mistake in our closer for what felt like the hundredth time, and I was on the verge of giving up entirely. One of the senior section leaders named Marcus noticed my frustration and stayed after practice to help me work through the difficult passage. He did not have to do that. He gave up his own free time for someone he barely knew, and that small act of generosity made me feel like I truly belonged there.

From that point forward, I began to understand what the marching band was really about. Yes, we worked toward competition trophies and public performances, but the deeper purpose was the culture of mutual support we maintained. Every person in that group of nearly two hundred students had a responsibility to the others. If one section was struggling, the rest of the band felt it. We either succeeded together or failed together, and that reality created a genuine sense of accountability I had never experienced before.

As I progressed through high school, my role in the community evolved. By my junior year, I was helping younger students navigate the same challenges I had faced as a freshman. I found that teaching someone else how to fix a mistake is often the best way to deepen your own understanding. Explaining fundamentals forced me to think more carefully about my own technique and my own attitude toward difficulty. I became a better musician and a better teammate at the same time.

The band also taught me how to handle pressure in a productive way. Performing in front of thousands of people at competitions means that nerves are unavoidable. But I learned that nervousness is manageable when you trust the people around you. Knowing that every member of the band had put in the same hard work gave me confidence on the field. We were not just individuals standing next to each other; we were something that only worked because of collective effort.

I carry the lessons of this community with me into everything I do. The value of showing up consistently, the importance of supporting others without being asked, and the understanding that meaningful achievement rarely happens alone are principles the marching band made concrete for me. They are not just ideas I read about in a book — they are things I lived through across four years of early mornings, rainy practices, and shared victories.

When I think about the kind of student I want to be in college, I think about the community I want to build there. I hope to find groups that challenge me, support me, and hold me accountable the way my band has done. I am ready for that next community, and I am bringing everything this one gave me.

Growing up, I always thought of community as something that just happened to you — the neighborhood you lived in, the school you attended, the family you were born into. It was not until I joined my high school's Model United Nations team that I truly understood what it means to actively choose a community and let it shape who you are.

I joined MUN my sophomore year mostly because a friend convinced me to sign up. I had no real expectations and honestly did not know much about international relations or parliamentary procedure. Walking into that first practice, I felt completely out of place. The other students seemed confident and knowledgeable, throwing around terms like "resolution" and "bloc" as though they had been speaking this language their whole lives. I remember sitting quietly in the back row, wondering if I had made a serious mistake.

But something kept me coming back. Slowly, I started to understand the structure of debates and the way arguments were built. I started staying after practice to research my assigned countries and their positions on global issues. I began to see that this community valued preparation and curiosity above everything else, and those were qualities I could develop if I worked at them.

By junior year, I had become a more active participant. I learned that MUN was not just about winning awards at conferences. It was about understanding perspectives that were completely different from your own. When I was assigned to represent a country whose policies I personally disagreed with, I had to argue in favor of those positions as convincingly as possible. That experience challenged me in ways that no classroom assignment ever had. I had to separate my own opinions from my responsibilities as a delegate, which turned out to be one of the most valuable lessons I have learned in high school.

The community itself is what made these lessons stick. My teammates pushed each other constantly, but it never felt cutthroat or discouraging. When someone struggled with public speaking, others offered feedback and encouragement. When someone wrote an especially strong position paper, the whole team celebrated that accomplishment. There was a shared understanding that each person's growth made the team stronger overall. I had never been part of a group that operated that way before, and it genuinely changed how I think about collaboration.

I also found myself taking on responsibilities I would have avoided in the past. I helped lead workshops for newer members, explaining concepts that had once confused me. Standing in front of my peers and teaching felt uncomfortable at first, but it eventually became something I genuinely enjoyed. I realized that leadership does not always mean having all the answers. Sometimes it means being willing to say, "I struggled with this too, and here is how I worked through it."

My relationship with this community has made me a more patient listener, a stronger writer, and a more confident speaker. More importantly, it has given me a model for how I want to engage with communities in the future. I want to find groups where intellectual curiosity is valued, where people challenge each other respectfully, and where individual effort contributes to something larger than any one person.

College represents the next opportunity to find and build that kind of community. I am looking forward to bringing the habits and values that MUN helped me develop into new environments, new conversations, and new challenges. I do not think I will ever again believe that community is something that simply happens to you. I know now that the most meaningful communities are the ones you commit to building, one practice at a time.

When I was nine years old, my grandmother placed a wooden crochet hook in my hands and told me that every stitch was a small act of patience. I did not understand what she meant at the time. I just wanted to make something. But over the years, as I became part of the broader crafting community, I began to understand that she was teaching me something much larger than a hobby.

The crafting community is not the kind of group most people think of when they imagine a tight-knit community. It does not have a uniform or a team name. It exists in living rooms, online forums, local yarn shops, and community centers. Yet the sense of belonging I have found within it has shaped who I am more than almost anything else in my life.

I first discovered that this community extended beyond my grandmother's kitchen when I joined an online crafting forum at age thirteen. I was nervous to post my beginner projects, certain that more experienced crafters would dismiss my clumsy attempts. Instead, I was met with encouragement, detailed feedback, and genuine warmth. People took time out of their days to help a teenager they had never met improve her technique. That experience taught me something important about generosity and the way communities can lift individuals up rather than gatekeep them out.

As I grew more skilled, I began attending a weekly crafting circle at my local library. The group included people of all ages, backgrounds, and skill levels. A retired teacher sat next to a college student. A grandmother compared notes with a teenager. What united us was not our demographics but our shared focus and our willingness to sit together quietly and create. In a world that often feels loud and disconnected, those afternoons felt like a form of shelter.

Being part of this community also pushed me to develop skills I did not expect. I learned to give constructive feedback without discouraging someone who had worked hard on a project. I learned to receive criticism graciously and use it to improve. I learned that teaching something to another person is one of the best ways to deepen your own understanding of it. When I started helping newer members of the crafting circle, I became more thoughtful about my own process and more aware of the choices I made in my work.

The crafting community also introduced me to conversations about broader issues I had not previously considered. Many crafters I follow and admire use their platforms to discuss sustainability, fair labor practices in the textile industry, and the importance of supporting small businesses. Through these conversations, I started thinking more critically about where materials come from and what values I want to express through my creative choices. A community I joined simply to share a hobby ended up expanding my understanding of the world.

There is something specific about making things by hand that draws a particular kind of person. Crafters tend to be patient, detail-oriented, and comfortable with the idea that good work takes time. Being surrounded by people who hold these values has influenced how I approach challenges outside of crafting as well. When I face a difficult assignment or a frustrating situation, I think about the fact that even the most experienced crafter occasionally has to unravel rows of work and start over. The willingness to begin again without giving up is something this community modeled for me long before I could articulate it.

My grandmother passed away two years ago. At her memorial, I wore a shawl I had crocheted myself, using a pattern she had taught me. It felt like the right way to honor what she had given me, which was not just a skill but an entire community and way of seeing the world. Every time I pick up a hook or sit down with my crafting circle, I carry that forward. I am still just trying to make something, but now I understand that making things is rarely only about the finished object.

Being a first-generation college student, I have always felt a strong connection to my community, a small town brimming with people who share similar dreams and aspirations. Growing up in a place where college was not a common path for many, I have seen firsthand the struggles and sacrifices that have shaped my journey. This community has been my foundation, supporting me in more ways than I could have imagined and shaping my identity and goals.

My parents immigrated to this country seeking a better life, and they worked tirelessly to provide for our family. From them, I learned the value of hard work and perseverance, traits that are deeply rooted in our community. Our town is a tapestry of diverse cultures and backgrounds, where everyone looks out for each other. This sense of camaraderie has instilled in me a strong sense of responsibility to give back and uplift those around me.

In high school, I joined a mentorship program designed to support students like me in their pursuit of higher education.
