I don't want to romanticize Guthrie. The nearest hospital is forty minutes away. Our school can barely fund its science lab. Some Friday nights feel so quiet I swear I can hear the earth rotating. But this place gave me an attention to detail that I carry everywhere, a habit of noticing what other people overlook—the way a fence line tells you the history of a property, the way a storm shelf cloud means you have exactly fifteen minutes.

I'm not applying to college to escape my background. I'm applying because I want the language and the tools to render it fully, to write about places like mine with the complexity they deserve. Nobody else is going to do it. There are only 183 of us.

The summer I turned fourteen, I helped my grandfather pull a calf from its mother at two in the morning, my arms slick to the elbows with fluid I couldn't name, the barn lit by a single bulb that swung whenever wind pushed through the gaps in the siding. The heifer was down on her side, breathing in shallow grunts, and my grandfather spoke to her the way he spoke to everything — quietly, without rush, as though time was something he'd personally negotiated with. When the calf finally slid free and lay steaming on the straw, he told me to rub it hard with a towel. I rubbed until my shoulders burned. The calf blinked, sneezed, and tried to stand. My grandfather said nothing else. He didn't need to.

I grew up in Harlan, population 803 on a generous day, in a county where the nearest traffic light is twenty-two miles east and the grocery store closes at six because everyone's already home by then. Our house sits on forty acres of mostly hill, bordered by a creek that dries to a trickle every August and floods its banks every March like clockwork. I know this land in a way that feels almost cellular — which fencepost leans, where the morels grow after a warm rain, the exact pitch of coyotes calling from the ridge behind the tobacco barn.

People sometimes talk about small-town life like it's simple. I understand why. From the outside, Harlan looks like a postcard somebody forgot to send: white church steeple, grain elevator, a gas station that still checks your oil. But growing up here taught me that simplicity and depth are not opposites. My neighbors can read weather in the behavior of cattle, predict frost by the color of a sunset, and fix a transmission with parts from three different decades. They carry knowledge that doesn't fit on a transcript.

Still, I'd be lying if I said Harlan gave me everything I needed. My high school graduating class has nineteen people. We don't have AP Physics or a debate team. When I wanted to study Mandarin, I found a free app and practiced in the cab of my dad's truck during hay season, stumbling over tones while dust came through the vents. When I got interested in constitutional law after watching a documentary at the library, I emailed a professor at the state university who, to my genuine shock, emailed back. We corresponded for six months. She sent me a reading list. I worked through it sitting on the tailgate during my lunch breaks at the farm supply store where I stock shelves on weekends.

I am not ashamed of where I come from, and I am not trying to escape it. But I've realized that loving a place and outgrowing certain parts of it can happen simultaneously, and that this isn't betrayal. My grandfather, who never left the county except for two years in the Army, told me once that the smartest thing a plant does is send its roots down and its leaves up, toward whatever light it can find. He was talking about his tomatoes. He was also not talking about his tomatoes.

I want to bring Harlan with me — its patience, its practical intelligence, its insistence that you show up and do the work regardless of weather or mood. And I want to bring back whatever I learn, because places like mine are often studied from the outside and rarely spoken for from within. I have mud on my boots and a reading list in my back pocket, and I don't see those things as contradictions. They're just both mine.

The summer I turned fourteen, I helped my grandfather pull a calf from its mother at two in the morning while freezing rain needled our necks. The heifer was a first-timer, wild-eyed and bellowing, and Grandpa's hands were too arthritic to get a solid grip on the chains. So I braced my boots in the mud and pulled when he told me to pull, and when the calf finally slid out in a rush of fluid and membrane, I stood there shaking, covered in things I couldn't name, feeling like I'd witnessed something ancient. Grandpa wiped his hands on his jeans and said, "Well, she'll do," and we walked back to the house without another word.

That silence is a language where I come from. Harmon, Nebraska, population 412 on a generous day, is a place where people communicate through what they do rather than what they say. My father tells me he loves me by checking the air pressure in my tires before I drive the thirty miles to school. Mrs. Klausen, who runs the post office and also somehow the library, shows she cares about my future by sliding college brochures into our PO box without comment. When old Dale Wecker heard my mom had the flu last January, a pot of soup appeared on our porch with no note attached. Everyone knew it was Dale's because he always uses too much dill.

Growing up here has made me fluent in this unspoken dialect, but it has also made me hungry for the spoken one. I am the kid who checked out every book our 600-square-foot library owned on subjects Harmon doesn't talk about: philosophy, politics, the history of jazz, feminist theory. I read Simone de Beauvoir while sitting on a grain bin and argued with Nietzsche in the passenger seat of my dad's truck on the way to get fence posts. There is no AP program at my school. My graduating class has eleven people. My chemistry teacher also teaches PE and drives a bus route. None of this is a complaint. It is just the shape of the container I grew up inside, and I have loved that container even as I've pressed against its walls.

The tension of loving a place and needing to leave it is something I carry in my chest like a second heartbeat. At football games on Friday nights, when the whole town folds into the bleachers and the field lights are the brightest thing for miles, I feel a belonging so physical it aches. I know every face. I know whose truck is whose by the sound of its engine. I know that the coyotes will start up at halftime and that Mr. Pruitt will fall asleep in the third quarter, and this knowledge is a kind of wealth. But I also know that when I brought up climate policy at the co-op last spring, three men stared at me like I'd started speaking Mandarin. I know that my best friend thinks college is a waste of money and that my neighbor still calls the Vietnamese family in the next town over a word I will not write here.

I do not want to be the person who escapes a small town and then mocks it from a distance. That story is too easy, and it is dishonest. Harmon taught me to work until my hands crack, to notice when someone needs help before they ask, to appreciate silence as a form of intimacy rather than emptiness. But I also want to sit in a room full of people who argue passionately about ideas, who come from places I have never imagined, who challenge the assumptions I don't even know I carry.

I think the most interesting version of myself exists at the intersection of these two longings, the girl who can pull a calf at two a.m. and then stay up until four reading James Baldwin. I do not want to choose between these selves. I want to build a life where both of them have room to breathe.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a cookbook at the town garage sale with a sticky note inside the front cover that read *For someone who deserves better than casseroles.* I bought it for a quarter and hid it under my bed like contraband. In Harlan, Kentucky, casseroles are not just food. They are language.

When someone dies, a green bean casserole appears on the porch before the body is cold. When a baby is born, there is something cream-of-mushroom-based waiting in the driveway. My grandmother has brought the same broccoli-cheese dish to every church potluck since 1987, and no one has ever once suggested she try something different. That consistency is not laziness. It is loyalty. I understand this now, but at fourteen I was mostly just embarrassed by it.

Growing up in a town of 1,600 people means everyone knows which truck is parked in whose driveway at two in the morning. It means the pharmacist asks about your mother by her first name and the librarian orders books she thinks you specifically would like. Mrs. Combs ordered me a collection of Appalachian poetry once without me even asking, and left it at the desk with a handwritten note: *Thought of you.* I read it in one sitting on the library's scratchy carpet, then checked it out four more times.

My identity is tangled up in contradictions I am still learning to hold. I grew up in a place that gave me extraordinary closeness and sometimes suffocating smallness in the same breath. My town is largely poor, largely white, and shaped by decades of coal's rise and collapse. People here carry grief like furniture they have stopped noticing. I noticed it because I grew up inside it. My father worked underground for eleven years before the mine closed. He does not talk about it often, but occasionally at dinner he will go quiet in a specific way that tells me he is back down there, somewhere in the dark.

I am a person made partly of that silence. I am also made of the way mountain vowels stretch when my aunt laughs, and the particular green of the hills in April, and the way strangers wave from their porches even when they do not know you. I have spent years being ashamed of the accent I code-switch away from in formal settings and proud of it in the same conflicted hour. I am still figuring out which version of my voice is the truest one.

The cookbook taught me something. I made my family roasted chicken with herbs from a pot on the windowsill, and my grandmother ate two helpings and then asked me to explain what tarragon was. She had never heard the word. I had never cooked anything that complicated in my life. We sat at the kitchen table for an extra hour, just talking, while the dishes soaked. That felt like something.

I do not want to leave Harlan behind the way a door gets shut. I want to carry it the way you carry a language, present in your mouth even when you are speaking something else. The closeness, the grief, the casseroles, the creek behind the elementary school, the pharmacist who knows my name—these are not limitations I am escaping. They are the specific, unglamorous material I am made of, and I intend to understand them more deeply, not less, wherever I go next.

The combine harvester breaks down every August, without fail. My dad says it's spite — the machine knows exactly when we need it most, right before the wheat comes in, and decides to quit. I grew up learning time by harvests instead of semesters, and I didn't know that was unusual until I was twelve and mentioned it to a girl at a regional spelling bee who looked at me like I'd said something in a foreign language.

Millersfield, Kansas has 847 people, one stoplight, and a gas station that doubles as a pizza place on Friday nights. I was born into a place where everyone knew my grandfather before they knew me, where my last name — Pruett — carried forty years of reputation before I ever opened my mouth. That weight felt suffocating sometimes. It also felt like something solid to stand on.

Growing up rural isn't an identity people talk about in the same breath as ethnicity or religion, but it shaped me as completely as either would have. I learned to read weather before I learned to read books. I understood the difference between a pickup that belonged to someone passing through and one that belonged to someone who mattered before I understood anything about social class. My cultural vocabulary was grain prices and county fairs and the particular silence that falls over flat land right before a thunderstorm rolls in from the west.

My school had sixty kids in my graduating class. I played in the band, ran cross country, joined FFA, and still had time because there was no traffic, no distraction, just the particular pressure of a small place where you cannot disappear. When I made a mistake, everyone saw it. When I figured something out, the same. There was no anonymity, and I both resented and relied on that.

What I struggled with more was the cultural isolation. We had one Mexican restaurant, no bookstore, and internet that worked about sixty percent of the time. I read constantly, partly from genuine love and partly from desperation. I found James Baldwin at fifteen through a library interlibrary loan that took three weeks. He wrote about being defined by where you're from in ways that didn't get to choose, and even though his experience was nothing like mine, something in the structure of that problem felt familiar.

My town is suspicious of outsiders and skeptical of change in ways that have caused real harm — I've watched good people leave because Millersfield didn't have room for who they were. I carry that knowledge honestly. But I've also watched my neighbor drive forty miles to sit with a stranger during surgery because the stranger's family couldn't get there in time. The same smallness that makes a place suffocating also makes it capable of that kind of attention.

I am from a place that taught me to fix things myself because the nearest mechanic is twenty miles away. I know how to work without supervision because there often wasn't any. I learned patience from planting things that take months to show results, and I learned humility from weather that doesn't negotiate. These aren't virtues I can fully claim — they were simply what the situation required.

What I want from college is the honest collision of this background with everything outside it. I want to sit in a room full of people whose cultural languages are completely different from mine and find out what we can make from that friction. I want to bring the particular knowledge of a place like Millersfield — its rhythms, its stubbornness, its occasional breathtaking generosity — into conversations that haven't included it.

The combine always gets fixed eventually. My dad patches it together with whatever's available, curses at it, and drives it back into the field. That's not a metaphor I'm trying too hard to impose on my life. It's just what I know. You work with what you have, in the place you're from, and then you carry that with you when you go somewhere new.

The summer I turned fourteen, I helped my grandfather castrate pigs. This is not a detail I typically lead with, but it is the one that keeps returning to me when I think about who I am and where I come from.

Caney, Oklahoma has 212 people, one gas station that doubles as a post office, and roads that Google Maps still gets wrong. I grew up in the particular silence of flat land, where you can see a storm coming from forty miles away and have just enough time to feel small before it arrives. My classmates and I graduated eight to a class. We knew everyone's grandparents, their embarrassments, their acreage.

That morning with my grandfather, I held a bucket and tried not to flinch. He had hands that looked like roots — thick, unhurried, built for difficult work. He talked me through everything without drama, explaining each step the way he explained baling wire repairs and soil pH and why certain fields drained poorly. Knowledge passed not through books but through proximity and necessity. I grew up learning that way, absorbing information the way you absorb weather: you step outside and it happens to you.

What I did not understand then was how invisible this education would become once I moved beyond Caney. I joined the regional academic team my sophomore year and rode the bus two hours each way to compete against students from Tulsa. They referenced shows I had not seen, used words that seemed designed to signal membership in something, asked what extracurriculars I did with a particular emphasis that made me feel my answer would disappoint them. I had helped deliver calves. I had driven a tractor since I was eleven. I had spent hundreds of hours in a truck cab with my grandfather learning how to read weather and when to cut your losses on a crop. None of this seemed to count.

I started performing a kind of translation on myself. I softened where I was from, answered questions vaguely, laughed along when rural places appeared in jokes as punchlines. I was ashamed of the very fluency that made me capable of moving between worlds, because I thought that fluency required abandoning one of them.

The change came slowly. Junior year, I wrote a history essay about agricultural consolidation and its effects on rural communities. My teacher asked me to read it aloud. Afterward, a girl from Tulsa said she had never thought about where food came from that way before. Not condescendingly — genuinely. She meant it as the beginning of a conversation, not the end of one.

I realized something that afternoon. The things I knew were not lesser knowledge — they were specific knowledge, the kind that comes from living inside a subject rather than reading about it at a comfortable distance. I understood land not as metaphor but as responsibility. I understood community not as concept but as the actual experience of depending on the same twelve families through drought years and hard winters. These were not gaps in my education. They were the education.

I will arrive at college carrying Caney with me the way you carry an accent — not always noticeable to myself, but present in how I think, what I notice, what questions I think to ask. I want to study environmental science because I have watched fields fail and recover. I want to understand systems because I grew up inside one small enough to see whole.

My grandfather has never heard the word interdisciplinary. But he taught me that everything connects to everything else — soil to weather, water to yield, one family's decision to the whole town's fate. That is the kind of thinking I want to bring to a larger world.

I held the bucket. I did not flinch.

The combine harvester breaks down every August without fail. My dad calls it Old Reliable, which is a joke, obviously — the machine hasn't been reliable since the Clinton administration. But every August, I'm the one wedged underneath it with a wrench, handing tools up to my uncle while gnats swarm my face and the Missouri heat presses down like something with weight and intention. This is how I learned what I am before I had language for it.

Orland, Missouri has 847 people, a single stoplight that blinks yellow after nine p.m., and a Dollar General that everyone calls "the store" because it's the only one. I grew up knowing my neighbors' middle names and their grandparents' middle names and the specific sound each of their trucks made coming down County Road 14. That intimacy — the kind where everyone knows when your family's struggling because they've noticed your dad's truck hasn't moved in three days — used to feel like suffocation. Now I recognize it as something more complicated.

My cultural identity is agricultural and Ozark and stubborn in ways that resist easy celebration. We aren't a community that appears in diversity statements or cultural festivals. We're the people academics study with a kind of sympathetic bewilderment, the subjects of trend pieces about "rural America." I've read a few of those articles. They always get something slightly wrong — the condescension hidden inside the compassion.

What those articles miss is the texture of it. The way my grandmother preserves green beans in jars lined up on shelves in her basement, and how that pantry has a specific smell of vinegar and dust and patience. The way my AP English teacher, Mrs. Callahan, drove forty minutes each way to teach at our school because, she said, "someone has to." The way people show up when someone dies — not with words, particularly, but with food and with presence and with the willingness to sit in a room and not require anything from you.

I've also had to reckon with what my community gets wrong. The reflexive suspicion of anything unfamiliar. The conversations at family dinners where I've had to decide, again and again, whether to push back or let it go. Being from a place means inheriting its failures alongside its strengths, and I've spent considerable energy sorting through what I want to carry forward and what I need to consciously put down.

I started a geography club at my school freshman year because I wanted to understand the larger world my town exists inside. We had seven members, including Mrs. Callahan, who joined unofficially. We spread maps across a pushed-together table and argued about borders and climates and why certain cities grew where they did. Those meetings felt like something opening — not an escape from where I'm from, but a way of locating it more accurately.

Because here's what I've understood, underneath that combine in the August heat: identity isn't a single inheritance. It's the accumulation of specific knowledge — how to read weather by the way cattle bunch together in a field, how silence between people can mean comfort or distance depending on context, how stubbornness can be either a wall or a foundation. I carry Orland with me not as limitation but as a particular lens, one ground from specific experience rather than abstraction.

When I arrive somewhere new, I won't have the cultural references everyone else shares. I'll probably mispronounce something. But I'll know how to work when the tools fail, how to sit with people in difficult rooms, and how to find the detail that everyone else's narrative is leaving out. Those are the skills my 847-person town gave me, and I intend to use them.

Growing up in a multicultural household has been one of the most defining aspects of my identity, shaping my perspectives and understanding of the world. My father is from India, and my mother is from the Midwest of the United States. This intersection of cultures has provided me with a rich tapestry of traditions, values, and experiences that have shaped who I am today.

In our home, the scent of spices from my father’s Indian cooking often mingles with the comforting aroma of my mother’s American recipes. These meals represent more than just nourishment; they are a symbol of the blending of two worlds. Each dish tells a story, one seasoned with the vibrant flavors of Indian cuisine and the other with the hearty warmth of Midwestern comfort food. Through these meals, I have come to appreciate the diversity of flavors that life has to offer and the importance of coming together around a table, regardless of our differences.

Language has also played a significant role in my exploration of identity. While English is my primary language, I grew up hearing Hindi spoken by my father on phone calls with family back in India. Although not fluent, I have always been drawn to the rhythm and melody of Hindi, which connects me to a heritage that feels both familiar and foreign. This exposure has fueled my interest in languages as gateways to understanding different cultures and perspectives. It has taught me the power of words not just as a means of communication but as a bridge to deeper empathy and connection.

Celebrating cultural holidays has been another way I have navigated my dual identity. Diwali and Thanksgiving, for example, are both eagerly anticipated in my household. During Diwali, the house is adorned with diyas, and we celebrate with sweets and fireworks, marking the triumph of light over darkness. A few weeks later, our family gathers for Thanksgiving, sharing gratitude over a feast. These celebrations have taught me the value of honoring diverse traditions and finding joy in both Indian and American festivities.

However, straddling two cultures has not always been easy. There were times in school when I felt like an outsider, not fully fitting into any one group. I grappled with questions of where I belonged and who I was supposed to be. But these experiences eventually taught me to embrace the uniqueness of my multicultural identity. I learned that identity does not require conforming to a single narrative but rather celebrating the mosaic of experiences that make me who I am.

Through this journey, I have developed a profound respect for the various cultures and histories that contribute to the tapestry of humanity. It has made me acutely aware of the importance of inclusivity and representation, ensuring that all voices are heard and valued. I strive to be an advocate for diversity, seeking opportunities to learn from those who are different from me and contribute to a community that celebrates multiplicity.

In embracing my heritage, I have discovered that identity is not a static concept. It is constantly evolving, shaped by experiences, relationships, and personal growth. I am a product of the histories that precede me, yet I am also carving my own path, informed by but not limited to those influences. My multicultural background has instilled in me a curiosity to explore and a willingness to engage with the unfamiliar.

As I look toward the future, I am excited about the possibility of attending a college where I can continue to explore these themes of identity and culture. I hope to join a community that values diversity in all its forms and encourages students to embrace their unique backgrounds. I am eager to contribute my perspectives and learn from others, continuing to expand my understanding of the world and my place within it.

Through my multicultural upbringing, I have learned that identity is a journey. It is about finding harmony in the intersection of influences and creating a narrative that is uniquely my own. I am proud of my heritage and ready to carry it forward, weaving new experiences into the fabric of who I am.

Growing up in a household that bridged two cultures has been both a challenging and enriching experience. My parents immigrated to the United States from India, bringing with them a rich tapestry of traditions that have colored every aspect of my upbringing. At home, we spoke both English and Hindi, watched Bollywood films, and celebrated festivals like Diwali with great enthusiasm. However, outside my home, I encountered a world that was vastly different. This dynamic has profoundly shaped my identity, offering both unique opportunities and obstacles as I navigated between these two worlds.

As a child, I often felt like I was living in two different worlds. At school, I was surrounded by friends from diverse backgrounds, and I learned to adapt quickly to a variety of cultural contexts. However, there were moments when I felt out of place, like when they referenced shows or toys I wasn't familiar with, or when lunchtime conversations turned to foods I didn’t eat at home. Although these experiences were initially isolating, they also sparked my curiosity and a desire to understand different perspectives.

My family placed a strong emphasis on education, viewing it as a means to success and a way to honor the sacrifices of previous generations. This belief in the power of education was instilled in me at a young age. Yet, it wasn't just about achieving high grades; it was about embracing the thirst for knowledge and understanding. I was encouraged to ask questions and explore topics that piqued my interest, whether they were related to science, history, or the arts. This mindset helped me thrive academically and cultivated a love for learning that I carry with me to this day.

Celebrating traditional Indian festivals also played a significant role in shaping my identity. Diwali, the festival of lights, was a time of joy and reflection. My family would come together to light lamps, cook festive meals, and exchange gifts. These celebrations were opportunities to connect with my cultural roots and to learn about the values and stories that underpin these traditions. Through these experiences, I have developed a deep appreciation for the values of family, community, and gratitude.

Simultaneously, growing up in the United States exposed me to ideas and experiences that broadened my perspective. I was part of a diverse community where I learned to appreciate multiculturalism and the beauty of differences. This exposure taught me to respect and celebrate diversity, fostering an inclusive mindset that I aim to carry forward in all aspects of my life. Participating in various cultural events and clubs at school showed me the importance of dialogue and understanding among different communities.

The intersection of my Indian heritage and American upbringing has instilled in me a sense of adaptability and empathy. Balancing two cultures has taught me to navigate differences with grace and to see challenges as opportunities for growth. It has also enriched my worldview, allowing me to blend the best aspects of both worlds into my daily life. This duality has not only made me more resilient but also equipped me with the tools to connect with people from various backgrounds.

As I look toward the future and the possibility of attending college, I am eager to explore more about who I am and how my background can contribute to the community. I am interested in joining multicultural organizations or cultural studies programs, where I can engage in conversations about identity and diversity. At the same time, I am keen to share my experiences and learn from others, continuing to build bridges between different cultures.

In conclusion, my identity as a first-generation Indian-American has been shaped by the rich blend of my cultural heritage and the diverse environment in which I grew up. This journey has taught me important values of resilience, adaptability, and empathy, equipping me to embrace the complexity of modern identity. As I prepare for the next chapter of my life, I am excited to bring these experiences with me, continuing to learn and grow in a world that is increasingly interconnected.

Growing up in a multicultural household has been the cornerstone of my identity, shaping how I view the world and my place within it. My father hails from a small town in Mexico, while my mother comes from the bustling city of Manila in the Philippines. This unique blend of cultures has exposed me to a rich tapestry of traditions, languages, and values that have profoundly influenced who I am today.

From a young age, I was immersed in a dual world. At home, the aroma of tamales mingled with the scent of adobo, creating a culinary experience that was both vibrant and comforting. These family meals were more than just food; they were a symbol of my dual heritage and the love that connects us. Sharing these meals taught me the importance of family bonds and the joy that comes from embracing one's roots.

Language played a crucial role in bridging my two worlds. Conversations at our dinner table often shifted seamlessly between Spanish, Tagalog, and English. By navigating these languages, I became adept at understanding and appreciating the nuances of different cultures. This skill has translated into an ability to communicate effectively with diverse groups, allowing me to form connections with people from various backgrounds.

Celebrating cultural holidays has been another key factor in shaping my identity. Every year, my family commemorates Dia de los Muertos and Santacruzan with equal enthusiasm. These celebrations are vibrant expressions of our cultural heritage, and participating in them has instilled in me a deep respect for tradition and the communities they bring together. Through these festivities, I have learned the importance of honoring the past while eagerly looking toward the future.

Beyond traditions and language, the values of both cultures have left a lasting imprint on me. My Mexican heritage has taught me the significance of resilience and determination, as I have often heard stories of my father's journey to build a life in a new country. From my Filipino side, I have learned the value of hospitality and generosity, witnessing my mother's unwavering kindness and her ability to make everyone feel welcome. These values serve as guiding principles in my life, motivating me to work hard and treat others with empathy and respect.

As I look toward my future, I embrace my multicultural background as a source of strength and inspiration. It has equipped me with a unique perspective that I believe will contribute positively to any college campus. My upbringing has cultivated a strong sense of adaptability, allowing me to thrive in diverse environments and approach challenges with an open mind. Additionally, my cultural heritage has fueled my passion for promoting inclusivity and celebrating diversity in all its forms.

In high school, I have taken steps to share and expand my cultural identity by participating in activities that foster cross-cultural understanding. As a member of the cultural awareness club, I have helped organize events that highlight and celebrate different traditions, encouraging my peers to appreciate the beauty of cultural diversity. These experiences have reinforced my belief in the power of education and dialogue to bridge differences and cultivate empathy.

In conclusion, my identity as the child of Mexican and Filipino parents is integral to who I am. It has provided me with a rich cultural heritage that serves as both a foundation and a compass for my life. Embracing my multicultural background has taught me to value diversity, cherish tradition, and approach life with resilience and empathy. As I prepare for the next chapter of my life, I am excited to bring my unique perspective to a college community, eager to learn from others and make a meaningful contribution to our shared world.

Growing up in a multicultural household has been the cornerstone of my identity, shaping my worldview and influencing how I navigate the world. My mother hails from a bustling city in South Korea, while my father is a native of a small Midwestern town in the United States. This unique blend of cultures has not been without its challenges, but it has imbued me with a deep understanding of diversity, resilience, and adaptability.

In our home, Korean was often spoken beside English, creating a harmonious symphony of languages. My mother would share stories of her childhood in Seoul, painting vivid pictures of vibrant festivals and the camaraderie of her tight-knit family. Meanwhile, my father’s tales recount life in the American countryside, emphasizing traditions like Thanksgiving and community spirit. These narratives have intertwined to form my own story.

Our meals were a testament to this cultural fusion. Kimchi could be found next to mashed potatoes at dinner, a reminder that embracing both cultures is as natural as breathing. This dining table taught me to appreciate variety and the richness it brings to life. It was at these meals that I learned to listen, to ask questions, and to understand perspectives different from my own.

Cultural celebrations have also played a significant role in shaping who I am. Lunar New Year is a significant event in our household. Dressing in hanbok, we partake in rituals to honor our ancestors. These practices connect me to my Korean roots, offering a sense of belonging and continuity. Conversely, celebrating the Fourth of July with my father’s side of the family grounds me in American traditions. These experiences instill a sense of pride and appreciation for both cultures.

Navigating these dual identities has instilled in me a profound capability for empathy. I’ve learned to recognize and respect the nuances in other people's backgrounds, knowing that everyone carries their own complex tapestry of experiences. These lessons have extended beyond my home, into classrooms and social circles, where I find myself often bridging gaps between differing viewpoints.

However, balancing two cultures has not always been easy. There were moments of feeling caught between worlds, developing a lingering question of belonging — a common theme in my adolescent musings. Yet, I’ve come to realize that my identity is not defined by a singular label, but by an array of experiences and influences. This epiphany has been liberating, allowing me to approach life with an open mind, unafraid of the richness that differences can bring.

In school, I’ve sought opportunities to further explore and share my cultural background. Through involvement in the multicultural club, I’ve organized events that celebrate various heritages, hoping to inspire others to embrace their own cultural identities. These activities have enriched my school community, promoting understanding and inclusivity.

Ultimately, my dual heritage has molded me into a person who values diversity in all its forms. It has taught me the importance of listening to the unheard voices and understanding the stories that might differ from my own. My cultural background is not merely a facet of who I am but is instrumental in shaping the decisions I make and the paths I choose.

As I stand on the cusp of the next chapter, ready to embrace the diverse experiences that college promises, I carry with me the lessons my multicultural upbringing has taught me. They have become the foundation upon which I will build my future, guiding me towards a life of understanding, bridge-building, and acceptance.

When people ask where I am from, I usually pause before answering. If I just say “New Jersey,” it feels incomplete; if I begin to explain that my parents are from India, the conversation often turns into a geography lesson. Over time, I’ve realized that being both Indian and American is not about choosing one side. It’s about learning how two seemingly different worlds can exist together in a single person. My identity has been shaped by the vibrant colors, traditions, and stories my parents carried across the ocean, as well as the individualism and curiosity that surrounded me growing up here.

At home, I smell cumin and cardamom before I even open the kitchen door. My mother hums old Hindi songs while preparing dal and rice, and I sometimes find myself quietly singing along. Yet outside, my friends and I eat pizza after football games and talk about college plans. For a while, I tried to separate the two worlds—Indian at home, American at school—but that division felt unnatural. Slowly, I began to see how they could coexist. When my classmates visited for Diwali, they loved the sparkling lights and sweet desserts; when my cousins from India came here, they joined me for barbecues and Fourth of July fireworks. Each experience made my world a little larger.

Language was the first bridge I built. My grandmother speaks mostly Hindi, so I started learning enough to hold a real conversation. At the same time, she began learning English phrases from me. Over the phone, we mix both languages freely, laughing at our mistakes. That exchange taught me that communication is not just about words but about effort and care. It reminded me that understanding someone else’s world often starts with listening, even when it’s uncomfortable or unfamiliar.

My identity also shapes how I face challenges. When I joined my school’s debate team, I was initially quiet, worried that my slight accent on certain words might draw attention. But my coach encouraged me to use that voice to share perspectives others might not consider. In one tournament about global education, I spoke about how my parents’ journey from modest beginnings to stable lives in America reflected the power of opportunity. That experience gave me confidence that my story—and by extension, my identity—had something valuable to contribute.

Being part of two cultures has also made me more open-minded. I have learned that customs are not barriers but invitations to learn. I now celebrate both Thanksgiving and Diwali with equal enthusiasm, finding meaning in gratitude and light. I appreciate how traditions evolve and adapt, just as I have. My parents taught me to respect my roots, but they also encouraged me to pave my own path. Through them, I learned that identity is not fixed; it grows with each new experience.

Today, I no longer hesitate when someone asks where I’m from. I say, “I’m from New Jersey, but my family is from India,” with pride instead of uncertainty. Those words are a reflection of who I am: someone shaped by two cultures, constantly weaving them into a single story. That story continues to evolve, pushing me to learn, connect, and find common ground wherever I go. My identity is both a foundation and an invitation—to carry my heritage forward while embracing the world around me.

When people ask me where I’m from, I usually hesitate before answering. I was born in New Jersey, but my parents immigrated from Vietnam, and at different times in my life, I’ve felt like I belonged more to one world than the other. Growing up, I was surrounded by both English and Vietnamese—one at school and one at home. My parents spoke Vietnamese to me, but I often answered in English, afraid that my accent would sound strange or childish. Over time, that small hesitation became a symbol of a larger tension within me: the desire to fit in and the fear of losing the language that connected me to my family.  

For most of elementary school, I tried to hide the parts of myself that felt “too different.” I begged my mom not to pack traditional lunches because I dreaded explaining the unfamiliar smells to classmates. I wanted sandwiches and chips, not rice and fried fish. Looking back, I realize that this desire to blend in came from a misunderstanding. I believed that being American meant being like everyone else, not realizing that everyone around me was balancing their own definitions of identity.  

As I grew older, my perspective began to shift. The turning point came during a family trip to Vietnam when I was fourteen. I had visited before, but this time I was old enough to notice more than just the sights and sounds. I noticed how my relatives spoke with a sense of comfort and belonging that I envied. Though my Vietnamese vocabulary faltered, the rhythm of the language felt familiar, like music I used to know by heart but had forgotten the lyrics to. That trip made me realize that my identity didn’t have to be split in half. I could be both fully Vietnamese and fully American; nothing required me to choose.

When I returned home, I started asking my parents more questions about their lives before coming to the United States. I learned how my father left Vietnam after the war and worked multiple jobs just to learn English. I learned how my mother helped her younger siblings adjust to a new country while still finishing high school herself. Hearing their stories filled me with admiration and a sense of responsibility. I began to see my cultural background not as a burden to carry, but as a story to honor.

At school, this new understanding changed the way I interacted with others. I joined the Asian Student Association and started teaching myself to read and write better in Vietnamese. Instead of avoiding questions about my culture, I welcomed them. During a heritage celebration, I volunteered to perform a traditional fan dance with a group of friends. Standing on stage in a silk costume, I felt nervous, but mostly proud. For once, I wasn’t trying to hide who I was; I was representing something I loved.

Through these experiences, I’ve discovered that identity isn’t fixed—it’s a process of learning, unlearning, and rediscovery. My cultural background has taught me empathy, resilience, and curiosity. It has shown me the value of listening to stories different from my own and celebrating the ones that shaped me. Being Vietnamese American means moving between two worlds and learning how they can strengthen each other.  

Today, when someone asks where I’m from, I answer without hesitation. I say I was born in New Jersey, but my family is from Vietnam. I speak English most of the time, but I’m still learning Vietnamese. I celebrate Thanksgiving and Lunar New Year with equal enthusiasm. My identity is not a conflict to resolve, but a mosaic to appreciate—one piece made bright by the other.

When I was younger, I never thought much about what it meant to have two cultures living inside one person. At home, my family spoke a mix of Spanish and English that flowed naturally, like a song with two melodies joining together. At school, however, I often chose only one voice, careful not to sound too different when my friends spoke about weekend trips or favorite TV shows. For a long time, I treated my background like separate rooms in a house, closing one door when I stepped into another. Only later did I realize that both doors could stay open and that the two spaces belonged to the same home.

My parents immigrated from Mexico before I was born, carrying a few suitcases, a handful of photos, and a determination that I now find inspiring. They talked about how hard it was to build a new life, but their voices always held notes of hope. As a child, I watched my mother work late while studying English, and I watched my father learn to fix whatever broke because there was no one else to call. Their perseverance shaped the rhythm of our household, teaching me that cultural identity is not just food or language—it is also the attitude you bring to every challenge.

Still, I sometimes felt caught between worlds. My classmates were kind, but they didn’t always understand why my family celebrated certain holidays or why our house smelled like chiles roasting on a Sunday afternoon. When someone made a joke about my lunch being “too spicy,” I laughed along, but secretly, I wondered if I needed to tone down more than just flavor. I began to see my cultural background as something that could set me apart in uncomfortable ways. It took time, and a few key experiences, to shift my perspective.

The first turning point came during a heritage festival in middle school. My mother convinced me to help her cook tamales for a booth representing Mexico. As I handed out samples and explained our traditions, I watched people’s curiosity replace hesitation. They asked questions about the ingredients and the stories behind our food. I realized that my background could be a bridge instead of a barrier, and that sharing it could lead to genuine understanding. For the first time, I felt proud instead of nervous to represent both sides of who I was.

In high school, this awareness deepened as I began volunteering as a tutor for students whose families had recently moved from other countries. They reminded me of my parents at the beginning of their journey—full of potential but unsure of where to start. When I translated assignments or explained school routines, I wasn’t just helping with words; I was helping them find confidence. Each session made me more grateful for the path my family had taken and for the possibility that I could be part of someone else’s transition.

Over time, I have come to see that my cultural identity is less about choosing between English or Spanish, tacos or pizza nights, Mexico or America, and more about blending these elements into one continuous story. It shows up in how I treat others, how I value hard work, and how I find beauty in both familiarity and difference. It gives me perspective when discussing world issues in class, because I instinctively think about more than one side of a situation. It gives me purpose when I consider my future, because I want to create spaces where everyone feels understood and seen.

As I look ahead to college, I no longer want to close any doors. I hope to bring my bilingual background, my appreciation for my parents’ journey, and my desire to connect with others from all walks of life. My identity is a combination of countless small details—stories told at our kitchen table, family traditions, and lessons learned from moving between cultures. Living between two worlds has taught me that belonging is not about finding a perfect fit but about embracing every part of who you are and inviting others to do the same.

Growing up in a bilingual household has shaped every part of who I am. My parents moved from Mexico to the United States before I was born, carrying with them Spanish words, family recipes, and traditions that filled our home with color and music. Outside our front door, though, the rhythm changed. At school and with friends, English was the only language that mattered. For a long time, I thought I had to choose between the two worlds, but I have come to realize that both belong to me equally. My identity is not split; it is doubled.

When I was little, I felt embarrassed when my mom spoke Spanish in public. I worried that people would stare or think we didn’t belong. I remember grocery store trips where I would whisper translations to her, hoping no one would notice. At school, I avoided mentioning that I spoke another language. I wanted to fit in, to sound like everyone else, and to smooth away anything that made me seem different. What I did not see then was that I was also ignoring a part of myself.

The turning point came in middle school when my grandmother came to live with us. She spoke very little English, so I became the family translator. At first, it felt like a chore. But slowly, I started to see how my ability to move between two languages connected generations in my family. My grandmother would tell stories about her childhood, and I would interpret them for my younger siblings, who laughed and listened. Growing up in a household where two cultures constantly intersected, I learned early on that identity is not something you simply inherit. Growing up as the child of Filipino immigrants in a small Midwestern town meant I was always navigating between two worlds. For most of my childhood, I saw this duality as a problem to solve rather than a gift to embrace. I remember being embarrassed in third grade when my mother packed pancit in my lunchbox instead of the usual sandwich. A classmate pointed at the noodles and asked what that weird food was, and I felt my face burn. That afternoon, I told my mom I only wanted "normal" lunches from then on. She looked hurt but nodded quietly. Looking back, I realize that small moment represented something much larger: my desire to erase the parts of myself that felt different.

As I got older, this tension only grew. I participated in school activities and made friends easily enough, but I always felt like I was performing a version of myself that left out important pieces. At family gatherings on weekends, my relatives would tease me for my American accent when I tried to speak Tagalog. At school, classmates sometimes made jokes about Asian stereotypes that I laughed along with because I did not want to seem overly sensitive. I existed in a space where I felt not quite Filipino enough and not quite American enough, and for a long time, that in-between space felt lonely.

The turning point came during my sophomore year when my grandmother visited from the Philippines. She stayed with us for three months, and during that time, she told me stories I had never heard before. She talked about growing up in a rural province, about working in rice fields as a girl, and about the sacrifices she made to send my father to college so he could eventually build a better life abroad. She spoke with such pride and resilience that I felt something shift inside me. Her stories were not just family history. They were the foundation of everything I had.

Inspired by her visit, I started a cultural awareness club at my school. The club began small, with just a few friends willing to share their own backgrounds, but it grew into a space where students from many different cultures could talk openly about their traditions and experiences. I organized a multicultural night where families brought food from their home countries, and I gave a short presentation about the Philippines, complete with a traditional tinikling dance that my mom helped me practice. Standing in front of my classmates and sharing something so personal was terrifying, but the applause and genuine curiosity I received afterward made it worth every nervous moment.

Through this experience, I learned that identity is not something you have to shrink to make others comfortable. It is something you can share to make your community richer. I stopped seeing my background as a barrier and started seeing it as a bridge. I began cooking Filipino recipes for my friends, teaching them Tagalog phrases, and openly discussing what it means to grow up between two cultures.

I still live in that in-between space, but now I understand that it is not a weakness. It has given me the ability to connect with people from different backgrounds, to listen with empathy, and to appreciate the complexity of identity. I carry my grandmother's resilience, my parents' sacrifices, and my own experiences as someone who has learned to embrace every part of who I am. As I look toward college, I want to continue building bridges between cultures and communities, bringing the same openness and pride that I have finally learned to feel about my own story.

This understanding has made me more empathetic toward others who feel caught between worlds. I no longer hesitate when someone asks me where I come from. Instead, I welcome the question as an opportunity to share a story that is still unfolding, one that I am proud to continue writing with every choice I make.
