Sometimes I felt limited by my surroundings and wished I had access to more things. But I think those challenges also built character and made me more resilient as a person. Overcoming obstacles is how you grow.

Education was valued in my home even if it was not always the most emphasized thing in my wider community. My parents always told me that learning was important and that I should take it seriously. I listened to that advice and worked hard in school because I wanted to make them proud. I believe education is the pathway to a better future and I have always kept that in mind.

As I look forward to college, I feel ready to bring my background and experiences with me. I want to share my perspective as someone from a rural community with the people I will meet. I think diversity of experience is just as important as other kinds of diversity and I have something unique to contribute. My identity is rooted in the values and lessons of the place I grew up in.

I am proud of where I come from even though it is small and not very well known. My rural upbringing gave me values, perspective, and a strong foundation. I know that college will challenge me in new ways but I am prepared to face those challenges. My identity will always be connected to that small town and everything it taught me.

There’s a peculiar magic in the moments just before sunset, when the sky becomes a canvas in transition, and shadows stretch long and gentle across our backyard. These moments are where I find myself most at peace, camera in hand, capturing the way light wraps around and softens the edges of tree trunks and fence posts. Photography isn’t just a hobby. It’s my lens to the world, a world I hope to document in its myriad forms—from delicate evening glow to the hard angles of a bustling city street.

Growing up, I spent afternoons after school on the worn leather sofa in our living room, flipping through dusty National Geographic magazines that my mom collected since college. Those pages held wonders I could barely fathom: green-tinted underwater caves, the serene majesty of snow-capped mountains, the vibrant chaos of crowded bazaars. Each photograph told a story richer than any words, a mosaic of colors and cultures that captured not just images, but lives. I longed to create something that could make others stop and look—and not just look, but see.

This was the spark, but it was a quiet evening with my grandfather that truly ignited my passion. He sat with me at our kitchen table, sharing tales of his days as a journalist, roaming from city to city with his trusty Minolta. His stories were filled with the kind of details only a keen observer could note: the smell of rain in Kolkata, the golden dust of the Serengeti at dawn. He gifted me that old Minolta on my fifteenth birthday, and with it, more than just a camera—a legacy.

That summer, I documented everything around me, learning to look deeper and see beauty in the mundane. I found it in the way rain pooled in our garden, in the stark lines of our school’s architecture, in people’s faces—every freckle and wrinkle a testament to their journey. I began to understand that photography was my way of connecting with the world, of preserving its fleeting beauty and echoing, in some small way, those magazine images that first captivated me.

However, it wasn’t until I volunteered with a community youth program that I realized the true potential of my passion. We were tasked with working on a project highlighting neighborhood stories and, naturally, I turned to my camera. As I photographed and interviewed residents, I understood the power these images held: to bridge divides, to foster empathy, and to give voice to those who might otherwise remain unheard. 

There was Maria, the baker whose hands never rested, flour dusting her apron like snow. And Jerome, who painted murals that danced across alley walls, his artworks a tapestry of resilience. Through their portraits, I didn’t just capture faces; I captured narratives, shared connections, moments of laughter, and tears of remembered pain. Each click of the shutter felt significant, a step toward a broader goal: to become a photojournalist, telling stories that matter through an honest and empathetic lens.

My journey thus far has taught me the critical importance of visual storytelling. I have felt its impact both in the moments I capture and in the moments shared by those who view my work. It’s a reminder that in a rapidly changing world, understanding and compassion are as vital as air—and that through photography, I can contribute to that understanding, one frame at a time.

The magic of those sunset moments in my backyard remains my anchor, a reminder of where this dream began. As I stand on the cusp of college and beyond, I carry with me the hope to illuminate stories yet untold, to preserve the ephemeral beauty of life, and ultimately, to inspire others to see, through my lens, the world anew.

As I squatted by the edge of the creek behind our home, I watched a water strider skate elegantly across the surface. Its effortless maneuvering ignited within me a deep curiosity that transformed the murky, serpent-infested water into a realm of discovery. I never imagined then that these encounters would form the foundation of my aspiration to become a marine biologist.

My fascination with aquatic life began with simple observations. Each time I bent over the creek, I felt like a scientist about to make an important discovery. My first makeshift experiment involved a jar and a selection of leaves to see what kind of creatures I could attract. I recall the thrill of spotting my first tiny fish wriggling amongst the foliage, its shimmering scales flashing silver in the sun. This was more enticing than any picture book.

The real turning point came when I visited the Monterey Bay Aquarium on a family trip to California. Stepping into that luminous, blue-lit dome felt like walking into a living painting. I was spellbound by the sheer variety of life: sea turtles that glided like ancient sentinels, captivations of jellyfish drifting like celestial orbs. Most awe-inspiring were the coral reefs, a riot of colors and life forms, as mesmerizing as any universe. And, of course, the resident octopus, its curious eyes hinting at intelligence greater than I'd ever encountered.

Throughout school, science was where I felt most at home. My biology teacher, Mr. Robinson, further fueled my burgeoning love for the underwater world. He had an old map of the world's oceans on his classroom wall and could make each corner feel like a vivid, undiscovered country. His passion was infectious; he always delivered more than a lesson—he shared a vision, one that encouraged us to seek the natural wonders beyond our textbooks. It was during one of his classes, while examining starfish under magnifying glasses, that I realized the depth of my desire to study marine ecosystems.

During high school, I volunteered at a local environmental cleanup program. Tugging on my rubber gloves, I would haul trash from suspiciously bubbling ponds or precarious riverbanks, leaving each water body a shade cleaner than before. The work was backbreaking but supremely fulfilling; with every paper wrapper pulled from the muck, I was reminded of my purpose. Here was my chance to protect those very creatures that once inspired me. The satisfaction of seeing a pond slowly return to life, creatures cautiously reclaiming their space, was the affirmation I needed.

Personal endeavor aside, my grandfather's stories also played a pivotal role in shaping my career aspirations. A fisherman before retiring, he told tales of the sea in ways that painted it not only as a passage for boats but as a complex, living kingdom. His reverence for the ocean, coupled with his knowledge of its necessity to human life, left an indelible mark on me. His weathered skin and bright, sea-worn eyes spoke to a life deeply tied to the currents, tides, and underwater realms I yearn to understand and conserve.

My hope is not only to become a marine biologist but to advocate for the preservation of marine life. The ocean is both vast and vulnerable, its ecosystems breathtaking yet fragile. I envision a future where my research contributes to sustainable practices, ensuring the balance of these incredible habitats. Imagining myself joining the ranks of the scientists I admire, like Sylvia Earle or Jaques Cousteau, is exhilarating.

Every splash in the creek, each visit to the aquarium, and all those classroom dreams have knit themselves into this hope. The streams I explored as a child may lead to larger waters, the fish in jars to whole schools in oceans. I aim not just to uncover the mysteries that lie beneath the surface but also to ignite the same awe and responsibility in future generations. With this, my aspirations becomes more than personal; they are my contribution to a world that continues to captivate and sustain us.

The pungent aroma of freshly ground spices enveloped me as I stood beside my grandmother, Nani, in her small, vibrant kitchen. It was a sacred space where cardamom and cinnamon sticks whispered secrets of our ancestry. My fingers tingled with warmth as I sprinkled garam masala into the simmering pot of curry—a recipe passed down through generations. Cooking with Nani was not just about nourishment; it was a conversation with my heritage. In those moments, I felt a profound connection to the intricate tapestry of Indian culture and history, a bond that has distinctly shaped my aspiration to become a cultural anthropologist.

Nani’s stories, intricately woven with her spices, transported me to her childhood in a bustling Delhi neighborhood. The way she recounted festival celebrations or simple market days compelled me to visualize and explore a culture far richer than any textbook could encapsulate. Her tales kindled a desire to delve deeper into the customs and traditions not only of my own background but of diverse cultures worldwide. I began to understand the essential role that food, language, and rituals play in defining the identity of a community, sparking my curiosity for cultural anthropology.

Throughout high school, my fascination with human stories found an outlet in the multicultural club, where I eagerly participated in organizing cultural fairs and heritage nights. Each event was an opportunity to glimpse the world through another’s eyes. I remember the vivid colors and lively beats of our Diwali dance performance, which bridged the gap between my Indian roots and my American reality. It was during these occasions I realized the shared human experiences that transcend geographical boundaries, reinforcing my aspiration to study the threads that connect us all.

A summer volunteer program at the local immigrant center further fueled my passion. There, I assisted a passionate group of diverse individuals striving to make a new life in America. We navigated language barriers and shared laughter over simple things, like my quirky accent attempts. Listening to their stories, I gained insights into the profound courage and adaptability it takes to blend old traditions with new beginnings. My role was ostensibly about aiding their integration, yet what I received was far greater—a lesson in resilience and the joy of cultural exchange. This experience solidified my intention to study these dynamics professionally.

The pursuit of cultural anthropology feels like a natural extension of my life's narrative. It combines my love for storytelling, exploration, and understanding the complex ethos of different communities. My hope is to conduct fieldwork that allows me to capture the vibrant spectrum of human traditions and publicize these findings in a manner that fosters cross-cultural connections. I envision leveraging modern technologies to present these narratives in innovative ways, perhaps through interactive museum exhibits or virtual reality experiences that transport audiences directly into another culture.

My journey is still unfolding, but the path is clearer with each step. The collective experiences with Nani in her kitchen, my vibrant high school community, and the real-world lessons from my summer at the immigrant center have all shaped my ambition. They have taught me the value of embracing diversity, questioning assumptions, and celebrating the myriad ways in which humanity expresses itself. I aspire to contribute to a world where cultural understanding and appreciation are as essential as nourishment, each feeding the soul in a unique way.

As I stand at the cusp of this next chapter, the legacy imparted by Nani and fueled by each new experience beckons me forward. My greatest hope is not only to become a cultural anthropologist but to be a conduit through which stories of the overlooked and misunderstood are told, fostering empathy and connection across all borders. And while I am still learning, just as the spices blend harmoniously in Nani's pot, so too might I find my place in the grand, flavorful mosaic of human history.

In the lesser-known corner of our bustling suburban neighborhood, Mrs. Martinez cultivates a small garden that she said was just for “a bit of solace.” At age twelve, with nothing but an insistent curiosity, I found myself drawn into her world of verdant wonder. Every Saturday morning, while most of my classmates groaned through drab chores, I darted to her house to plunge my eager hands into the rich, loamy soil that clung reassuringly to my fingers. 

Mrs. Martinez’s garden was a tapestry of life, blending scents that danced eternally: the potent, sweet allure of blooming lavender, the peppery zest of fresh basil, and somewhere beneath it all, the heady, calming aroma of moist earth after a night’s gentle rain. One morning, as we crouched next to tomato plants heavy with promise, Mrs. Martinez shared a slice of ripe fruit, still sun-warm. The taste unraveled on my tongue—sweet, tangy, with a playful hint of summer’s inevitable embrace—and with it, a revelation: I wanted to harness this alchemy, to embrace the connection between the land and the sustenance it provided.

It was here, amidst the unruly charm of that petite Eden, that my hope took root: to become a sustainable agriculture expert. I desired to forge a life that balanced human needs with nature’s rhythms, nurturing both the land and the community it feeds.

The following summer, I convinced my parents to transform a neglected corner of our backyard into a miniature test field. Armed with books from the library and advice from Mrs. Martinez, I planted an array of heirloom vegetables. If the garden flourished, we would donate the produce to the local food bank. It wasn’t all smooth sailing; there were invasions of aphids, wilted leaves from overenthusiastic watering, and persistent squirrels with an apparent affinity for my hard-won veggies. But with each challenge came a lesson in resilience and innovation.

One scorching July afternoon, as I surveyed rows of thriving kale and swelling squash, I marveled at the tangible outcome of my efforts: not just edible greens, but the hope they represented. After delivering our bounty to the food bank, the gratitude in the coordinator’s eyes mirrored the sense of purpose that bloomed within me. Growing food wasn’t just about filling plates—it was about forging connections and empowering communities.

During high school, I sought opportunities to deepen my understanding of sustainable agriculture. I joined the environmental science club and interned at an urban farm downtown. My supervisor, Joel, was an agronomist with a fervor for soil health that rivaled Mrs. Martinez’s passion. He taught me about crop rotation, the benefits of compost, and companion planting—a botanical symbiosis that mirrored human cooperation. Together, we sifted through the soil, discussing not merely how to nurture plants, but how to nurture a world that sustains us all.

As I watched bees flit between sunflowers and marigolds, endless cycles of renewal and growth became metaphors for how I viewed my future. I envision myself advocating for policies that promote biodiversity, conducting research to refine agronomic practices, and working hands-on in the fields to cultivate food systems that are as just as they are fruitful.

Mrs. Martinez’s garden was more than a refuge; it was my awakening. The aromas, textures, and flavors of those Saturday mornings have woven themselves into my aspirations. Through sustainable agriculture, I hope to honor the land's generosity and share it with those around me. I dream of a world where gardens, big and small, can flourish, ushering in a future that harmonizes humanity with the earth we tread upon.

These seeds of hope were sown with Mrs. Martinez’s guidance and nurtured by my persistent hands. They've emboldened me to cultivate not just crops—but a field of dreams.

When I was eleven, I built a telescope out of PVC pipe, a magnifying lens from a broken pair of binoculars, and—regrettably—a little duct tape. I had seen a tutorial online, and I was convinced I’d be photographing Saturn by midnight. Instead, I got a blur so fuzzy it could have been a streetlight or an alien encounter. Still, I remember lying on the driveway afterward, the asphalt cooling under my back, and feeling a kind of electric wanting. I didn’t know for what, exactly, but it had to do with seeing further than my eyes let me.

That half-working telescope somehow turned into a string of experiments on my bedroom floor. I built a paper model of the solar system that collapsed under the weight of Jupiter (cardboard is not a structural engineer’s friend). I took apart my mom’s kitchen scale to “measure gravity.” I wasn’t very good at any of it. What I was good at was staying up too late sketching diagrams and trying to name what I felt when I watched the night sky. Wonder sounds too simple. It was more like curiosity braided with stubbornness and awe: a hunger to understand what holds things up, what sets them spinning.

My middle school science teacher, Mrs. Hsieh, noticed that I turned in lab reports with three times the required length but half the correct answers. She taught me that science wasn’t about being right on the first try; it was about asking sharper questions after you were wrong. That idea hit me like a door opening. Failure, reframed, wasn’t an ending but an instrument. I started bringing in my botched projects—the time I tried to power a flashlight from lemon batteries, for instance—and she’d make the class dissect my mistakes. That’s when I began to think maybe I could do this for real someday.

High school solidified it. I joined the robotics club because it was the closest thing we had to an engineering course. Our first robot, which we named Scrappy, was basically a zigzagging shopping cart. My wiring kept shorting out the motor, and when the judges asked what our design philosophy was, I said honestly, “trial and error.” But between the smell of solder, the soft hum of servos, and the way my teammates and I argued about code until the janitor kicked us out, I felt sure there was nothing more interesting than building something that moves and thinks because you made it.

Last summer, I interned at a small engineering lab that designed prosthetic hands. On my first day, the head engineer placed a 3D-printed hand on my desk and said, “Make it move.” I stared at it—the pale plastic knuckles, the jointed fingers—and felt the same wanting I’d had on my driveway years earlier. By the end of the week, with a lot of help, I got one finger to curl around a pen. Watching that small motion, I realized I didn’t just want to study engineering; I wanted to use it to restore motion, to solve the puzzles of how things—and people—function.

My hope now is to become a biomedical engineer who builds for real bodies, not just prototypes. I want to translate the imagination of that eleven-year-old stargazer into tools that matter right here on Earth. When I think about the future, I picture the intersection of curiosity and compassion: a workshop that smells of melted filament and coffee, a team clustered around a model hand that, this time, closes perfectly. I don’t expect it to be easy; it may be mostly failure at first, as most of my experiments have been. But if I’ve learned anything, it’s that the blur of uncertainty—the fuzzy blur that once hid Saturn—isn’t a problem to fix. It’s the place where discovery starts.

When I was ten, I asked my grandma why she talked to her tomato plants. She laughed and said, “Because they listen better than most people.” That summer, I’d sit beside her in the small square of garden behind her apartment, knees mottled with dirt, listening as she told me the difference between impatience and patience in the shape of a sprouting vine. Every time a tiny tomato ripened, she’d hold it up like a treasure. I didn’t know then that she’d grown up in a city where nothing green survived except the moss between cracked pavement. She wanted to fill her late years with life that depended on her gentleness.  

When she passed away during my freshman year, I stopped tending the garden. The soil hardened and the mint overtook everything. I didn’t see much point—plants die; people die. But one March afternoon, I found a small green shoot pushing up through the weeds. It wasn’t supposed to be there; the frost could still come any night. I crouched over it, holding my breath as if my attention alone could protect it. That stubborn sprout did not die. For reasons I couldn’t fully explain, neither did my desire to see things grow.  

I began studying biology with the same devotion Grandma had given to her plants. The first time I looked at a seed under a microscope, I realized I was looking at potential coiled into silence. Every cell seemed to hold a secret about durability. My teachers encouraged me to ask questions that didn’t have neat answers: How do plants sense light without eyes? How can a microorganism survive drought when a human can’t? Those questions felt like keys—tiny, unassuming, but capable of opening doors to resilience.  

That curiosity expanded when I joined the environmental club at school. We restored a neglected patch of land beside the football field—seven of us with mismatched gloves and more enthusiasm than skill. Clearing litter in ninety-degree heat was not glamorous, but we planted pollinator flowers and native grasses anyway. Weeks later, bees came. Then butterflies. I remember crouching down one afternoon, the air heavy with summer sweetness, and feeling a pulse of pride. We had made a place for life to return.  

Working in that soil again reminded me that hope isn’t passive. It’s work. It’s sweating through an afternoon even when you’d rather be in the air-conditioned library. It’s admitting that some seeds won’t sprout, and planting again anyway. That realization reshaped how I saw responsibility—to the environment, my community, and the future.  

I want to become an environmental scientist who studies urban ecosystems, working to bring green spaces into cities like the one my grandma grew up in. I’ve seen how a single square of garden can change the way people move through a neighborhood; they slow down, notice the lavender, linger in conversation. If science can help more neighborhoods feel like that—less concrete, more color—I want to be part of it.  

To prepare, I’ve thrown myself into research papers, documentaries, and experiments that sometimes end as failures. Once I tried composting in my bedroom using a shoebox and fruit scraps. My mom still teases me about “the great banana-peel incident,” when I discovered I’d mainly cultivated fruit flies. Yet even cleaning up that mess taught me something important about decomposition, about how breaking down can be its own form of creation.  

Science, like a garden, demands humility—experiments fail, seasons change—but it also rewards persistence. I think that’s why I keep returning to the soil. Each observation, each data point, is another reminder that small acts accumulate. A sprout appears. A bee lands. A vacant lot breathes again.  

Someday, I hope to be the kind of scientist who listens to the world the way my grandma did to her tomato plants—quietly, attentively, believing that what grows under our care can change what feels barren. And if I ever have a garden of my own, I’ll talk to my plants too, just to let them know someone’s listening.

When I was eight, my parents gave me a used microscope that still smelled faintly of the neighbor’s garage. The base was chipped, the knobs resisted turning, and the light flickered out if I breathed too hard. But under that stubborn lens, a drop of pond water turned into a small, teeming universe: darting specks, translucent loops, creatures that split or swallowed one another whole. I remember pressing my eye so close to the eyepiece that the rubber ring left a pale circle on my skin. The discovery felt electric—life was not a static picture in my textbook; it was restless, twitching, alive.  

That first thrill has tangled itself into my hope of becoming a microbiologist. It’s not the glamorous kind of science you see on posters—no roaring rockets or test-tube explosions—but the patience of it draws me. The act of staring down a microscope until vision blurs is, to me, an act of faith: faith that even something invisible can hold meaning.  

In middle school, I started an ill-fated “backyard lab” on our patio. My equipment consisted of old yogurt cups, coffee filters, and a borrowed pipette. The day I tried to culture bacteria from the kitchen sponge, my mom came outside, sniffed the air, and—after one look at the greenish fuzz—made me bleach everything. I was furious then, but later I photographed the surviving colonies on my phone and noticed, almost lovingly, how they resembled constellations. That mixture of frustration and fascination became familiar; I learned that curiosity often breeds chaos before clarity.  

In tenth grade biology, I met the scientist I want to become. Not an individual person, but a way of being: my teacher, Mr. Patel, handled every student question as if it were a hypothesis worth testing. When I asked why some antibiotics stop working, he didn’t just answer; he handed me an article about resistance patterns in hospitals and told me to explain it back to the class next week. For three nights I stared at the glowing screen, looking up terms like “efflux pump” and “plasmid transfer,” amazed that microscopic negotiations could decide whether someone recovered or not. By Friday, my classmates were half-asleep during my presentation, but I had never felt more alert.  

Last summer, I volunteered at a local clinic’s health fair. I was assigned to the children’s hand-washing booth—a humble post, but one perfectly suited to someone who once grew bacteria on purpose. Watching kids lather soap between their fingers (or, more often, forget to), I thought about how the invisible world I loved studying could also hurt people. I’d always imagined research as something done in quiet labs, but this was research’s moral twin: sharing what we know so others stay healthy. That realization made me see microbiology not just as a study of cells, but as a language of care.  

My parents, who emigrated from Vietnam, sometimes tilt their heads when I describe wanting to spend my life looking at “tiny bugs.” They value practical success: stable income, straightforward job titles. I understand their worry, but I also know that their journey—crossing oceans with little more than determination—shaped the same persistence I’ll need in research. Whenever an experiment fails, I think of my mom learning English by labeling every object in the house with sticky notes. Progress, for us, has always started small.  

What I hope to do, ultimately, is to stand in a lab where the hum of equipment feels as familiar as breathing. I want to trace how bacteria evolve, how genes travel, how resistance can be slowed. Maybe my work will end up as a line on a medical report, or buried in a journal hardly anyone reads. That’s all right. The beauty of microbiology lives in the uncelebrated details, the places no one usually looks.  

I still have that old microscope, now mostly ornamental on my windowsill. Its light no longer turns on, but sometimes, when the afternoon sun strikes it just right, the glass catches a flash that looks almost alive. I like to think it’s reminding me to keep looking closer.

I used to think I would become a doctor because that’s what my parents said “smart kids” did. Then, one Saturday morning during my sophomore year, my dad’s car wouldn’t start and he called me outside. The sky was that grim winter gray that makes everything feel half-frozen. He handed me a wrench and said, “Here, hold this.” What followed was three hours of me trying not to drop tools into unreachable engine spaces while he cursed at a corroded battery terminal. But when the car finally coughed awake, sputtering then humming steadily, I felt something like adrenaline mixed with wonder: we had turned frustration into movement.

That moment didn’t turn me into a mechanic, but it cracked open the way I looked at problems. Machines weren’t mysterious boxes to me anymore; they were puzzles, systems whose logic could be traced if you were willing to get your hands dirty. I began noticing how often I liked seeing *why* things worked the way they did. My math teacher had once complained that I asked too many “But why does it *that* work?” questions. Now I wore that curiosity as a badge.

When the pandemic hit and my classes moved online, my younger sister’s laptop began shutting off randomly. Instead of waiting for help, I spent hours watching repair videos, paused every two seconds to take notes, then carefully opened the back casing. I cleaned dust from the fan with a Q-tip, replaced the worn battery, and discovered that applying thermal paste is much harder than the tutorials make it look. When I finally powered it up and saw her screen glow steady blue, she hugged me so fast she smudged thermal paste across my cheek.

I realized then that what excites me most is not just fixing things but restoring people’s ability to *do* things. My sister used that laptop to finish her art portfolio. My dad used the car to make it to work on freezing mornings. The connection between their tools and their opportunities felt deeply human to me.

That’s what first led me toward electrical engineering. Last summer, I joined a volunteer group that refurbishes old laptops for low-income students. The first day, our workspace smelled of warm dust and ozone from the soldering irons. A mentor named Alex showed me how to trace a faulty power connection. We worked side by side at a long metal table lined with machines in various states of undress. I loved the quiet intensity of that room—the click of keyboards being tested, the snap of plastic casings reattaching, the low murmur of people tackling tiny problems together. Every time we sent off a working device, it felt like we were shipping out hope in a cardboard box.

What’s shaped my hope most, though, isn’t just success—it’s the unpredictability. Not every repair works. I’ve fried motherboards and misplaced screws that rolled away forever. But in each failure, there’s this stubborn urge to understand, to adjust, to try again. That persistence feels tied to something larger than machines. It’s how I want to live.

Someday, I hope to design technologies that make accessibility effortless—devices that don’t just function but invite everyone to use them. I imagine communities where reliable connectivity is a given, not a privilege. I see myself working on the circuitry behind that, still with the posture of the student standing beside a broken engine, listening closely before acting.

When I think back to that cold morning under the hood of my dad’s car, I can still smell the oil and the faint metallic tang of effort. I didn’t know then that I was starting to shape my future. I only knew that the engine had come back to life, and something inside me had, too.

At 5:42 every morning, my mother’s alarm goes off in the dark, and by 5:49 she is standing at the kitchen counter tying her apron strings with the same quick tug she uses to pull our whole day into motion. She leaves for the bakery before sunrise, carrying a thermos that smells like cinnamon and burned coffee. When I was younger, I thought adulthood meant never sitting down.

On Saturdays, when she could not find childcare, I went with her. The bakery was always warmer than outside, even in January, and the windows fogged so completely that the street disappeared. My job was small and serious: lining muffin tins with paper cups, sweeping flour off the floor, counting the jelly packets in the back room. The grown-ups spoke a fast mixture of English and Spanish over the industrial mixer, and I learned to read moods by sound—the slap of dough, the metal tray dropped too hard, the short laugh that meant someone was more tired than amused.

What I remember most is not the pastries but the customers. There was an elderly man who came every Tuesday for two conchas and stayed just long enough to tell my mother about his doctor’s appointment. A nurse from the clinic next door ordered six bagels every Friday and once cried quietly while waiting for her card to process. A construction worker paid in quarters and apologized for being short by eleven cents. My mother slid the bag across the counter anyway. “Next time,” she said, though we both knew there would not be a next time for the eleven cents.

I watched people arrive carrying invisible things. Worry. Pride. Embarrassment. Grief. Hunger. Some of them wanted bread, but many of them wanted to be seen without having to explain themselves. My mother never called this service. She called it paying attention.

At home, paying attention looked different. It meant noticing when my younger brother pushed peas around his plate instead of eating, because that usually meant he had been teased at school. It meant translating insurance letters for my parents and hearing the pause in my father’s voice before he asked, “What does deductible mean?” It meant learning that competence can be quiet, that care is often administrative, and that love is sometimes a person sitting at a table with a highlighter and a stack of forms.

These moments have shaped what I hope to become: a physician who treats patients with the same kind of exact, unspectacular attentiveness my mother gives to everyone who walks through the bakery door. I do not want medicine because I like the idea of prestige or because I think science is clean and heroic. I want it because the most important moments I have witnessed were intimate and ordinary: a hand resting on the counter a second longer than necessary, a question someone was ashamed to ask, relief arriving not as a miracle but as an explanation.

That hope became sharper last year when my father injured his back unloading delivery crates. In the emergency room, we waited under fluorescent lights that made everyone look equally exhausted. My father kept saying he was fine even while gripping the side of the chair. When the physician finally came in, she did not rush. She crouched so her eyes were level with his, asked where it hurt, then asked what kind of work he did, then asked if he had been sleeping. Something in him changed. He stopped performing toughness and answered honestly. She still prescribed medication and physical therapy, but what stayed with me was the order of her questions. She treated him not as a back with a person attached, but as a person whose back happened to be hurting.

I have spent years loving biology for its precision—the choreography of cells, the electrical logic of the heart—but I have also spent years in rooms where people needed language, patience, and dignity as much as they needed information. I hope to become the kind of doctor who can hold both truths at once. Someone who can read a chart closely and a face carefully. Someone who understands that healing is not only technical skill, but attention paid at the right moment, in the right tone, with enough respect that another person can finally exhale.

Every morning, my mother still ties her apron strings with that quick tug. Watching her, I no longer think adulthood means never sitting down. I think it means rising early to meet other people’s needs with steadiness, whether you are carrying a tray of bread or a stethoscope. That is the life I hope to grow into.

When I was nine, I sat on the crinkly paper of an exam table swinging my legs while my mother translated my grandmother’s symptoms into English. The room smelled like hand sanitizer and the doctor’s pen clicked impatiently against his clipboard. My grandmother kept pressing her palm to the center of her chest and saying, in Korean, that it felt “heavy, like a stone bowl.” My mother turned to the doctor and said, “She has some discomfort.” I remember thinking that “discomfort” was much too small a word for the look on my grandmother’s face.

Nothing dramatic happened that day. We went home with antacids and instructions to avoid spicy food. But on the drive back, I watched my grandmother in the rearview mirror, one hand still on her chest, and understood for the first time that language can fail people in ways that are dangerous. I could not explain anatomy or medicine then, but I knew there had been a gap in that room, and my family had fallen into it.

Since then, I have wanted to become a physician, but not because of the glossy reasons people usually give. I do not dream about a white coat or a framed diploma. I think instead about the exact tilt of my grandmother’s shoulders on that paper-covered table, and about all the ways patients can become smaller inside a system that was built to help them. What shapes my hope is not only illness, but translation: the effort to carry pain, fear, and memory from one person’s world into another’s without dropping anything important on the floor.

As I grew older, I became the family member everyone handed forms to. At the pharmacy, I learned to pronounce medications before I learned to drive. At appointments, I translated words that seemed straightforward until I had to find their emotional equivalent: “benign,” “monitor,” “side effect,” “risk.” I realized that medicine is not only science; it is interpretation. A doctor can recognize a murmur or read an X-ray, but if a patient feels unseen, if their description gets flattened into convenient vocabulary, the diagnosis is already missing information.

That realization followed me into places outside clinics too. I joined our school’s peer listening program, where students come during lunch with problems that are rarely dramatic enough for adults to notice but heavy enough to bend a teenager’s whole week. There, I learned that people often speak in approximations. “I’m tired” can mean “I haven’t slept because my parents are fighting.” “I’m fine” can mean “I need you to ask again.” Listening well became, for me, a kind of translation too: paying attention to pauses, to what is repeated, to what someone cannot yet say directly.

In biology class, I love the precision of the body—how ion channels open, how valves prevent backflow, how one misfolded protein can alter a life. But I am just as drawn to the messier precision of human conversation. I want to practice medicine that respects both. I hope to become the kind of physician who asks one more question when an answer seems too neat, who notices when a family member keeps glancing at the patient before speaking, who understands that cultural fluency is not a bonus skill but part of competent care.

My grandmother is healthier now, and I am older than my mother was when she first had to interpret a medical world for someone she loved. Sometimes I think back to that exam room and wonder what would have changed if the doctor had waited, listened differently, or asked my grandmother to describe the “stone bowl” in her own language through an interpreter. I cannot rewrite that appointment. But I can shape what I become because of it. I want to be a doctor who hears the bigger word inside “discomfort.”

At 5:42 every morning, my father’s alarm used to go off with the same tinny marimba ringtone, and a few minutes later I would hear the front door click shut behind him as he left for his shift. I was too young then to understand what exactly he did at the hospital, only that he came home smelling faintly of hand sanitizer and cafeteria coffee, with deep half-moons under his eyes and stories he edited carefully for the dinner table. He never talked about medicine in dramatic terms. Instead, he would mention the man who was less afraid once someone explained the procedure in Spanish, or the woman who had no family nearby and just needed a hand held while she waited. Long before I could define compassion, I watched it walk through our kitchen in blue scrubs and tired sneakers.

For a while, I thought what I admired was simply endurance. My father worked double shifts, my mother translated bills and insurance letters at the table, and our apartment seemed to run on lists taped to the refrigerator. But in eighth grade, when my grandmother moved in after her stroke, I began to see something more precise. Recovery was not one triumphant moment. It was repetition. It was crushing pills into applesauce, learning which jokes made her laugh even when speech was frustrating, and sitting beside her during physical therapy as she gripped a foam block with trembling fingers. It was also language: the difference between “She seems confused” and “She is scared because she cannot find the word.” I started going with my mother to appointments, partly to help, partly because I hated how quickly people spoke when they assumed we would nod along.

In those exam rooms, I noticed how much trust could depend on a single sentence. When one doctor turned toward my grandmother instead of over her, slowing down enough for her to answer, her shoulders dropped an inch. When another addressed only the chart, the whole room tightened. I could not change her diagnosis, but I could translate, ask questions, and make sure she was not reduced to a problem to solve. That was the first time I felt the pull of who I hope to become: a physician who treats communication as part of care, not a footnote to it.

That hope has been shaped as much by small, unglamorous moments as by anything impressive. It was shaped by sorting medications into a plastic organizer every Sunday night and realizing how easy it is for one missed instruction to become a real danger. It was shaped by volunteering at a community clinic, where I watched patients arrive with grocery bags, toddlers, bus-transfer slips, and the quiet embarrassment of not being able to miss another day of work. It was shaped by my own job bagging produce at a supermarket, where I learned to recognize the look of someone doing math in their head before swiping a card. Health does not begin in the exam room. It begins in whether you can afford time, transportation, food, and the confidence to ask for help.

I do not want to become a doctor because I imagine medicine as heroic. I want it because I have seen how vulnerable people are when their bodies stop cooperating, and how much dignity can be preserved by attention, patience, and clarity. I hope to be the kind of physician who notices when a patient has gone silent for reasons deeper than agreement, who explains without condescension, and who remembers that fear often sounds like irritation. The future I want is not abstract to me. It sounds like a chair pulled close to a bedside, a question asked twice in two different ways, and a person leaving a room feeling more visible than when they entered.

At 6:12 every morning, the radiology reading room looks less like a hospital and more like a submarine. The lights are dim. The monitors glow blue-white. A half-finished cup of coffee sits beside a keyboard crowded with shortcut stickers. I know this because I have spent many dawns there, trailing my mother as she settles into her chair and begins scrolling through other people’s insides.

At first, I came for practical reasons. My bus to school did not run early enough for her schedule, and she did not want me home alone before sunrise. I would curl up in the corner with algebra homework and a granola bar. But the room kept pulling my attention away from quadratic equations. On the screens, cloudy lungs opened like gray wings. A wrist fractured in three places looked, to me, like a snapped tree branch. A tiny bright pebble in a kidney explained why someone had doubled over in pain all night. I watched my mother move through image after image, noticing what I could not see, and then turn those shadows into a sentence that could change what happened next.

What shaped my hope was not one dramatic case. It was the accumulation of ordinary mornings. It was hearing her dictate, in a calm voice, “suspicious mass,” and then watching her call the ordering physician directly instead of letting the report sit in the queue. It was the way she never said “the gallbladder in room four” but “Ms. Ramirez” or “the kindergarten teacher with the twins.” It was learning that medicine, at its best, is not a parade of heroic rescues. More often, it is disciplined attention. It is caring enough to keep looking.

I hope to become a physician, ideally a radiologist, because I am drawn to that combination of precision and human consequence. I like that radiology asks you to be both skeptical and imaginative. You have to doubt your first impression. You have to build a three-dimensional story from flat slices. You have to know that a millimeter matters. That way of thinking already feels familiar to me. In my engineering class, I spent three weeks debugging a simple-looking code error in our robotic arm. The problem turned out to be one reversed sign buried in a sea of lines. When the arm finally stopped jerking and placed a block exactly where it was supposed to, I felt the same satisfaction I had seen on my mother’s face after solving a difficult case: the quiet pleasure of finding the small thing that changes the whole picture.

But what has shaped this hope even more was being on the other side of medicine. When my grandfather had a stroke, my family learned how terrifying uncertainty can be. The emergency physician spoke quickly. The neurologist was blunt. Everyone was busy. Then a radiology resident came by with the CT images pulled up on a workstation and explained, in plain language, where the bleeding was and why time mattered. He did not soften the seriousness, but he made the unknown less shapeless. My grandfather still struggled to speak, but I remember his shoulders dropping a little, as if understanding itself were a kind of treatment.

That moment changed what I thought a doctor was. Not just someone with expertise, but someone who can lend clarity when a family’s thoughts are scattering in ten directions at once. I want to become that kind of person. I want to be the one who can look carefully, think rigorously, and then speak to people in words that do not make them feel small.

I know the profession I hope for will be demanding. I have seen my mother come home after twelve hours with red marks from her glasses and still answer a clinician’s call over dinner. I have also seen her grin over a perfectly caught diagnosis and then drive to my debate tournament the next morning anyway. Her example has shaped my hope not because her work looks glamorous. It usually does not. It looks like responsibility carried daily.

That is what I want: a life built on attention, steadiness, and usefulness. In a dark room lit by scans, I first saw images. Over time, I began to see a future.

The summer I turned fifteen, I found a dead hawk in our backyard in Tucson. It was a Harris's hawk, rust-colored shoulders still bright against the dust, and I remember crouching over it for a long time, studying the geometry of its talons, the way its feathers overlapped like shingles on a roof. My mother called me inside for dinner three times before I moved. I wasn't sad exactly. I was trying to understand something about the engineering of a body that could, hours earlier, have dropped from two hundred feet onto a jackrabbit at sixty miles per hour.

I want to become a veterinary epidemiologist. That phrase sounds clinical and distant from a dead hawk in the dirt, but the connection is direct. I want to study the patterns of disease in animal populations—not to treat one patient at a time, but to understand why entire species get sick, how landscapes of illness shift, and what we can do before the dying starts.

The hawk probably died from West Nile virus. I didn't know that then. I learned it a year later when I started volunteering at the Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum, where Dr. Elena Marcos, the staff veterinarian, let me shadow her on weekends. She showed me the necropsy reports. Harris's hawks in southern Arizona had been dying in clusters since 2003, and she tracked the cases on a map pinned above her desk with red thumbtacks that looked, from across the room, like a rash spreading across Pima County. That map changed something in me. One dead bird was a mystery. Forty dead birds were a story with causes and chapters, and potentially a different ending.

Dr. Marcos taught me to see animals not just as individuals but as indicators. She said a sick population is like a sentence in a language most people can't read. I wanted to learn that language. Junior year, I designed my science fair project around tracking avian mortality data from eBird and correlating it with mosquito abundance records from the Pima County Health Department. The statistics were messy. I taught myself to use R programming at midnight, watching YouTube tutorials and making errors that crashed my laptop so many times my father joked I should major in computer destruction. But when the regression model finally showed a significant correlation between late-monsoon mosquito blooms and raptor mortality spikes, I felt something I can only describe as recognition—like I'd been reading that sentence Dr. Marcos talked about and suddenly a few words made sense.

I know veterinary epidemiology means years of training: undergraduate biology, veterinary school, probably a master's in public health. I know it means spreadsheets more often than fieldwork, grant applications more often than adventure. I am not romanticizing it. But I think there's something genuinely thrilling about detective work at the scale of ecosystems, about being the person who looks at a map full of red thumbtacks and asks the right questions.

What shaped this hope isn't one dramatic moment. It's the accumulation of small ones: pressing my thumb against the waxy sheath of a hawk feather, learning the Latin names of mosquito species, staring at a scatter plot at one in the morning and realizing the dots meant something. It's Dr. Marcos handing me a pair of nitrile gloves and saying, "You're going to help me today," as if I already belonged in that work.

Last spring, I found another dead bird near our house—a curve-billed thrasher, common, unremarkable. I picked it up with a plastic bag, noted the location on my phone, and emailed the Desert Museum. It might have been nothing. It might have been the first red thumbtack on a new map. The point is, I've started paying the kind of attention that makes it possible to tell the difference, and I don't want to stop.

The summer I turned fifteen, I found a dead heron in the creek behind our house. It was tangled in fishing line, its neck bent at an angle that made my stomach drop. I crouched there for a long time, watching the water move around its body, and I remember thinking that something about this felt like my responsibility—not the death itself, but what came after. I carried it to the bank with my bare hands, feeling the surprising lightness of its bones, and buried it under the sycamore tree where the soil was soft.

That was the same summer my mother was diagnosed with lupus, and I spent most of July driving her to appointments in our Corolla with the broken AC, windows down, her hair whipping across her face while she pretended not to be scared. I watched doctors talk to her in language that felt deliberately opaque, watched her nod along and then cry in the parking lot because she didn't understand what was happening inside her own body. I started translating for her—not from another language, but from medical jargon into something she could hold onto. I'd go home and read journal articles on my laptop until two in the morning, then explain them to her over breakfast, drawing little diagrams on napkins.

I want to become a physician. But when I say that, I mean something specific, something that has to do with the heron and my mother and the napkin drawings all at once.

In our town of Caldwell, Texas, population eight thousand, there is one primary care doctor for every four thousand residents. Dr. Muñoz, who treated my mother, also treated my neighbor's diabetes, my friend's broken collarbone, and half the livestock injuries that the large-animal vet was too booked to see. She was stretched so thin that appointments lasted eleven minutes on average—I timed them once, sitting in the waiting room with a stopwatch app, which I realize now was a strange thing for a teenager to do. But I was trying to understand why my mother left every visit still confused, still afraid. Eleven minutes. That was the answer, or at least part of it.

I've since volunteered at the Burleson County health fair for three years running, taking blood pressures and handing out pamphlets about heat stroke and water safety. Most people who come through are uninsured. They ask me questions I'm not qualified to answer, and I've learned to say "I don't know, but let me find someone who does" with a kind of earnestness that makes people trust me rather than dismiss me. A woman once grabbed my wrist and asked if the lump on her daughter's neck was cancer. The daughter was six. I found the nurse practitioner, who examined her and said it was a swollen lymph node, probably from a cold. The mother cried with relief, and I stood there understanding that medicine is not just about the body. It is about the space between terror and knowledge, and who helps you cross it.

I think about the heron sometimes. How no one was coming to untangle it. How the fishing line had been there long enough to cut grooves into its leg. I think about systems that create harm slowly, invisibly, and the people who are caught in them without knowing how to get free.

I don't want to be a doctor who speaks in a language my patients can't understand. I want to practice family medicine in a community like Caldwell, where eleven-minute appointments are the norm because there simply aren't enough hands. I want to be one more pair of hands. I want to sit with someone and draw on a napkin until the fear in their eyes turns into something manageable—not gone, but named. Naming things is the first kind of care I ever learned, kneeling in creek water with a dead bird in my arms, understanding that even what is lost deserves our full attention.

The summer I turned fifteen, I found a dead bat on our porch in Tucson, its wings folded like a collapsed umbrella. Instead of flinching, I knelt down and studied the thin membrane stretched between its finger bones, the way the cartilage branched like the veins of a leaf. My younger sister screamed from the doorway. I asked my mom for a Ziploc bag and a pair of kitchen gloves.

That bat ended up in our freezer for three weeks, wedged between a bag of frozen corn and a tub of mint chocolate chip, until my mom finally insisted I either do something with it or throw it away. So I did something with it. I spent an afternoon with a craft knife and a YouTube tutorial on specimen preservation, pinning the wings open on a piece of cardboard. The result was clumsy and imperfect, the left wing torn slightly where I'd stretched too hard. But I could see everything—the radius, the ulna, the impossibly elongated phalanges—and I kept thinking about how this animal had essentially evolved hands into wings, how the same bones in my fingers had been repurposed for flight in another species.

I want to become a veterinarian who specializes in wildlife rehabilitation, specifically for species most people would rather not think about: bats, opossums, snakes, the animals that make my sister scream from doorways. This ambition didn't arrive as a single revelation. It accumulated, the way sediment builds into rock, through a series of encounters with creatures that were unwanted and often misunderstood.

Junior year, I started volunteering at a raptor rescue center twenty minutes outside the city. Most of our patients were red-tailed hawks and great horned owls that had collided with cars or swallowed rodent poison. My job was unglamorous: scrubbing mews, thawing frozen mice, recording weights on a clipboard. The center smelled like cedar shavings and iron. But I learned to hold a Harris's hawk on my gloved fist, to read the dilation of its pupils as a measure of stress, to tube-feed a dehydrated screech owl at two in the morning during monsoon season when the phone wouldn't stop ringing with reports of downed birds.

What struck me most was how many calls we got from people who wanted us to remove healthy animals simply because they found them disturbing. A perfectly fine gopher snake sunning on a driveway. A family of javelinas passing through a yard. A bat colony roosting in an attic. The callers didn't want these animals rehabilitated. They wanted them gone. And I started to understand that the biggest obstacle to wildlife conservation isn't always habitat loss or climate change in the abstract. Sometimes it's a visceral, personal disgust that makes people unwilling to share space with other species.

I think veterinary medicine sits at the exact intersection where I can address this. A wildlife vet doesn't just set broken bones and treat infections. She stands in the gap between human communities and the animals those communities would prefer to ignore. She translates. She says, that bat colony is eating ten thousand mosquitoes a night, that opossum is practically immune to rabies and eats the ticks off your lawn. She makes the unfamiliar less frightening through knowledge, patience, and proximity.

I'm under no illusion that this path is straightforward. Wildlife vets often earn less than their small-animal counterparts. The work is emotionally grueling, and many patients don't survive. During one shift at the raptor center, I held a Cooper's hawk while the director euthanized it, the bird's talons gripping my glove until they suddenly didn't. I cried in my car afterward and still drove back the next Saturday.

That dead bat on my porch was never disgusting to me. It was an engineering marvel, a solution to a problem evolution had been working on for fifty million years. I want to spend my life fighting for the animals that other people walk past or recoil from, making the case that they deserve not just tolerance but genuine wonder. The bones in their wings are the bones in our hands. We are not so different.

The summer I turned fifteen, I found a dead hawk on the shoulder of Route 9. It was a red-tailed hawk, and its wing was bent at an angle that made my stomach drop, the way a broken umbrella looks in a storm drain. I crouched next to it for what must have been ten minutes, studying the barred pattern on its tail feathers, the yellow scaling on its feet, the way its eye had gone cloudy but still seemed to hold something. A car honked. I took a photo with my phone and carried the image around for weeks like a secret.

I want to become a wildlife veterinarian. Not the kind who works with golden retrievers and tabby cats in a cheerful suburban office, though I respect that work. I mean the kind who gets called at odd hours to sedate a black bear tangled in fishing line, or who splints the wing of a barred owl that flew into a cell tower. The kind whose hands shake a little from adrenaline but stay steady enough to suture. I know this sounds specific, and maybe romantic, but the hope didn't come from a movie. It came from that hawk, and from what happened after.

I brought the photo to Mrs. Okafor, my biology teacher, who told me the hawk had likely been struck by a car. She said it happens constantly—more than we think—and that most injured raptors never reach a rehabilitation center. They just die on roadsides while we drive past at sixty miles an hour. Something about that sentence rewired me. The casualness of it. The scale.

That fall, I started volunteering at the Tidewater Wildlife Refuge, a small operation run out of a converted barn about twenty minutes from my house. The director, a woman named Patrice who wears the same mud-stained Carhartt jacket every single day, taught me how to prepare food trays for recovering songbirds and how to clean enclosures without stressing the animals. The work was not glamorous. I smelled like fish and disinfectant every Saturday. My friends started calling me "Bird Girl," which I pretended to hate but secretly liked.

The moment that solidified everything happened in March of my junior year. Patrice let me assist while she examined a great horned owl that had been found in someone's backyard, unable to fly. The owl was enormous up close, its ear tufts rigid with fear, its talons gripping the leather glove so hard I could see the tendons flexing. Patrice spoke to it in a low murmur while she palpated its wing. She found the fracture by touch alone. I watched her face—completely focused, completely calm—and I recognized something I'd been circling around for two years. This was the kind of attention I wanted to build my life around. Not attention as a buzzword, but the real thing: the practice of noticing what a creature cannot tell you in words.

I know the path is long. Undergraduate biology, then veterinary school, then likely a specialized residency or certification in wildlife medicine. I know the pay is modest and the emotional toll is real. Patrice has told me about the animals she couldn't save, the funding that dried up, the nights she questioned whether any of it mattered. I've already had my own small version of that feeling, holding a cedar waxwing that died in my hands during intake, its body going from warm to not-warm in a span of seconds that felt both instant and eternal.

But I keep coming back to that hawk on Route 9. Something in me refused to look away, and I think that refusal is the truest thing I know about myself. I don't want to drive past. I want to be the person who stops, who kneels in the gravel, who has the knowledge and the skill to actually do something. That is the hope. It has feathers and talons and a cloudy eye that still holds something I can't quite name.

My grandmother keeps a notebook full of wrong answers. Not mistakes exactly — more like hypotheses she tested on her patients over forty years as a general practitioner in rural Georgia. "Mrs. Tatum's fatigue," one entry reads, followed by three crossed-out diagnoses before the circled correct one: celiac disease, which nobody in Clinch County was diagnosing in 1987. The notebook is water-stained and smells like the cedar closet where she stored it, and I have read it cover to cover twice.

I want to become a physician-researcher, someone who works at the exact seam between treating people and understanding disease. I know that sounds like what everyone says. But my wanting comes from somewhere specific enough that I trust it.

Last summer I worked as a volunteer at Grady Memorial Hospital in Atlanta, mostly doing intake paperwork and occasionally sitting with patients while nurses were occupied elsewhere. One afternoon I sat with a man named Mr. Osei who had been waiting six hours to learn whether the mass in his lung was cancer. We talked about fufu. He had a specific, almost academic opinion about the correct texture, and he wanted me to understand the distinction between his mother's version and every inferior version he had encountered since immigrating from Kumasi in 1998. I listened and I thought about how the body contains a whole person, their whole history of eating and moving and loving specific things, and how medicine has to somehow hold all of that while also being rigorous and evidence-based and fast.

The tension between those two demands — the human and the scientific — is what I want to spend my life inside.

My high school has a research program, and junior year I designed a small study examining whether patients at a local free clinic were receiving consistent hypertension follow-up. They were not. Forty-three percent of patients with documented high blood pressure had no follow-up appointment scheduled, and the pattern correlated strongly with whether the patient spoke English as a first language. I wrote up the findings and felt something I can only describe as furious clarity. The problem was visible. The mechanism was visible. The intervention was imaginable. I presented the data to the clinic director, who thanked me, looked tired, and told me they lacked the staff to implement anything new.

That encounter shaped me as much as anything. It taught me that knowing something is not the same as changing it, and that changing it requires understanding systems, not just bodies. I have been reading about health policy with the same appetite since. I want to work in academic medicine precisely because it lets you move between scales — between Mr. Osei's specific lung and the structural conditions that determined how long he waited.

My grandmother's notebook is also a record of a system. She practiced alone, without specialists nearby, which meant she had to think harder and document more carefully than her urban colleagues. She told me once that isolation made her a better diagnostician because she could not outsource her uncertainty. She had to sit with not knowing, sometimes for weeks, while continuing to care for the patient in front of her. I think about that when I feel impatient with complexity.

I am not naive about what medicine requires. I have watched my grandmother's knees deteriorate from decades of standing, watched her miss holidays, watched her cry once on the phone when a patient she had known for thirty years died alone during COVID because no one could enter the facility. I understand the cost.

But I also watched her open that cedar closet, take out the notebook, and show it to me like it was something precious. All those crossed-out wrong answers, the ones that eventually led somewhere true. She handed it to me without a word, and I understood she was offering me not just her history but a method: stay curious, stay honest, keep looking.

I want to keep looking.

My grandmother keeps a notebook full of wrong answers.

Not mistakes, exactly — more like guesses. She's been learning English for eleven years, ever since she moved from Guangzhou to live with us in Fresno, and every Tuesday she sits at our kitchen table with a spiral notebook and writes down words she doesn't know yet, followed by her best attempt at what they mean. "Melancholy: when rain stays too long." "Reckless: driving like my grandson." Most of them are wrong. She doesn't care.

I used to find this embarrassing. When I was thirteen I watched her hand a grocery store cashier a piece of paper that said "I wood like the chicken please" and I pretended to be intensely interested in a display of paper towels. I was ashamed of the gap between who she actually was — a woman who ran a textile factory, raised four children, survived things I still don't fully understand — and the stumbling, approximate version of her that English allowed.

Then last winter I took AP Chemistry and discovered I was the stumbling, approximate version of myself.

Electrochemistry broke me. I understood the words individually. I could not understand them in sequence. I sat with the textbook for three hours one Sunday and filled a page with wrong diagrams, wrong equations, wrong everything. My teacher, Mr. Garza, found me in his classroom before school and looked at my work without speaking for a long moment. Then he said: "You're thinking about it. You're just thinking about it wrong. That's actually the interesting part."

I've been thinking about that sentence for a year.

What Mr. Garza meant, I think, is that the wrong answer contains information. It shows you the exact shape of your misunderstanding, which is different from showing you nothing at all. My grandmother's notebook isn't full of failures — it's a map of how a mind moves toward something it doesn't have words for yet. "Melancholy: when rain stays too long" is wrong, but it's wrong in a way that tells you she understands what melancholy feels like. The definition is the feeling.

I want to become a scientist. More specifically, I want to work in materials science, in the design of things that don't exist yet — new polymers, new composites, substances built molecule by molecule for a specific purpose. I became interested in this the way you become interested in most things: accidentally. I read about aerogel in a magazine at my dentist's office when I was fifteen. A solid that is ninety-nine percent air. A material that looks like frozen smoke and can support thousands of times its own weight. I thought: someone had to think of that. Someone had to be wrong about solids for long enough to arrive there.

I think the skill I most want to develop — in college and beyond — is precisely that: the ability to be productively wrong. To make a guess that is specific enough to be incorrect, and to read my own incorrectness carefully. This is different from the kind of intelligence that looks like confidence, the kind that gives answers quickly and with authority. I've met people like that. I've also met my grandmother.

She got the word "melancholy" right last March. She showed me her notebook, pointed at the original entry, and wrote the real definition beside it. Then she said, in careful English: "I liked mine better."

I think about materials science as a field that lives in that space — between what we know a substance can do and what we haven't asked it yet. I want to spend my life in that particular kind of not-knowing, the kind where you write something down wrong so you can find out why.

My grandmother still has the notebook. She's started a second one.

My grandmother keeps a notebook full of wrong answers. Not mistakes, exactly — she calls them "almost-truths," the chemical equations she wrote in her pharmacy school in Seoul during the 1970s that her professors marked incorrect but that she believed, with some deep stubborn instinct, were pointing somewhere real. She showed it to me last spring while I was melting chocolate for a science fair experiment on emulsification, and I remember the smell of cocoa butter mixing with the musty paper smell of decades. "The wrong ones teach you more," she told me, in the particular mix of Korean and English our family uses when something matters.

I want to become a research chemist. Not because chemistry came easily to me — it didn't, not at first — but because of the specific texture of the moment when it finally does make sense, which feels less like receiving an answer and more like watching fog lift off a specific mountain you've been staring at all morning.

My entry point into serious chemistry was actually a catastrophe. Sophomore year, I was trying to synthesize a simple ester for a lab report and instead produced something that smelled aggressively like nail polish remover mixed with old gym socks. My lab partner held her nose and declared the experiment ruined. But I stayed after class, genuinely confused about where the reaction had gone sideways. My teacher, Mr. Okoro, didn't explain it to me directly. He handed me two papers from a journal and said, "Tell me what you find." That felt, at the time, like being abandoned. Later, it felt like being trusted.

Reading those papers, I encountered for the first time the way scientific language carries its own kind of excitement — hedged, precise, refusing to overclaim, but underneath all that restraint, clearly thrilled. The researchers were describing catalyst behavior in esterification reactions, and they kept using the phrase "warrants further investigation." I started a list of things that, to me, warranted further investigation. The list now has forty-three items.

Item seventeen: why certain polyphenols in green tea inhibit the same enzymes that some anti-cancer drugs target, but through completely different binding mechanisms. I fell into this question after my mother started drinking three cups of green tea every day following her breast cancer diagnosis two years ago. She did it because her oncologist mentioned, almost casually, that some research suggested a possible protective effect. I wanted to know what "some research" meant. I read everything accessible to me, which wasn't much, and understood half of what I read, which was less. But the not-knowing didn't frustrate me the way it once had. It had become its own kind of fuel.

My grandmother's almost-truths notebook sits on my desk now. She let me borrow it, which felt significant given how carefully she normally guards it. Inside, her handwriting is small and urgent, and next to many of the marked-wrong equations she's written tiny notes to herself in Korean — questions, mostly, about why she'd thought what she'd thought. That practice, of interrogating your own reasoning rather than just abandoning it, seems to me like the core of what a scientist actually does.

I don't want to discover things in the dramatic, eureka sense. I want the slower work: designing experiments that fail in informative ways, reading papers until the gaps in knowledge become visible, building understanding the way my grandmother built hers — incrementally, stubbornly, with a notebook that keeps track of everything including the wrong turns.

When I imagine my future, I'm in a lab that smells like ethanol and the specific sharp smell of a fume hood running, and I'm writing in a notebook of my own. Some of what's in it will be wrong. I want to be the kind of scientist who keeps those pages anyway, who treats uncertainty not as an obstacle but as the actual terrain — the place where the real questions live, and where the work I most want to do begins.

My grandmother keeps a laminated card in her wallet that lists the side effects of her blood pressure medication. She's had it for eleven years. The edges are soft from handling, and two of the corners have separated into white fibers. Every time she sees a new doctor — which is often, because she lives in rural Appalachian Kentucky where physicians rotate through the county clinic like substitute teachers — she pulls it out and places it on the desk before they can say anything. It is her way of saying: I have done my homework. Please do not talk around me.

I have watched that card my whole life without understanding what it meant until two summers ago, when I sat with her through six appointments in eight weeks following a small stroke. I took notes. I asked questions. I translated — not between languages, but between registers, between the clipped clinical shorthand the doctors used and the plain English my grandmother needed to actually make decisions about her own body. After the third appointment, she told me I should be a doctor. I told her I wanted to be something more specific than that.

What I want to be is a physician who works in places like her county. Not as a temporary rotation, not as a gap-year mission, but as a sustained presence. I want to be someone's regular doctor, the kind whose name a patient knows well enough to complain about.

This want has a specific shape because it grew from specific experiences. I grew up watching my mother drive forty-five minutes each way for my sister's asthma follow-ups. I worked last summer as a health educator at a free clinic in our town, where I learned that about a third of our patients delayed care not because they couldn't afford it but because they couldn't take a day off work without losing wages they couldn't spare. I started doing Saturday morning calls with patients who had missed appointments, just checking in, asking what had gotten in the way. The clinic director called it "retention outreach." I called it listening.

What I heard, again and again, was not just logistical frustration but a particular exhaustion — the weariness of feeling like a problem to be managed rather than a person to be treated. One woman, whose name I won't use, had been living with undiagnosed Type 2 diabetes for what turned out to be at least three years. She had suspected something was wrong. She hadn't sought care because her last doctor had made her feel stupid for not already knowing what hemoglobin A1C meant. She had decided she would rather not know than feel that way again.

I think about her when I think about what kind of doctor I want to be. I think about the laminated card. I think about what it costs a person to become their own advocate in a system that requires expertise to navigate and withholds that expertise as a kind of power.

I am interested in internal medicine and rural primary care. I am interested in health literacy and in how trust is built and broken between patients and institutions. I have been reading about physician shortages in rural counties and about the structural incentives that pull doctors toward urban specialty practices, and I understand those incentives. I don't think wanting to go somewhere underserved makes me selfless. It makes me someone who has watched a specific problem from close range for a long time and developed a specific idea about how to help.

My grandmother still has the laminated card. Last month she added a new medication to the list in her careful block handwriting, pressing hard with a ballpoint pen through the laminate's slick surface, leaving the faint ghost of the letters on the line below. She didn't ask anyone's permission. She already knew what she needed to know.

I want to be a doctor worthy of that card.

The low harmonic hum of the sewing machine was the soundtrack to many of my evenings growing up, as my mother meticulously stitched fabric into dreams. Tables stacked with vibrant textiles, tracing paper, and peeking pins filled our small living room, transforming it into a colorful labyrinth that was both my playground and my parents' workplace. This makeshift tailoring shop wasn’t just a business for us; it was our lifeline, threaded with hope and resilience. Watching my parents work tirelessly in this way nurtured in me a profound respect for their dedication and an aspiration to carve a similar path of tenacity, albeit in a different fabric—the fabric of engineering.

Though I never quite mastered the art of threading a needle, the intricate process of piecing together parts to create something greater drew me in. My fascination with how things were made began at our dining table, turned workshop, where I often indulged in taking apart old electronic toys to see what magic lay within. Unlike sewing machines, which I found daunting, small circuit boards and tangled wires felt like untapped potential waiting for discovery.
