One chilly autumn weekend, I concocted my first experiment—a small vertical garden, built from salvaged pallets and rusted nails from Dad's workshop. It was an echo of those stacks of bricks Mrs. Thompson used for her first hives. Tiers of lettuce and herbs thrived, a miniature testament to resiliency. That success kindled a desire not only to study but to mend the delicate dance between growth and conservation. I dream of building sustainable networks, communities where the old wisdoms of farm tales meet the innovations of technology.

The land shapes us as firmly as we shape it. From the dusty trails leading past whispering cornfields to the verdant swell of harvest-ready orchards, my town is both my muse and mentor. I’m determined to preserve and nourish these landscapes, ensuring future generations inherit not just land but a legacy of care. My aim is to devour experiences like freshly picked apples–crisp, challenging bites, each one leading me closer to a consciousness about our connection with the earth.

Every morning, as the dawn stretches its sleepy fingers, bathing our town in hues of amber and rose, I remind myself of the growing hope planted within. I’m committed to a path rooted in preservation and sustainability, not just for my town, but for communities beyond our rural boundaries. With an education in sustainable agriculture, I seek to become a steward of the earth, guiding the world into a future where harmony graces every planted acre and every home that rests upon it.

Every Sunday morning, like clockwork, the scent of fresh hay mingled with the crisp air as I ambled down the dirt road to my grandfather's farm. The dew clung stubbornly to the fields, glistening like stubborn little pearls that refused to melt away under the sun's commanding glare. My grandfather, with hands that told stories a century old, would be waiting by the old red barn, its paint peeling like rough snake scales.

Living in Hartsville, a town so small that the grocer doubled as the post office, I learned that wild imaginings were bred from quiet surroundings. But it wasn’t just the endless rows of corn or the muffled hum of the crickets at night that fueled my dreams; it was the stories that my grandfather, a retired botanist turned farmer, whispered like secrets.

His tales stretched far beyond the confines of our dusty patch of earth. He spoke of rainforests that bowed beneath the weight of their own lushness and deserts that danced, as if alive, with shimmering heat. Each story was a thread in a tapestry of vivid worlds waiting to be understood and explored. On one particularly rash summer afternoon, as we repaired the barn's weather-worn fence, he said, “Nature is the world’s greatest map, Lula. With each leaf and grain, it holds the power to teach and transform.” 

His words planted a seed in me, one that still unfurls its leaves today.

It’s my ambition to become an environmental scientist, a steward of the landscapes that have taught me since childhood. This calling isn’t simply an echo of the past but a deliberate shout into the future. As I’ve watched the seasons sweep through our town, unapologetic in their change, I’ve come to realize how delicate and interconnected our world truly is. The relentless summer heat threatens the yield of Mr. Clarke’s wheat fields. It withers the dreams I see in my own family’s eyes each planting season.

High school science classes offered me the first glance into the micro-universes I longed to understand. Under the flicker of fluorescent lights, we unraveled the mysteries of photosynthesis and cellular respiration. My biology teacher, Mrs. Morrow, spotted my curiosity early. “You’re like a detective,” she said, after class one day, while peering over her tortoise-shell glasses, “only you’re solving mysteries of the very world itself.”

Spending hours in the school laboratory, inhaling the sharp scent of ethanol, I felt a thrill unmocked by any rodeo or town fair. I conducted experiments that teased apart the symphonies of nature, much like how my grandfather had detangled the complex and wondrous threads of the ecosystems in his stories.

But it isn’t only the call of curiosity that fuels this hope. It’s a dedication to protect and nurture, to give back to the dirt roads and sprawling fields that offered me a canvass for my dreams. I wish to decipher the impact of human hands on the natural world, seeking answers as patiently as I once propped up sagging fence posts.

So, I dream to unravel these great mysteries, to contribute to sustainable methods that promise life not just to plants and animals, but also to the cultures and dreams that rely on them. To peer under the leaf’s edge and see not just the veins and cells, but the tapestry it knits in the grand design.

Each aspiration I hold is rooted in the rust-bucket truck rides across town, as my grandfather shared the richness of a world interwoven with science and soil. In every breath of country air, I hear an invitation, a calling to learn and protect—the complexity, beauty, and fragility of our natural world.

Late summer mornings in a small rural town begin with the syrupy smell of honeysuckle hanging in the air, a signature scent carried by the whisper of cornfields and the rustling leaves of old oaks. It's a place where roads stretch out eternally, lined with silos that stand like sentinels under expansive blue skies. Here, possibilities feel both endless and confined, a paradox I have grown up with and one that propels my hope of becoming an agricultural scientist. 

The seeds of this hope were planted during my childhood. Each day after school, I'd find myself in the company of my great uncle, a wiry yet sturdy farmer with skin like worn leather and a hat that never seemed to leave his head. His farm was a world of its own, a sprawling temple of nature's orchestra, where each gust of wind through the barley seemed to play its own symphony. He taught me to appreciate not just the beauty but the science behind these everyday miracles. I became fascinated with the ways we could help crops resist diseases or survive in harsher climates—not just for profit, but to ensure everyone could share in this bounty.

One sun-drenched afternoon, as we sat by the small pond tucked away at the back of his land, Uncle Fred spoke of an unusual problem: his tomatoes were resilient against the usual pests but seemed to falter in the sporadic dry spells that increasingly plagued our summers. His eyes, normally twinkling with confidence, clouded with doubt. It was then the realization struck me: the very soil I'd always taken for granted held questions waiting to be answered. That summer, I spent countless hours poring over agricultural guides and pest control manuals, trying to devise a sustainable watering system. The thrill of the challenge ignited a passion I had never anticipated.

In our school library, there's a single shelf dedicated to agriculture, and I devoured it all—intent on grasping every legend and leaf behind the science. But textbooks could only take me so far, and it was in the National Botanic Gardens during a school trip that my aspirations crystallized. There, amidst sprawling research plots and glasshouses, I saw the future being engineered: projects converting drab weeds into crops that could thrive in near-barren soil. I felt a magnetic pull, a whisper promising that this was where I belonged.

Working on my uncle’s farm taught me the value of resilience and innovation. While others flocked to far-off cities with skylines, I remained, learning to weave modern science with time-honored traditions. It is from this synthesis that my ambition took root: to harness genetic technologies to create sustainable food sources, allowing farm communities to flourish and providing food security to battling regions worldwide.

Our town's annual harvest festival—our marquee celebration—showcases the pride and challenges of rural life. As families gather for the customary display of produce and potluck, I see not just the fruits of labor but resilience sewn into each waxy apple and every carved-out pumpkin. This spirit drives me, urging me forward with the knowledge that the science I pursue is more than just theory, it’s a lifeline for places like mine.

In a world often governed by the hum of technology and urban sprawl, I hope to stay rooted yet soar. I envision myself in research labs and fields yet to be tilled, developing sustainable practices that stem from a deep understanding of the earth I know so well. As I journey toward this goal, the whisper of those cornfields and the hopeful notes carried by the rural breeze will accompany me, guiding my path like the North Star above the plains—constant, unyielding, and full of promise.

When the morning fog settles over the pastures behind our house, it makes the hay bales look like they’re floating. That quiet, bleached light is the same one I woke up to every morning before school—feeding our chickens before the bus arrived at 6:45, my shoes damp with dew, the metal latch on the coop always colder than I expected. I used to resent the routine, the way the whole town seemed stuck in chores and tradition. But somewhere between collecting warm eggs and wiping dust off the old feed buckets, I began to sense something meaningful hiding in the rhythm: this small-town life had shaped how I wanted to help the world beyond it.

Our town has one stoplight, a diner that closes at 7, and a high school graduating class of forty-eight. On paper it doesn’t sound like much, but to me it’s a map of people who make things work with almost nothing. When the school’s heating system broke my freshman year, our custodian, Mr. Riggs, welded parts together in his shed to fix it. When the neighbor’s barn caught fire, half the town gathered that night with shovels and buckets, working silently until smoke gave way to sunrise. Those moments showed me how a community survives on shared problem-solving—and how fragile that survival is when opportunities run out.

I started noticing the ways our economy was fraying. The feed store closed. My best friend’s parents left for jobs forty miles away. Even our 4-H club struggled to afford supplies. That’s when I began tutoring younger kids for free at the library, using the only resource we had—each other. Sitting with fourth graders tracing fractions in pencil smudges, I saw how a little patient guidance could change the way they looked at themselves. It felt similar to watching a seed push through dry soil: invisible work beneath the surface, until suddenly there’s life.

That realization—that growth can happen even in scarcity—sparked my interest in economics and community development. I want to study how small towns can build sustainable local systems instead of depending on whatever company arrives and leaves with the next recession. The hope isn’t abstract; it’s the wish that our main street won’t be just a memory by the time my younger brother graduates. I want to become someone who can draft budgets, policies, and plans that echo the ingenuity I grew up around, but with tools that might actually last.

One summer, I convinced our town council to let me run a small “pop-up market” beside the hardware store. Farmers sold tomatoes, two classmates sold handmade soaps, and even Mrs. Petree, who is nearly eighty, brought rhubarb pies. We didn’t make much money, but there were people smiling on that empty stretch of pavement, talking, exchanging things they had made. At the end of the day, as I helped stack the folding tables into my dad’s truck bed, I felt the same quiet satisfaction as those foggy mornings on the farm: proof that something small could hold real weight.

What has shaped my hope isn’t escape from my town—it’s the stubbornness it taught me. When you grow up watching people patch fences with leftover wire, you learn that every problem has a possible solution if you turn it over enough times in your hands. I carry that same habit inside my notebooks, sketching ideas about rural entrepreneurship and resource sharing like someone mending a wire one twist at a time.

Someday, I want to return to communities like mine with knowledge strong enough to meet their resilience halfway. I want to stand in a room where the air smells like coffee and sawdust, spread out plans for a clean-energy co-op, and know that I’ve built something that could keep the lights on for another generation. My town taught me that hope is work—the steady kind formed by early mornings and cold latches—and that’s the kind I plan to keep doing.

Every morning of my childhood began with the sound of roosters and the smell of diesel. Our neighbor’s tractor would start just before sunrise, and the low hum carried across the cornfields, climbed the hill behind our house, and slipped through my open window. That steady mechanical rhythm was the backdrop of my life in Millersburg, a town so small the only traffic light sits crooked in front of the post office.  

Growing up here taught me that work is never just work—it’s weather, it’s timing, it’s an act of faith. My mother ran the cash register at the feed store, always with hay dust on her sleeves. My father hauled milk for nearby dairy farms, his hands permanently nicked from loading metal cans. I used to think success meant escaping these routines. But slowly, the very rhythms I once wanted to outrun started to shape what I hope to become.  

The summer before ninth grade, I got my first job picking tomatoes for Mr. Alvarez, who leased three greenhouses behind the hardware store. Sweat pooled inside my gloves before the sun cleared the trees, and I learned that the smell of ripe tomatoes is both earthy and sweet, like rain on metal. Mr. Alvarez used to talk about how fragile plants are when they first sprout, how a sudden storm could flatten months of work. Still, he'd show up each morning with quiet persistence. Watching him, I began to recognize a kind of courage that isn’t loud.  

That same summer, one of the growers in the next town lost almost his entire crop to a fungus that spread unseen until it was too late. Hearing neighbors talk about how “maybe we could’ve stopped it if we’d caught it early,” I started wondering if there were better ways to help farmers predict or prevent those losses. My curiosity led me down a spiral of YouTube tutorials, then soil science articles, and eventually a free online course about sustainable agriculture.  

It felt strange trying to do web research on our spotty Wi-Fi while my parents thought “soil sensors” sounded like something from a science-fiction movie. But when our own backyard garden started thriving after I tried basic soil testing kits, my dad looked at me with a little spark of pride. That small patch of healthy plants felt like proof that I could turn curiosity into something useful.

Now I hope to become an agricultural engineer, focused on sustainable farming for rural communities like mine. It’s a mouthful to say at the diner, where most people still measure success in acres. But I want to help design affordable tools or systems that make it easier for farmers here to adapt—maybe automatic irrigation that uses less water, or handheld sensors that predict soil deficiencies before crops fail.  

Everything about my hometown has shaped this hope—the smell of hay, the sound of diesel engines, the way the sky turns copper before rain. Even the frustration of watching neighbors fight blight or drought has pushed me toward solutions instead of despair. Some nights, when the moonlight shines over empty fields, I imagine sensors blinking quietly in the rows, giving farmers data as reliably as they once relied on instinct.  

I’m aware that college will take me far beyond Millersburg’s single traffic light, but I’ll carry its rhythms with me. I don’t want to escape the world that made me; I want to understand it better, and maybe send something useful back home. If the boy who once picked tomatoes just for pocket money can help those same greenhouses thrive through science, then every sunrise—with its hum of tractors and its scent of soil—will feel like a beginning instead of an ending.

Every morning before the bus arrived, I stood on the cracked concrete of our driveway and watched fog rise off the hayfield across the road. The smell was tangy—sweet from the grass, metallic from the dew on barbed wire. My neighbor, Mr. Keller, always waved from his tractor as he fed his cows. That quiet, ordinary sight—fog, fence, tractor—has shaped nearly everything I want to do in life: I want to become a civil engineer who helps rural towns like mine prepare for a changing climate while keeping what makes them home.  

I grew up in a town of 1,200 people, where the nearest movie theater was a forty-five‑minute drive and the loudest sound at night was the grain dryer behind the co‑op. When the river flooded in spring, it cut off Farm Road 7, forcing everyone on that side to drive an extra hour to get groceries. I remember standing with my parents on the small bridge that April, listening to the suck and roar of brown water, wondering why anyone had built a two‑lane bridge at the lowest point in the valley. My dad just shrugged and said, “It was cheaper here.”  

That phrase—“cheaper here”—explained so much of my town’s problems: the one unreliable water main, the potholes large enough to swallow a mailbox, the sagging gym roof at the elementary school. But it also made me curious. Why couldn’t we build better—even in small places?  

When I was thirteen, I started experimenting with duct‑tape “dams” in the creek behind our house. I’d wedge rocks and branches, trying to direct the flow toward the goose pond, proud when the water actually obeyed me for a few minutes. Later, in tenth grade, when I took my first physics class, the equations finally gave words to what I’d noticed—the pull, the pressure, the slope. It felt like uncovering the hidden grammar of every ditch and culvert I’d ever driven past.  

My school didn’t have an engineering club, so I made one. We met in the ag room, surrounded by fertilizer trucks and broken seed counters. We built a small model of the old Keller bridge using tongue depressors, testing how weight and arch shape affected stability. I’ll never forget the faint lemon smell of the glue sticks and the laughter when our first prototype collapsed under three cans of root beer. But by the third try we’d designed a truss that held. When we presented it to the town council, one member joked that maybe we should build the next real bridge too.  

What deepened my hope wasn’t just success but seeing how people reacted when something worked. The following summer, I interned with the county road department, riding in a truck that rumbled like an empty stomach. We patched asphalt, set reflective markers, and drew sketches of drainage systems on the backs of receipts. Mr. Sanchez, the foreman, said, “You learn fast, kid,” when I spotted that the slope near the new culvert was too shallow. I felt, for the first time, that I could make our roads safer not by leaving my town behind but by understanding it better.  

Still, I know I’ll have to leave to learn. My county library holds one shelf of engineering books, most older than me. I want to study in a place with labs, mentors, and classmates who see data not as numbers on a screen but as stories about people and land. I picture returning years from now, walking across a new bridge that doesn’t sag, knowing the design accounted for hundred‑year floods that now come every five.  

The fog will still rise from the fields then, but maybe a road will stay open longer, a farmer will reach town faster, a school bus won’t have to detour. That’s what I want—to become an engineer who builds hope as sturdily as steel, who learns from where I’m from, and never forgets that a small town deserves big ideas.

When I was thirteen, I could tell the time of day by the sound of the grain dryer across the river. Its low hum at dawn meant Dad had already hitched the auger; its silence at noon meant he’d come in for lunch, salty with sweat and dust. Our town has one stoplight, three churches, and a gas station where everybody leaves their engines running—out of habit, trust, or both. From here, it takes an hour and a half to reach the nearest hospital with more than ten beds. I didn’t think much about that until the day it mattered.

It was a Sunday in August, brittle hot. I was helping Mom at the church picnic when Mrs. Anders, who’s over seventy, collapsed near the dessert table. Everyone froze. Pastor Dan called 911, but the dispatcher’s voice crackled that it’d be thirty minutes before an ambulance could come from the county seat. I knelt beside Mrs. Anders while Mom fanned her with a paper plate. She kept murmuring that her chest hurt and that she couldn’t catch her breath. My only first-aid training came from a short unit in health class, but I remembered two things: keep her calm, and don’t move her. So I talked. I told her how her cinnamon rolls always sold out first and that I still owed her for fixing the tear in my homecoming dress. Her eyes stayed open, barely. When the EMTs finally arrived, she was still conscious. Later, Mom told me that might have made a difference.

That day replayed in my head for weeks—the sound of ambulance tires crunching on gravel, the strange stillness just before they came. I kept thinking how “thirty minutes” could stretch longer than any hour. Out here, time moves differently, especially when you’re waiting for help. That’s when I started saying, out loud to anyone who’d listen, that I wanted to be a nurse. Not a vague “someday” kind of wish—more like a determination that rooted in me, the way Johnson grass sneaks through every fence line.

People from my town either stay or leave and promise not to come back except for holidays. The ones who stay usually farm or teach. The idea of leaving to study nursing at a university feels almost rebellious, like I’m stepping out beyond the edge of a map everyone else assumes ends at County Road 12. But what shaped that hope isn’t rebellion—it’s responsibility. I don’t want another Sunday like that one to pass where everyone just waits, helpless, for flashing lights.

To test myself, I joined the volunteer fire department’s youth program. The first call I went on was a tractor rollover. I was only allowed to watch, but what I saw wasn’t chaos—it was coordination. The chief’s calm voice, the rhythm of motion, the shared understanding of purpose. I carried that feeling home with me. It was the opposite of waiting.

School doesn’t offer advanced science classes, so I’ve made my own experiments. Using an old stethoscope from our 4H club’s “health barn,” I practiced listening to heartbeats on my younger brother and recorded them on my phone—part curiosity, part hope that someday I’ll trade the static hum of the grain dryer for the steady rhythm of a human pulse. This might sound strange, but when I listen, I imagine each beat as a kind of promise.

I know nursing will demand more than steadiness—it’ll require precision, empathy, and resilience. But those skills are grown here, too: in the patience of mending fence wire one staple at a time, in the empathy of bringing a casserole to a grieving neighbor, in the resilience of watching a hailstorm destroy what you’ve planted and starting over anyway.

I used to think I’d outgrow this place. Now I understand I’m shaped by it—by distance, by waiting, by the people who wave even when they don’t know you personally. What I hope to become isn’t just a nurse; it’s someone who can bridge that distance for others, turning thirty minutes into twenty-nine, twenty-eight, maybe less. Enough to make a difference. Enough to matter.

At night in my town, the dark is complete enough to hear. The grain elevator goes quiet, dogs bark somewhere across the fields, and the only bright light comes from the gas station by the highway where my mom works the late shift. People who pass through call our town “peaceful,” but growing up here has taught me that quiet can mean many things. Sometimes it means safety and neighbors who know your name. Sometimes it means there is no one close enough to ask for help.

I learned that on a February evening when my grandfather had trouble breathing. My parents were at work, the roads were slick, and the volunteer ambulance was coming from twenty miles away. I sat on the kitchen floor beside him with the cordless phone pressed hard to my ear, repeating what the dispatcher told me while the radiator hissed and my grandfather tried to joke through each shallow breath. I remember watching the red numbers on the microwave change minute by minute and realizing how far away emergency care could be when you live between cornfields and county lines. He recovered, but I did not forget that feeling of waiting.

Since then, I have wanted to become a rural physician, the kind of doctor who does not see small towns as places to leave but as places worth staying for. That hope did not arrive all at once. It grew in small, stubborn ways, shaped by the people around me and by the gaps I kept noticing. In my town, the school nurse is also the attendance secretary. The nearest pediatric clinic is forty minutes away if the weather is good. My neighbor, who raises hogs, once ignored chest pain for two days because it was planting season and “the beans won’t wait.” Around here, people are tough, but toughness is not the same as access.

What has also shaped me is the way rural communities care for one another when systems fall short. After my grandfather came home from the hospital, Mrs. Hernandez from church brought over chicken soup in a dented stockpot and refused to take it back for a week. My ag teacher checked in on us when I started missing first period. My mom, exhausted after standing behind the gas station counter until midnight, still drove an elderly customer to her dialysis appointment because there was no one else. In a town this small, care is rarely abstract. It looks like casseroles on porches, tractors pulling cars from ditches, and people using their lunch breaks to translate medical forms for neighbors.

I have tried to take part in that kind of care with the tools I have now. At school, I started a health information table during basketball games with our FFA chapter and the county clinic, mostly because I was tired of hearing adults say, “Nobody tells us these things.” We handed out blood pressure cards, flu shot dates, and simple flyers about diabetes screening. Most people walked by for the popcorn, but some stopped to talk. One night, a man I have known all my life asked me to explain what “prehypertension” meant because he was embarrassed to ask the nurse at his last appointment. That moment stayed with me: how often fear disguises itself as pride, and how much easier it is to ask questions when the person answering sounds like home.

I know becoming a doctor will require years of study far beyond what my town can offer me. I want that challenge. I want college to stretch me in classrooms, labs, and clinics, and to teach me the science I need to match the responsibility I feel. But I do not want education to sand down where I come from. Growing up rural has made me observant, patient, and practical. It has taught me to listen carefully because people do not always say directly what hurts. It has taught me that a problem can be urgent even when it is ordinary, like a missed appointment, an icy road, or a pharmacy an hour away.

I hope to become a doctor who can hear the meanings inside a town’s quiet. I want to return to places like mine and practice medicine with skill, but also with familiarity, respect, and persistence. I want a child sitting on a kitchen floor with a phone in her hand to know that help is not impossibly far away.

On clear nights in my town, the sky looks unfinished, as if someone forgot to stop adding stars. We have one stoplight, one grocery store, and a volunteer fire department whose siren everyone recognizes before it even finishes its first wail. Growing up here meant knowing which gravel roads flood first, which fields hold deer at dusk, and which porch lights stay on because someone is always expected home late. It also meant learning how much a place can give you and how much it can leave out.

My town does not have a hospital. If someone needs stitches after the clinic closes, or if a baby decides not to wait, the drive to the nearest emergency room takes forty minutes if the roads are dry and longer if a tractor is crawling ahead of you. When I was eleven, my neighbor Mr. Larkin sliced his hand open fixing a combine header. I remember the blood on the towel wrapped around his palm and the way his wife kept saying, “Stay awake, stay awake,” though he was never in danger of fainting. What I remember most, though, is the waiting: waiting for someone to find keys, waiting for the truck to start, waiting for the distance between us and help to shrink.

Since then, I have noticed how often people here wait. My grandmother waits three months for a specialist appointment in the city. My friend’s little brother waits for his inhaler refill because the pharmacy only gets certain shipments once a week. My mom, who teaches second grade, keeps granola bars in her desk because some students wait until Monday to eat something more filling than chips. In a place where people pride themselves on handling things themselves, I have seen how hard it is to “make do” when what you really need is access.

That is why I hope to become a rural physician. I do not mean this as a shiny childhood dream I picked out of a costume box. I mean it as a practical promise, the kind built slowly from watching my community improvise around shortages. I want to be the doctor who knows that the patient in front of me may have driven an hour, missed work, and arranged child care just to keep this appointment. I want to practice medicine with the same attention my town gives weather reports and livestock prices: closely, seriously, because lives depend on it.

This hope has also been shaped by smaller moments. In biology, my teacher let me borrow an old microscope with a cracked eyepiece, and I spent a week peering at onion cells at our kitchen table while my dad cleaned mud off his boots nearby. During basketball season, I learned to tape sprained ankles and recognize when someone was pretending they were “fine.” At the county fair, I volunteered at the first-aid booth and discovered that half of helping people is staying calm enough that they can borrow your calm. Science gave me language for what was happening inside the body, but my town taught me to notice what was happening around it.

I know I am leaving a place where everybody knows my truck by its dented tailgate and my last name opens conversations before I do. But I do not want to leave in order to become someone entirely new. I want to leave so I can return more useful. Someday, I hope to drive back on these same two-lane roads with a stethoscope on the passenger seat, pull into a clinic with lights still on after five, and shorten the distance between my town and the care it deserves.

At night in my town, the dark is complete enough to hear. You can hear gravel settling after a pickup turns into a driveway, the hiss of sprinklers over alfalfa, and once in a while the two-note whistle of the freight train cutting past the grain elevator. I grew up where the tallest building is the co-op feed store and directions are given by barns: turn left at the red one, keep going until the white one falls in on itself. For a long time, I thought everyone lived this close to weather, livestock, and each other.

In a town of 1,200, other people’s problems do not stay other people’s problems for long. When Mrs. Hensley slipped on ice and broke her wrist, my mom started dropping off casseroles before anyone asked. When our neighbor’s well pump failed in July, my dad ran a hose from our place across the ditch so their cattle could drink. Help moved through town the way irrigation water does, finding the lowest places first. I grew up watching that, and I think it taught me my first definition of responsibility: noticing what needs doing and doing it before it becomes urgent.

But I also learned that caring is not the same as access. When my grandpa had chest pain during calving season, the clinic in town could only do so much. The nearest hospital with a cardiac unit was over an hour away, and in winter that hour could stretch forever behind a snowplow. I remember sitting in the passenger seat while my mom drove, her knuckles pale on the steering wheel, the heater blasting against the cold leaking through the doors. I watched fence posts flick past in the headlights like tally marks. It felt wrong that something as basic as seeing the right doctor depended on a good road, a full gas tank, and luck.

After that, I started paying attention. I noticed how my friend Wyatt missed school every few months because his asthma got bad and his family waited too long to make the drive. I noticed the school nurse keeping crackers, bandages, and spare inhalers in her desk like she was running a tiny emergency room. I noticed how people in town were practical about pain, almost proud of it. They would say things like “I’ll get it checked after harvest,” as if bodies, like machinery, could be run one more season on a bad part. The older I got, the less I admired that toughness.

I hope to become a doctor and practice rural medicine. I do not mean this in an abstract, brochure-like way. I mean I want to work in a place where I might treat a sprained ankle after basketball practice, then explain blood pressure medication to someone who still calls me by my first and middle name because they knew me as a kid. I want to be the kind of doctor who understands why a patient hesitates to schedule surgery during branding, why missing one shift at the mill matters, and why some people trust advice more when it comes from someone who knows the road to their house turns to mud in March.

What has shaped this hope is not only what my town lacks, but what it has. My science classes were small enough that my teacher let me stay after school and use the microscope long after the bell. Volunteering at our EMT fundraiser breakfast, I listened to first responders tell stories between flipping pancakes and refilling coffee. Last summer, I shadowed a family physician in the next county. In one morning he stitched a ranch hand’s palm, checked a toddler’s ears, and called an elderly patient in a snowbound area to make sure her oxygen had been delivered. Nothing about his day was glamorous. It was repetitive, interrupted, and deeply useful. I went home more certain than ever.

Growing up here has made me ambitious in a way that probably does not look flashy from the outside. I do not dream about leaving and never looking back. I dream about returning with skills my community should not have to drive sixty miles to find. I want to become a doctor because I have seen how distance can turn a manageable problem into a crisis, and how being known can make people brave enough to ask for help. In a small rural town, you learn that your life is tied to other lives. Mine has taught me what I want to do with that fact.

In my town, the tallest thing is the grain elevator, and the loudest sound on fall Friday nights is the marching band warming up behind the football field. We have one stoplight, one grocery store, and a post office where everybody knows whose mail belongs in which box before they even look at the names. I grew up where roads turn to gravel without warning and where weather is not small talk but a real force that decides whether the hay gets baled, whether the creek rises, whether school starts two hours late. For a long time, I thought the world was mostly made of fence lines and county roads.

What changed that was the clinic.

It sits next to the pharmacy in a brick building that used to be a dentist’s office, and it is where my family goes for everything from strep tests to stitched fingers. When I was twelve, my dad sliced his palm open fixing a combine header. I remember the blood soaking through the towel he wrapped around his hand, my mom driving too fast down Highway 14, and the waiting room smelling like coffee and rubbing alcohol. The nurse practitioner on duty cleaned the wound, joked with my dad to keep him from looking, and explained every step to me when she caught me staring. Her voice was calm in a way that made the room feel bigger. I watched her make something frightening manageable, not by doing anything dramatic, but by knowing what to do and by treating us like neighbors instead of a problem to solve.

After that, I started paying attention to how often people in my town have to wait for care. My grandfather drives forty minutes to see his cardiologist. My friend’s little brother had to miss school for speech therapy because the nearest specialist only comes to our county twice a month. Last winter, our clinic closed early three days in a row because one provider had the flu and there was no one else to cover. In a city, that might be inconvenient. Here, it can mean choosing between losing a day of work and ignoring a pain that should not be ignored.

I hope to become a physician and return to a rural community like mine.

That hope has been shaped partly by what I have seen and partly by what I have been asked to do. On weekends, I help my grandmother at the church food pantry, carrying boxes of canned beans and pancake mix into truck beds while she asks after people’s mothers and knees and blood pressure. At school, I started a ride-sharing spreadsheet for students who needed transportation to after-school tutoring and medical appointments in the next town over because I was tired of hearing, “I would go if I could get there.” None of this is heroic. In a small town, you notice quickly that life works only because people keep stepping in for one another.

I want to bring that same practical care into medicine. Not the version of a doctor from television, rushing down bright hallways, but the kind who understands that a patient’s cough is also a question about gas money, harvest season, childcare, and pride. Growing up here has taught me that health is never just biology. It is distance, weather, work schedules, and whether someone feels known enough to ask for help.

I used to think leaving town was the only way to grow. Now I think growth can also mean coming back with skills your community needs. I want to become the person who can walk into a room that smells like coffee and antiseptic, meet fear with steadiness, and make care feel possible close to home.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a dead coyote on the shoulder of Route 9, just past the Hendersons' soybean field. Its body was intact but wrong somehow, too thin, its fur patchy in places that made me think of the mange cases Dr. Paulson talked about at the feed store. I crouched there for maybe ten minutes, close enough to see the flies already working, and I remember thinking not about death exactly but about the fact that nobody in our town of eleven hundred people could have told this coyote what was wrong with it. We had a large-animal vet who drove forty minutes from Carlinville, and we had WebMD on slow internet. That was our healthcare infrastructure, animal and human alike.

I want to become a rural physician. Not the kind who passes through for a residency requirement, but the kind who stays. The kind whose truck is recognized at the gas station and who knows that Mrs. Oleska will refuse a referral to Springfield no matter how many times you suggest it because she hasn't driven on the interstate since 1997.

What shaped this isn't one clean narrative. It's accumulated. It's my grandmother rationing her insulin because the pharmacy in Palmyra closed and the one in Girard didn't always have her brand. It's watching my mom drive my younger brother forty-five minutes each way for his asthma checkups, missing half a shift at the grain elevator office every time. It's the year three kids in my class had strep throat for weeks because their parents couldn't take the time off to get to a doctor, and eventually Mrs. Connolly, our school secretary who had no medical training whatsoever, started Googling symptoms at her desk like she was running triage.

I understand why doctors don't come here. I'm not naive about it. Our town can't offer the salary, the specialty colleagues, the restaurants, the anonymity. My AP Biology teacher, Mr. Fitch, who is also our JV basketball coach and who also drives the bus on Tuesdays, once told me that wanting to come back to a place like this is either the most noble or most foolish thing a person can do. He said it while dissecting a fetal pig, which felt appropriately unglamorous.

But I've been paying attention to what absence costs. I watched my grandfather die of a heart attack in our kitchen because the nearest hospital was thirty-two minutes away and the ambulance came from even farther. He was sixty-one. My grandmother found him on the linoleum floor next to the coffee maker, and by the time paramedics arrived, there was nothing. I was nine, standing on the porch in bare feet, watching red lights come up our gravel road too slowly. That image hasn't faded. The red lights on the gravel, the way they lit up the bean field in pulses.

I don't think I can fix rural healthcare by myself. I'm seventeen and I just learned what a P-value is. But I know what it feels like to live somewhere that the medical system has essentially decided isn't worth the investment, and I know the specific textures of that neglect — the expired EpiPen in the school nurse's drawer, the veterinary antibiotics people quietly use on themselves, the GoFundMe pages for surgeries that insurance won't fully cover and that require travel no one can afford.

I want to sit in an exam room in a town like mine and say your name correctly on the first try because I already know you. I want to be the person who doesn't have to drive in from somewhere else. I've spent my whole life in a place people leave, and I want to come back to it with something it needs. The coyote on Route 9 deserved better. So does Mrs. Oleska.

The summer I turned fourteen, our neighbor's Holstein collapsed in the field behind our house. I watched from the kitchen window as Mr. Jeffers knelt beside her, his hand on her distended belly, and I knew before my mother said anything that I'd be spending my afternoon helping instead of swimming at the creek. By the time the large animal veterinarian arrived from forty-five minutes away, the cow had been down for nearly three hours. Dr. Muñoz worked calmly, repositioning the calf, her forearms slick and trembling with effort. The calf came out still. She tried anyway—cleared its airway, compressed its small ribcage with both hands, breathed into its nostrils. Mr. Jeffers told her to stop before she told herself.

On the drive home, my mother said sometimes you do everything right and it still goes wrong. I didn't answer. I was thinking about Dr. Muñoz's hands, how they never hesitated, how she seemed to already know the animal was gone but refused to let that knowledge make her careless.

I want to become a rural veterinarian. Not the kind who treats golden retrievers in suburban clinics with waiting rooms that smell like lavender plugins, but the kind who drives a mud-caked truck between farms at dawn, who reaches into the warm insides of animals while their owners stand behind her chewing their thumbnails. I know this sounds unglamorous. That's sort of the point.

I live in Hardin County, Iowa, population scattered. The nearest veterinary clinic that handles large animals is in Iowa Falls, and even that one has been talking about scaling back services. When Dr. Muñoz retired last year, she wasn't replaced. Her clients absorbed into practices even farther away, some over an hour's drive. This means that when a ewe is struggling to lamb at two in the morning, a farmer has to make a calculation: is this animal worth the emergency call fee, the mileage, the wait? Sometimes the math says no. Sometimes the animal dies in the gap between need and access.

I've been working at Tilton Farm Supply since I was fifteen, loading bags of mineral supplement and dewormer into truck beds, and I've learned to read the quiet worry in a farmer's voice when they ask me which over-the-counter cattle antibiotics we carry. They're not being cheap. They're being abandoned by a system that doesn't find them profitable.

In school, I dissected a fetal pig with the kind of reverence that made my lab partner uncomfortable. I memorized the structures not because the test required it but because I wanted to understand the architecture—how the renal artery branches, how the small intestine coils with such unlikely neatness. I check out veterinary parasitology textbooks from the University of Iowa interlibrary loan system. My browser history is almost entirely populated by videos from the Cornell College of Veterinary Medicine's YouTube channel. I realize this makes me a specific kind of strange.

But I think about the calculations happening right now on farms I can see from my bedroom window. The heifer with a retained placenta. The horse with a swollen fetlock. The dog that guards the chicken coop and hasn't been vaccinated in three years. These animals and the people who depend on them deserve someone who will show up.

I think about Dr. Muñoz compressing that calf's ribcage, the way she fought for something already lost because the fighting itself was a kind of promise. I want to be the person who makes that promise. I want to drive the mud-caked truck. I want to show up.

The first time I sutured skin, it was on a pig leg my mother brought home from the butcher. I was fourteen, sitting at our kitchen table in Harlan, a town of eleven hundred people in the southeast corner of Kentucky, watching a YouTube video uploaded by a veterinary school in Colorado. The needle was a curved upholstery needle from my grandmother's sewing box. The thread was dental floss. I made seven stitches, and five of them were terrible, but the two decent ones held the edges of a cut together in a way that made me feel like I had just performed a small miracle.

I want to become a rural physician. Not a doctor who passes through a small town during a residency rotation and then leaves for a city with better restaurants. I mean the kind of doctor who knows that Mrs. Combs on Route 119 will not take her blood pressure medication unless you also ask about her tomato plants, because that is the conversation that makes her trust you enough to listen.

What shaped this hope is not one clean narrative. It is a accumulation of moments that smell like rubbing alcohol and wood smoke.

When I was nine, my father sliced his forearm open on a piece of sheet metal while repairing our barn roof. The nearest emergency room was forty-three minutes away in Middlesboro. My mother wrapped his arm in a dish towel printed with cardinals and drove seventeen miles per hour over the speed limit the entire way. I sat in the back seat watching the towel turn from white and red to just red. He needed twenty-two stitches. The doctor who treated him was a locum tenens physician filling in for two weeks. She did not know my father's name. She did not know he was allergic to codeine. My mother had to tell her three times.

Our town lost its only primary care provider the year I turned twelve. Dr. Patel retired and moved to Lexington to be closer to his grandchildren, which I understand but which left a hole that has never been filled. Now people drive to Harlan ARH or they wait. They wait until the chest pain becomes a heart attack. They wait until the mole changes shape and then changes again. They wait because gas costs money and the road to the hospital winds through mountains that ice over in November and do not thaw until March.

In high school, I started volunteering with the Harlan County Health Department, mostly doing intake paperwork at free dental clinics and flu shot drives. One Saturday, a woman came in with her two children, and while I was writing down their information she told me she had not seen a doctor in six years. Six years. Her youngest child was five. She had given birth at home, not by choice but because she could not afford the hospital bill from her first delivery and was afraid to go back. I wrote her information on the form in neat block letters and felt the inadequacy of a clipboard in the face of that confession.

I am not romantic about small-town life. The opioid crisis hollowed out families on my street. The coal economy collapsed and left behind both poverty and a stubborn pride that makes asking for help feel like defeat. But I love the way my neighbor Mr. Hensley leaves deer jerky on our porch without knocking, and the way the whole town shows up when the creek floods, passing sandbags hand to hand in the dark.

I want to be the doctor who stays. I want to know which patients are allergic to codeine before they have to say it themselves. I want to make stitches that hold, on real skin, in a place that has waited long enough.

The summer I turned fourteen, I found a dead calf in the east pasture with its jaw swollen to the size of a cantaloupe. My dad knelt beside it, pressed his thumb against the distended tissue, and said, "Lumpy jaw. We lost this one because I didn't catch it in time." He wasn't making excuses. He was stating a fact the way people in Elmore, Nebraska, state facts — plainly, with the understanding that knowing wouldn't undo anything but might prevent the next loss.

That dead calf has stayed with me longer than it probably should. I think about it when I imagine what I want to become, which is a large animal veterinarian who returns to a community like mine — one where the nearest vet is forty-seven miles away and the wait for an appointment can stretch past the point of saving an animal.

Elmore has a population of 803, a co-op grain elevator, two churches, and a gas station that sells both diesel and surprisingly good breakfast burritos. What it doesn't have is a veterinarian. Dr. Paulsen retired in 2019 and moved to Lincoln to be near his grandchildren. Since then, my family and our neighbors have relied on Dr. Kessler out of Broken Bow, a kind woman who covers a territory so vast she once told my mom she puts 60,000 miles on her truck every year. When our border collie, Hatch, tore his ACL last spring, we waited nine days for an appointment. He limped around the kitchen, toenails clicking on linoleum, and I carried his water bowl to wherever he decided to lie down.

I've spent most of my life being the unofficial animal person in our house. At eleven, I helped my dad pull a breech calf at two in the morning, my arms slick to the elbows, my heart hammering so hard I could hear it in my ears. At thirteen, I sutured a gash on our quarter horse's shoulder using a YouTube video and a kit Dr. Paulsen had left with my father before retiring. The stitches were ugly — puckered and uneven — but they held, and the mare healed without infection. These moments taught me that I'm not squeamish, but they also taught me something more important: the gap between what rural families need and what they can access is enormous, and it has real consequences measured in animals that suffer longer than they should or don't survive at all.

In school, I gravitate toward biology and chemistry not because they come easily but because I can see where they lead. When I learned about anaerobic bacteria in AP Biology, I thought immediately of lumpy jaw — Actinomyces bovis, the organism that killed that calf. Suddenly a textbook concept had hooves and weight and a smell I remembered. That connection between the classroom and the pasture is what drives me.

I know the path is long. Undergraduate work, then four years of veterinary school, likely debt that would make most people choose a more lucrative small-animal practice in a city. I understand why people make that choice. But I also know what it feels like to watch your dad do math at the kitchen table, calculating whether the cost of a vet visit exceeds the value of the animal, and I know the look on his face when the math says to let it go. That arithmetic leaves something out. It doesn't account for my younger sister, Josie, who named every heifer in our herd and cried into her cereal the morning after we lost one to a prolapsed uterus we couldn't manage alone.

I want to be the person who shows up. I want to drive the muddy roads between ranches with a truck full of supplies and the training to use them properly, so that the next fourteen-year-old standing over a dead calf hears a different fact — not "I didn't catch it in time," but "call me, and we'll catch it early."

Elmore gave me everything that shapes this hope: the work ethic, the proximity to animals, the understanding of what's missing. I want to be what's missing.

The summer I turned twelve, our town's only grocery store closed. Not quietly—the owner, Mr. Vasquez, taped a handwritten note to the door that said "Thank you for 31 years." My mom stood in the parking lot reading it twice, then drove forty-seven miles to the nearest Walmart while my brother and I counted the number of fields we passed. We counted twenty-three.

That drive became routine. Every two weeks, we'd load the cooler into the back of the pickup and make the trip, and I'd watch my mom calculate gas money against the grocery list in her head, crossing off items she decided we didn't really need. String cheese. Sparkling water. The good shampoo. I started to understand that the distance between where we lived and where resources existed wasn't just physical—it was something that compounded quietly, invisibly, month after month.

Hartwell has about 2,400 people if you count the farms outside town limits. We have one stoplight, a volunteer fire department, and a school where my sophomore English teacher also coached girls' basketball and supervised study hall. She did all three things well. I want to be clear about that—she was excellent—but I also knew she was stretched in ways that weren't fair to her or to us.

What I started noticing, slowly and then all at once, was how much the gaps in our town felt less like accidents and more like decisions someone else had made. The grocery store closed because the economic math stopped working. The doctor's office became a clinic open two days a week because full-time physicians didn't want to come here. These weren't natural disasters. They were policy outcomes.

I found public health the way you find something you've been circling for years without knowing it. A school nurse named Mrs. Okafor drove forty minutes from the county seat every Tuesday to visit our school. She was the one who finally figured out I'd needed glasses since fifth grade. She sat with me while I looked out the window through the new prescription and said, "The trees actually have individual leaves." She laughed. Then she said, seriously, "You shouldn't have waited this long." I thought about all the kids in towns like mine who were still waiting.

Rural public health is, I've learned since, a field that combines policy, medicine, community trust, and logistics in equal, difficult parts. It asks you to work with systems and also with people who are rightly suspicious of systems. It requires understanding why someone drives past the county health van without stopping—because they remember the last time they trusted an institution and it left. I want to study epidemiology and health policy with the specific intention of returning to rural communities, not as a visitor, but as someone trying to reduce the number of items crossed off grocery lists, the number of miles driven for basic care.

My town taught me to look at absence and ask who's responsible for filling it. It taught me that competence deployed without resources is still an unfair ask. It taught me, through Mr. Vasquez's handwritten note and Mrs. Okafor's Tuesday drives, that people are always doing their best within systems that require better design.

I am not idealistic about this in the way that's easy to be at seventeen. I've watched enough programs arrive in Hartwell with enthusiasm and leave with data that never turned into change. I know solutions are slower and stranger than mission statements make them sound.

But I also know what it felt like to see individual leaves for the first time and understand, with genuine anger and genuine clarity, that I should have seen them years earlier. That feeling is directional. It points somewhere specific. I intend to follow it.

The summer I turned fourteen, a drought hit Harlan County hard enough that my father stopped sleeping. I'd find him at two in the morning standing at the kitchen window, staring at the cornfield like he could will water out of the sky through sheer concentration. The well dropped eight feet in six weeks. We hauled water from my aunt's place in orange five-gallon jugs that left rust rings on the truck bed, and I watched my father do math on paper napkins, multiplying yield estimates by commodity prices, subtracting loan payments, arriving at numbers he'd immediately crumple up.

That's when I started paying attention to soil.

Not out of some romantic notion about the land — I was angry at the land that summer, honestly — but because our county extension agent, a woman named Mrs. Paulette Dorsey, drove out one August afternoon and spent three hours in our field doing something I'd never seen an adult do: she crouched down, picked up a handful of dirt, smelled it, rubbed it between her fingers, and then told my father with calm specificity exactly what had gone wrong over the past decade of planting cycles. She talked about water retention and organic matter and mycorrhizal networks like she was describing a patient's chart, and my father, who hadn't slept properly in two months, sat on the tailgate and listened like a man being handed a lifeline.

I want to be that person.

Not the extension agent specifically, though Mrs. Dorsey became something of a hero to me afterward, answering my questions over email with the patience of someone who genuinely believes knowledge should travel. I want to be the person who understands a problem well enough to walk into the middle of a crisis and offer something real — a scientist who works at the intersection of agricultural ecology and climate adaptation, someone who develops drought-resistant soil management practices for farming communities that don't have the luxury of waiting for better conditions.

Harlan County has about 7,400 people, one stoplight, and cell service that disappears between the grain elevator and the highway overpass. Growing up here taught me to solve problems with whatever happened to be available, which is different from the kind of problem-solving I imagine happens in well-funded labs with predictable supplies. My chemistry teacher, Mr. Beckham, ran our entire AP class out of a converted storage room because the school couldn't afford a proper lab, and we titrated solutions on folding tables next to boxes of volleyball equipment. I learned to take precision seriously even in imprecise conditions. I think that matters.

I've spent the past two summers doing soil sampling for a small agricultural research cooperative three counties over, driving routes through farms that look exactly like mine, collecting cores at marked GPS points before sunrise because the heat distorts the afternoon readings. The work is unglamorous and repetitive, and I love it with an embarrassing intensity. Each core is a record of decisions — what got planted, what got applied, what got neglected — and reading that record feels like a form of translation I was born to do.

What I hope to become is specific: a researcher who produces science that functions like Mrs. Dorsey that afternoon, arriving with something useful in a place where the stakes are not abstract. My father farms 340 acres that his grandfather broke from oak scrub. The climate that threatens those acres is not a future problem. It arrived early and stayed, and I watched it cost my family sleep and money and a particular kind of confidence that's hard to rebuild.

I want to understand that problem well enough to help solve it.

The summer I turned fourteen, the grain elevator in Millbrook caught fire. By the time the volunteer fire truck arrived — twelve minutes, because half the volunteers were still pulling on their boots — the east wall had already collapsed. My neighbor Dale Hoffmann lost forty thousand bushels of soybeans and stood in the gravel lot watching the smoke go orange against the dark sky, and nobody said a single word to him. We just stood there together, smelling the sweet char of ruined crops.

That silence bothered me for weeks. Not because it was cruel — it was actually the opposite, something like reverence — but because I kept wondering what the right words would have been. What could someone have said to Dale that would have mattered?

I grew up in a place where 1,400 people share a single stoplight and three churches, where everyone knows which families are struggling and which fields flooded in April, where information travels fast but help sometimes travels slower. I watched my mother, a home health aide, drive forty minutes each way to reach elderly patients because there simply were no clinics nearby. I watched my high school lose its only counselor when the district budget got cut, which meant the kid in my class dealing with his parents' divorce had nowhere to go except the parking lot behind the auto parts store. I started to understand that in rural places, the distance between people and resources isn't just geographic. It's structural. It's policy.

I want to become a rural health policy advocate — specifically, someone who works to close the gaps in mental health access for agricultural communities. That sentence sounds formal and a little stiff, but the feeling behind it is not. The feeling is Dale in the gravel lot. The feeling is my classmate driving circles around town with nowhere to land.

My junior year, I started volunteering with an extension program that connects farm families to financial counseling after crop losses. I sat in on sessions where a counselor named Patricia would ask farmers to describe their stress using a scale from one to ten, and I watched grown men who had never once complained about a broken finger struggle to find language for what they were carrying. Farm stress, depression, and suicide rates in rural counties are significantly higher than national averages — I know that statistic now because I looked it up after one of those sessions — but living inside it feels less like a statistic and more like a slow leak.

What I noticed, and what I keep thinking about, is how much the stigma around mental health in farming communities is tied to identity. Asking for help feels like admitting the land is beating you. Any solution that ignores that specific culture will fail. Policy has to be designed with that texture in mind, not just with numbers.

I want to study public health and political science. I want to understand how programs get funded and why rural counties are perpetually last in line. I want to eventually work at the intersection of state agricultural departments and community health systems, building something that reaches people who will not drive to the city, who will not call the hotline, who need the help to come to them looking like something they already trust.

I do not think I can fix what Millbrook is missing. But I know how to sit in the gravel lot. I know how to recognize a silence that means something, and I am learning, slowly, to translate it into language that makes legislators lean forward in their chairs and actually listen.

The summer I turned fourteen, the grain elevator in Milford burned down. I remember standing in the Kwik Star parking lot at two in the morning watching orange light eat the skyline, and what I remember most clearly is not the fire itself but my father's face — the way he calculated loss in real time, running numbers in his head while the smoke rose. Three families stored their corn there. One of them was ours.

My father never talked about the financial part directly. He talked around it, in the way that men in Milford talked around most things — through practicalities, through decisions about seed costs and rented acreage. But I watched him that fall sit at the kitchen table with a yellow legal pad, and I understood, even at fourteen, that the numbers on that pad were the difference between staying and leaving.

We stayed. Barely.

What I didn't understand then, and spent the next four years slowly figuring out, is that there were people whose entire job was to help families navigate exactly that kind of catastrophe — and that those people almost never came from places like Milford. Our county's agricultural extension office was staffed by two people managing four counties. The nearest farm financial counselor was in Des Moines, ninety minutes away, and charged fees that assumed a certain kind of client. We were not that kind of client.

I want to become a rural agricultural economist. I want to do the work that wasn't available to my father that fall.

This isn't a romantic ambition. I'm not operating under any illusion that economics is a clean discipline or that policy translates smoothly into help for actual people. I know that because I've spent the last two years interning with our county extension agent, Linda Forstner, who has taught me more about the gap between academic agricultural research and farm-level reality than any textbook could. Linda once showed me a federal risk management report that suggested farmers "diversify revenue streams" — written by someone who had clearly never tried to diversify anything while also planting 400 acres in a five-week window.

What shaped this hope is specific: it's the yellow legal pad. It's Linda's coffee-stained desk covered in constituent files. It's a conversation I had last spring with a woman named Darla who was trying to decide whether to sell her late husband's farm because she couldn't make sense of the USDA loan paperwork. I sat with her for two hours and we figured it out together, and when she left she said, "I didn't know anyone could help me with this." That sentence has lived in my chest ever since.

I've taken every economics and statistics course my high school offers, which is not many. I've read outside them compulsively — I worked through most of an introductory econometrics textbook this past winter, which my mother found both admirable and slightly alarming. I know that college will give me tools I can't currently access. I know that graduate work in agricultural economics will let me eventually produce research that is actually legible to the people it's supposed to serve.

What I hope to become is someone who closes the distance between expertise and the people who need it. Someone who shows up in the county that doesn't have enough extension agents, who writes the report that a farmer in Milford can actually use. Someone who, when the elevator burns, is already there.

I grew up watching the fields go brown in drought and green in rain and I learned that the land holds no sentiment. But the people working it do. I want to work for them.

Ever since I was young, I have been captivated by the power of storytelling. Growing up, my parents would often find me nestled in a corner of our library, absorbed in books that transported me to worlds beyond my own. I quickly realized that stories have the power to connect, educate, and inspire people. This realization has shaped my hope to become a screenwriter who crafts narratives that resonate with audiences worldwide.

My interest in screenwriting was ignited during my freshman year of high school when I joined the film club. We were tasked with creating short films, and I discovered the intricate process of translating a story from script to screen. I was fascinated by how words on a page could evoke emotions and spark conversations. Through this experience, I saw firsthand the profound impact stories could have on individuals and communities.

My desire to tell compelling stories was further fueled by my participation in a summer creative writing workshop. During the workshop, we explored various aspects of storytelling, from character development to plot structure. It was an enlightening experience, allowing me to delve deeper into the nuances of writing. I learned that screenwriting requires not only creativity but also discipline and perseverance, as it involves multiple drafts and revisions. This realization motivated me to work diligently on my craft, eager to hone my skills and bring my visions to life.

Additionally, my English teacher, Mrs. Roberts, played a crucial role in shaping my aspiration. Her passion for literature and storytelling was infectious, and she encouraged me to explore diverse narratives and perspectives. Her feedback on my writing was invaluable, pushing me to refine my style and voice. With her guidance, I realized the importance of stories that challenge conventions and spark meaningful discussions about societal issues.

My community has also significantly influenced my hope to become a screenwriter. Growing up in a multicultural neighborhood, I have been exposed to an array of stories and experiences. Each person I encounter brings a unique narrative, and these interactions have broadened my understanding of the world. They have taught me the importance of representing diverse voices and perspectives in my writing, ensuring that my stories reflect the complexities of the human experience.

I am particularly drawn to stories that explore themes of identity and belonging. These themes are close to my heart, as I have often grappled with questions about my place in the world. Through storytelling, I aim to give voice to those who may feel marginalized or misunderstood, highlighting their struggles and triumphs. I believe that stories have the power to foster empathy and understanding, bridging gaps between different cultures and communities.

Looking ahead, I hope to study screenwriting in college, where I can immerse myself in an environment that encourages creativity and collaboration. I am eager to learn from accomplished screenwriters and gain insights into the industry. My goal is to create films that not only entertain but also leave a lasting impact on audiences, prompting them to reflect on their own lives and the world around them.

Ultimately, I aspire to become a screenwriter who crafts stories that inspire change and ignite dialogue. I have faith in the transformative power of storytelling and its ability to foster connections among people. I hope to contribute to the world of cinema by bringing new, diverse narratives to the screen. Embracing the art of storytelling, I am determined to use my voice to make a difference, one script at a time.

Throughout my life, I have been fascinated by stories. Whether I was reading a novel, watching a film, or listening to someone recount their experiences, I have always found myself captivated by the power of narrative. This fascination has fueled my desire to become a writer, someone who crafts stories that entertain, inspire, and provoke thought. My hope is not only to create engaging and immersive stories but also to use storytelling as a means to connect people and share diverse perspectives.

One major influence that has shaped this aspiration was my grandmother. She would often share tales about her childhood and experiences growing up in a different era. Her stories were vivid and poignant, filled with details that painted a picture of a world I could only imagine. Listening to her opened my eyes to the importance of preserving these stories and the wisdom they contain. This early exposure to storytelling fostered a deep appreciation for the art of narrative that has stayed with me ever since.

Another significant factor in shaping my aspiration to become a writer is my love for reading. Through books, I have traveled to far-off lands, lived alongside characters with varied and complex lives, and witnessed a multitude of human experiences. Each book I read has contributed to a growing understanding of the world around me and the myriad ways people think, feel, and live. This exposure has taught me that stories are an essential part of the human experience, a way to explore and express our connections to each other and the world.

In school, I had the opportunity to participate in a creative writing workshop. This experience was instrumental in furthering my writing skills and understanding of narrative structure. The workshop emphasized the importance of perspective and voice, encouraging us to experiment with different styles and genres. Receiving feedback from both peers and mentors helped me refine my technique and develop greater confidence in my ability to convey emotion and tension within my stories.

Beyond the classroom, I took an interest in community storytelling initiatives. I volunteered at a local library where I led storytime sessions for children. This experience taught me the joy and responsibility that comes with telling a good story, as well as the impact stories can have on young imaginations. Witnessing the children's reactions to different tales reinforced the idea that stories can spark curiosity and provide comfort, encouraging me to pursue this path even further.

Moreover, the digital age has expanded the way stories are told and shared, from blogs and podcasts to interactive media and beyond. This evolution in storytelling offers an exciting landscape for a budding writer like myself. I am eager to explore these platforms and discover new methods for engaging audiences, understanding that each medium offers unique opportunities to reach and resonate with people.

My hope to become a writer is not solely based on personal passion. It is also driven by a desire to have a broader impact, using stories to address issues that matter. Through writing, I aim to bring attention to social, cultural, and environmental topics that are essential to our global discourse. By shedding light on these issues, I hope to inspire dialogue and contribute to change in however small a way.

Ultimately, my journey as an aspiring writer is just beginning. I am eager to further develop my skills and immerse myself in the literature and voices that have yet to influence my work. As I look to the future, I envision myself as a writer who not only crafts stories worth reading but also uses storytelling to foster understanding and empathy across different communities.

The path to becoming a writer is one that is continuously evolving, both challenging and exhilarating. It is a path I am committed to pursuing, driven by the inspirations of my past and the possibilities of the future. I hope to create stories that leave a lasting impact, much like the ones that have shaped my own life and ambitions.

As I look towards the future and consider the paths available to me, I hope to become an environmental scientist. This aspiration is deeply rooted in my desire to positively impact the planet and draw attention to pressing sustainability issues. Growing up in a suburban neighborhood surrounded by nature, I spent countless hours exploring the woods and streams near my home. This fostered a sense of wonder and respect for the environment, which has only deepened over time.

One pivotal experience that has shaped my hope to enter this field occurred during a family trip to a national park. While hiking, we came across a group of volunteers working on a trail restoration project. Watching them labor with such dedication to preserve the natural beauty of the area was inspiring. I decided to join in and, for a few hours, experienced firsthand the satisfaction that comes from contributing to environmental preservation. This moment ignited a passion in me to protect the natural world.

In high school, I joined the Environmental Club, which provided me with opportunities to learn and advocate for environmental initiatives. Organizing events such as recycling drives and clean-up campaigns allowed me to engage my peers in sustainability efforts, further cementing my commitment to this cause. Additionally, these activities honed my leadership skills, teaching me how to motivate others and work collaboratively towards a common goal.

My love for science has been another critical factor in guiding me toward environmental science. Biology and chemistry classes revealed the intricate systems that sustain life on Earth. I became particularly interested in how human actions affect these systems, leading me to conduct a research project on the impact of pesticides on local bee populations. This project reinforced my understanding of the delicate balance within ecosystems and the significant consequences of disrupting them.

My interest in global issues also plays a key role in my ambition. I am deeply concerned about climate change and its effects on diverse communities. Through documentaries and articles, I have learned about the devastating consequences of extreme weather events and rising sea levels. These stories highlight the urgent need for scientific research and policy changes to mitigate these impacts. I want to be at the forefront of these efforts, using my skills to contribute to solutions that address environmental challenges on a global scale.

Traveling has also expanded my perspective and influenced my career aspirations. During a school exchange program, I visited a coastal community that was heavily affected by pollution. Seeing the contrast between their struggling environment and my relatively untouched neighborhood was eye-opening. It underscored the disparity in environmental conditions around the world and fueled my determination to work towards equitable environmental solutions.

The guidance and encouragement I have received from teachers and mentors have reinforced my confidence in pursuing this path. My biology teacher, in particular, encouraged me to enter the science fair with my research project and provided invaluable feedback. Her support throughout the process was instrumental in my success at the competition and solidified my desire to further explore environmental science in college.

As I embark on this educational journey, I am eager to deepen my understanding of environmental systems and develop innovative approaches to conservation and sustainability. I envision a future where I can collaborate with experts in the field to research and implement strategies that promote environmental health and resilience. I look forward to contributing to sustainable development practices and advocating for policy changes that protect natural resources for future generations.

In summary, my hope to become an environmental scientist is rooted in a lifelong connection to nature, a passion for science, and a commitment to global sustainability. Through my experiences, I have developed a keen awareness of the challenges facing our planet and a strong desire to be part of the solution.
